chapters.summary: Council staff opens the hearing on finance, reminds attendees not to approach the dais, and asks everyone to silence electronic devices before the chair begins.
chapters.title: Hearing called to order and housekeeping announcements

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's New York City Council hearing on the community on finance.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please no one may approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please silence your electronic devices.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, you may begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee welcomes everyone to the final day of FY27 Executive Budget hearings, introduces colleagues including Speaker Menon, and thanks the Finance Division staff by name for their tireless preparation work.
chapters.title: Chair Linda Lee welcomes members and introduces Finance Division staff

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to the final day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Well, technically we have the public testimony tomorrow, but final day of the FY27 Executive Budget Agency hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thankfully, I still have all my hair and didn't get too gray in the process.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But we are joined by, we will be committing the hearing today with Mayor's Office of Management and Budget, followed by Comptroller, Department of Finance, and the Independent Budget Office.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: At this time, I would like to introduce my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Of course, we are joined by our speaker, our Deputy Speaker Williams, Majority Whip Hanks, Majority Leader Abreu, Councilmember Shulman, Dinowitz, Ung, Lewis, Krishnan, Narcisse, and I think that is it for now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, and Councilmember Banks.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So before we start, I would like to extend my sincere gratitude to the dedicated staff of the City Council Finance Division for their tireless efforts in preparing for today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know it was a lot of long

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This work doesn't happen on its own, and I want to acknowledge the mighty team of dedicated professionals who make it all possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Finance Director Nathan Toth, Managing Director Jonathan Rosenberg, Deputy Directors Amre Edev, Chima Okbisher, Paul Simone, and Isha Wright, Chief Economist and Assistant Director Delaria Dimnanku.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Supervising Economists Paul Sturm and Andrew Wilber, Assistant Directors Alia Ali, Julia Haramis, Florentine Cabor, Caitlin O'Hagan, James Reyes, and Jack Story, Chief Counsel Nicholas Connell,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Senior Counsel Nadia Jean-Francois, and my Committee Counsel Brian Sarfo.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My senior advisor, Athena Say, and many finance analysts, economists, and support staff who have worked behind the scenes to bring everything together.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Welcome to Director Sharif Solomon, to you and your team, and thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

chapters.summary: Speaker Menon thanks the finance team, welcomes OMB Director Solomon, and notes the executive budget no longer includes a property tax increase or reserve drawdown, incorporating over 20 council recommendations from its budget response.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon delivers opening remarks on executive budget progress

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before I go any further, I want to invite Speaker Menon to give her opening remarks.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Shirley.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And first of all, I want to also thank our incredible council finance team who has logged in such long, hard, dedicated hours.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have an unbelievable team, and I just want to thank each and every one of you for your amazing work.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So good morning, everyone, and we want to welcome you, Director Solomon, and your whole team to be here today on the penultimate day of the hearings on the administration's proposed 124.7.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: billion executive budget for fiscal year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Nearly two and a half months ago, when you were last before this committee, we mentioned a number of issues that the council had with the preliminary plan that had been presented to the council.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I was very clear that the idea of increasing taxes for all New York property owners was not something this council was willing to consider.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Additionally, we pointed out to you how detrimental to the city's financial stability drawing down annual reserve funds would be.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Today, I want to thank you for taking those concerns seriously.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The executive plan no longer includes revenues from an assumed increase in the property tax, nor does it draw down on the annual reserves.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Additionally, the council wants to thank you for taking the council's response to the preliminary plan seriously.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I note that the current plan includes over 20 of the recommendations the council presented in our budget response,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: providing alternative resources rather than tapping reserve funds or increasing property taxes for all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We hope that the administration's acceptance of so many of our proposals is an indication of how budget adoption can be achieved.

chapters.summary: Speaker Menon releases the Council's updated revenue forecast showing nearly $2 billion more tax revenue than OMB in FY26-27, a manageable $2.5 billion FY28 gap, and calls for funding Fair Fares, parks, NYC Kids Rise, FDNY fifth firefighter, and DCWP.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon presents Council's updated revenue forecast

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Council staff has spent countless hours analyzing this financial plan and the underpinning economic assumptions that drive your revenue forecast.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As with every economic forecast, slight variations in assumptions can lead to differing revenue projections.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Today,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: My office released the Council's updated revenue forecast for its fiscal 2026 through 2030.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The Council's economic outlook foresees steady growth with a slight improvement from the March forecast.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As a result of this improved outlook and stronger than predicted current year collections, the Council's revenue forecast now includes nearly $2 billion more tax revenue than OMB in fiscal 2026 and 2027.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The Council's updated forecast also shows that once you adjust for the in-year reserves, the fiscal 2028 gap falls from over $7 billion to about $2.5 billion in manageable gap.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: With these additional funds, as well as the additional proposals presented in our budget response that were not included in the executive plan, the Council and the administration can agree to fund many programs for the

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: success, health, and safety of all New Yorkers, including the expansion of the Fair Fares program, which has been a top priority for the Council, bringing the parks budget in line with historical spending,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: implementing an expansion to the New York City Kids Rise program, the allocation of funding allowing the FDNY to reinstate the fifth firefighter,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: increased funding for the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection, increased funding for the Department of Investigations and other priorities.

chapters.summary: Speaker Menon highlights the Council's decade-long forecasting accuracy, notes economic uncertainty from Washington D.C. actions, and urges using surplus revenue to bolster the rainy day fund to protect city services from inevitable downturns.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon urges bolstering rainy day fund amid economic uncertainty

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I won't list our whole exhaustive list that the council has prioritized in crucial areas of the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the record speaks for itself.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Over the past decade, the Council's forecasts have the best performance in estimating the actual tax revenue available to the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And just as we have confidence in the Knicks, we are confident that this winning track record will continue.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we also understand that forecasting is as much as an art as it is a science.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's higher uncertainty we know in the economy, largely due to actions taken by those in Washington, D.C.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For that reason, the Council also believes that some of this nearly $2 billion surplus should be used to bolster the city's rainy day fund to protect our investments and city services from the inevitable downturn.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've come a long way since the release of the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The state budget has been adopted and the city is now receiving closer to its fair share of resources.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I hope that as a result we can move forward and adopt a budget that is both financially sound and also provides for the critical services that all New Yorkers require.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we look forward to hearing from you today about the administration's fiscal year 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you and I'm now going to turn it back over to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee details the Council's forecast of $2 billion more in tax revenue than OMB across FY26-27, driven by stronger personal income tax growth, and announces public testimony scheduled for the next day at 9:30 AM.
chapters.title: Chair Lee summarizes Council's tax revenue forecast and economic outlook

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So yesterday the Council released, as the Speaker just mentioned, our economic and tax forecast for the FY27 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And New York City's economy continues to show resilience with higher tax revenues than projected over FY26 and 27, even though our economic growth continues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: to lag below our long-run average.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The City Council's tax revenue forecast remains largely the same as our previous forecast this past March, with shifts in the timing of revenues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We project that the city will collect a total of nearly $2 billion more in tax revenue in the current and next fiscal years than OMB has projected in their May plan combined,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: driven by stronger personal income tax revenue growth and higher collections in property, personal income tax, and unincorporated business taxes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Additionally, we project $1.5 billion greater tax revenue collection in FY27, $3.1 billion in FY28, and $4.1 billion in FY29, and $4.9 in FY30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Council sees tax revenue set to grow at an average of 4.3% annually through the forecast period, which while trending the right way, remains below the 5.5% annual growth the City saw in the prior decade.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Council remains optimistic in the City's financial outlook, and we will continue to ensure that we are using every resource we have on hand to improve the lives of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We continue to have what we need to preserve the cornerstones and lifelines of our city and to protect the essential services that keep New Yorkers on their feet.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The more we invest in our fellow New Yorkers, the more our city will get back.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I look forward to seeing how our discussion today can help guide us towards discussions and decisions that improve the lives of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I have to say this PSA before every hearing, which is that we are going to take public testimony for all.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: all agencies on the executive budget tomorrow, Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9.30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we're going to go all day, which includes testimony for all the different agencies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please join us tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Make sure you sign up.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Tell your community members to sign up for public testimony at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we will see you all then.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I will now turn it over to Brian Sarfo, our committee counsel, to swear in the witnesses.

chapters.summary: Brian Sarfo swears in Director Sharif Solomon and six deputy directors who affirm to tell the truth before the committee. Chair Lee also recognizes additional council members who joined.
chapters.title: Committee counsel swears in OMB witnesses

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Director Solomon.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: First Deputy Boyard.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Deputy Gustorf.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Senior Deputy McKinney.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Deputy DiStafano.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Deputy Goldstein.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: And Deputy Hoffman.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, just really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just want to recognize we've also been joined by Councilmember Areola on Zoom, Councilmembers Wong, Morano, and Altabal.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Now you can go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry.

chapters.summary: Director Solomon testifies about the administration's approach to confronting a $12 billion gap from chronic under-budgeting, applying savings plans, additional tax revenue, and state support to present a balanced $124.7 billion executive budget without raising property taxes or drawing down reserves.
chapters.title: OMB Director Solomon: Addressing inherited $12 billion budget gap

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And good morning.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Good morning, Speaker Menon, Chair Lee, Leader Carr, members of the Finance Committee, and all members here with us today.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today about the executive budget for fiscal year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I am joined today by First Deputy Director Tara Berard, Senior Deputy Director Latonya McKinney, and Deputy Director

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Directors Josh Goldstein, Nathan Gustorf, Liz Hoffman, and Patrick DiStefano.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: When I testified in the preliminary budget in March 1st, in March, I spoke about significant and chronic under budgeting across many areas in the approach that our administration took to introduce transparency,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: predictability, and less risk in the city's budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Addressing billions of dollars in known expenses in the preliminary budget was a prudent, fiscally responsible, and necessary step to take,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: but it meant that we needed to confront a staggering $12 billion gap over fiscal years 2026 and 2027.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We immediately went to work and applied a variety of strategies to address this inherited challenge.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Through a combination of savings plans led by newly appointed chief savings officers, additional tax revenue, and significant financial support from our partners in state government, we eliminated the gap and presented a balanced

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: One that puts the city back on a strong financial footing, one that invests in an affordable, safe, and vibrant city, and one that continues core government operations that New Yorkers depend on.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we balance the $124.7 billion

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: fiscal year 2027 budget without raising property taxes, without

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: out drawing down the rainy day fund of the Retiree Health Benefit Trust Fund, and without slashing services.

chapters.summary: Solomon details $1.77 billion in savings across leasing, IT modernization, contract efficiencies, program savings, financial management improvements, and vacancy alignment, all without slashing services, with Executive Order 12 now embedded in agency operations.
chapters.title: OMB Director Solomon: $1.77 billion in savings from Chief Savings Officers

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This morning, I will discuss in greater detail the multifaceted approach we took to close the gap, focusing on three main areas, recurring savings initiatives, significant state aid,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: and restructuring unfunded pension liabilities.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The executive budget includes $1.77 billion in savings from chief savings officers and vacancy alignment over fiscal years 2026 and 2027, all without slashing services.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: By category,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Chief Savings Officers saved $24 million from giving up unused space, consolidating leases, and other leasing savings and efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: $28 million from modernizing city technology, software licensing efficiencies, and other IT savings.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: $94 million.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: From pursuing better contract rates, terminating contracts in favor of insourcing, and other contract savings and efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: $368 million from improving the efficiency of public services, managing overtime, and phasing out unused programs and other program savings.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: At $947 million, improving our financial management, claiming revenue that the city's owed, and accurately estimating expenses.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And finally, $307 million from the...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: vacancy alignment exercise.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This is the first phase of this exercise.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Executive Order 12 is now embedded into agency operations, and our savings officers will continue to work with OMB and the Office of the First Deputy Mayor to find savings and efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As I noted, $307 million of the $1.77 billion was achieved by reducing vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This savings exercise was paired with the removal of the two-for-one hiring restriction that has largely been in place since COVID and slowed down the hiring process in many agencies.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Agencies can now move quickly to hire up to their funded personnel services budget, personal services budget, which will improve service delivery.

chapters.summary: Solomon discusses policy changes to contain costs in special education due process cases and the CityFEPs program, including $150 million for special ed pre-K, centralizing support functions, and strengthening oversight to curb spending growth without cutting vouchers.
chapters.title: OMB Director Solomon: Due process and CityFEPs cost containment reforms

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: On top of savings achieved through the Chief Savings Officer's initiative,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: and the vacancy alignment exercise.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In the executive budget, we implemented policy changes to contain rapidly escalating costs in due process cases and the City FEPS program.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In the executive budget, we had made investments to address the underlying systemic issues, which have led to a shortage of services available to special education students in the public school system.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This includes adding $150 million for special education pre-K and ensuring that children in private schools have access to DOE service providers, which are less expensive.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: alternative to the private market.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: At the same time, we will improve the administrative procedures for due process hearings.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that CityFEPS is a lifeline for thousands of New Yorkers leaving the shelter system and seeking stable housing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As both the stewards of the program and an administration that firmly believes in its purpose, we want to protect it by placing it on firm financial footing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That is why we are pursuing reforms such as centralizing support functions, strengthening oversight, and implementing administrative improvements that protect the program's future.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We can curb spending growth without cutting vouchers and, at the same time, develop strategies to improve the program.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I look forward to discussing this with the Council in the weeks ahead.

chapters.summary: Solomon thanks Governor Hochul and legislative leaders for $383 million in foundation aid, corporate tax decoupling generating $700 million, a $1.5 billion commitment including youth programming and hospital funding, and approximately $4 billion total through direct aid, authorizations, and new revenue.
chapters.title: OMB Director Solomon: Significant state aid package from Albany

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Next, I would like to express my deep gratitude to Governor Hochul, Majority Leader Stuart Cousins, Speaker Hastie, and members in both houses

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: for supporting the significant package of state aid that helped address our budget challenges.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I would also like to thank you, Speaker Menon, and Chair Lee and the entire Council for your advocacy on behalf of additional resources for the city.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The Governor's executive budget included an additional $383 million in foundation aid and a provision to allow the city to decouple

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: from certain corporate tax cuts that have been included in H.R.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: 1 that will generate approximately $700 million in fiscal year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: State support continued after the governor's executive budget with a $1.5 billion commitment from the governor on the day before we released our preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That included $500 million in one-time unrestricted aid, a recurring restoration of cuts and cost shifts, including $300 million per year on youth programming, $150 million

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: per year to reverse the sales tax intercept for distressed hospitals, and $60 million to reverse lower reimbursement rates for public health funding.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: An additional $97 million in school aid was also realized.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Finally, the enacted state budget included approximately $4 billion more through a mix of direct aid, such as restoring the state share of line-of-duty survivor benefits, state authorizations,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: such as a more flexible schedule for class-size compliance, and new revenue from the state's first pied-à-terre tax for non-primary resident homes valued at over $5 million.

chapters.summary: Solomon explains the pied-à-terre tax on luxury non-primary residences over $5 million generating $500 million annually, and the pension restructuring proposal extending payments to 2037 with flat level dollar payments, generating $652 million in FY26 and $1.64 billion in FY27 with no impact on retiree benefits.
chapters.title: OMB Director Solomon: Pied-à-terre tax and pension restructuring

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It has long been understood that the property tax system is inequitable.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This reality manifests itself in many ways, including the regressive way that the luxury market is taxed.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The pied-à-terre tax will

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: remedy some of that inequity for high-value homes over $5 million and will generate $500 million in recurring tax revenue that will support much-needed city services across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The enacted state budget also authorized the restructuring

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: of unfunded pension liabilities in the city's retirement systems, subject to approval by the trustees of each retirement system to ensure that we are making predictable and even payments over time.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The restructuring proposal allows a short five-year extension to a 22-year payment schedule that began in 2010, which required employer contributions to pay down

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: unfunded actuarial liabilities as calculated by the Office of the Actuary.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The original amortization schedule was back-ended with annual payments beginning at roughly $4 billion and climbing to about $7.2 billion at the end of the amortization period in 2032 when the cost would plummet to zero.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There is a better way to finance long-term obligations.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Flat dollar, level payments, and that is what the pension restructuring proposal accomplishes.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Instead of completing payments in 2032, we will get there in 2037 with no impact.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I underscore no impact on retiree benefits.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Smoothing the pension payments over time will generate $652 million.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: and savings this fiscal year, $1.64 billion in fiscal 27, and roughly $1.6 billion annually in the remaining out years of the plan.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And our retirement systems will remain fundamentally sound.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The average funded ratio for the five retirement systems is 86%, higher than the 79%.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: National average.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This reasonable funding policy and the multi-year savings it will produce will ensure that New Yorkers have a local government that is more financially stable while ensuring a continued pathway to a 100% funded ratio for the city's retirement systems.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And I thank the council for including this proposal in its preliminary budget response.

chapters.summary: Solomon covers the balanced FY26-27 budget, the $7.1 billion FY28 gap in line with historical averages, replenished reserves at $2 billion for Rainy Day and $5.2 billion for Retiree Health Benefit Trust, and a revenue forecast revised up $75 million in FY26.
chapters.title: OMB Director Solomon: FY27 budget technical details and reserves

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Now I'd like to share technical details about the fiscal year 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Fiscal years 26 and 27 remain balanced.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The fiscal year 2028 gap is at $7.1 billion, which is in line with historical averages.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The gaps in fiscal years 2029 and 2030 of $9.1 billion and $9.8 billion, respectively, are lower than during the Great Recession years, the percentage of gaps over revenue.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We will continue to identify structural solutions and make long-term systemic changes to improve the city's long-term fiscal outlook and reduce out-year gaps.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Our administration is committed to rebuilding and maintaining high levels of reserves to ensure that we can weather an economic table.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We replenished the Rainy Day Fund to $2 billion and the Retiree Health Benefit Trust Fund to $5.2 billion.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As a percentage of revenues, our reserves are approximately 8%, higher than the pre-COVID 10-year average of 7.3%.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Turning now to our revenue forecast, we recognize headwinds, including the ongoing war in the Middle East and a subdued labor market, but also economic

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: strengths, including continued market momentum, financial and artificial intelligence investment, and elevated commercial leasing activity that is driving down vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The result is that before accounting for changes in savings and tax programs, the tax revenue forecast in the executive budget

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: has been revised up by $75 million in fiscal year 2026 and is unchanged in fiscal year 2027 as compared with the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we incorporated the council's proposal to reduce the unincorporated business tax credit, which will generate $68 million annually also as of fiscal year 2027.

chapters.summary: Solomon highlights $17 billion in new spending across prelim and exec budgets with only 8% for programmatic initiatives, including baselined funding for libraries, parks, Fair Fares, cultural organizations, CUNY, childcare, community safety, hate crimes prevention, and waste containerization.
chapters.title: OMB Director Solomon: New programmatic investments in the executive budget

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: By virtue of our strong fiscal management, we have made new critical investments in New Yorkers that are focused on retaining our tax base, taking preventative measures to save money down the line, and spending more efficiently

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: to deliver faster results for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: From a

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: A big picture perspective, we added roughly $17 billion in new spending across the preliminary and executive budgets with just $1.39 billion, or 8%,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: attributable to programmatic administration initiatives, with the rest going towards addressing chronic underbudgeting.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: For known critical needs and core government operations like snow removal and IT costs.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In terms of programmatic investments, our administration has baseline needs that were typically deferred to adopted budget negotiations and funded for just one fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In the executive budget, we baseline funding for the library systems, the parks department, fair fairs, cultural organizations, and CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This removes the uncertainty many New Yorkers experience every July around ongoing access to their neighborhood library, cleanliness in their parks, the cost of traveling to and from work or their doctor, accessing the public art sphere.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And preserving CUNY as an engine of upward mobility.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We also made investments to improve the lives of our youngest New Yorkers by increasing childcare provider rates, expanding the New York City Reading and Solves program, and funding the Little Apple, the city's first ever municipal daycare program.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We also deepened our commitment to a safer New York with investments in the newly formed Office of Community Safety, an 800% increase in funding for the Office of Prevention of Hate Crimes,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: and staffing within the CCRB and the fire department.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The mayor also expanded his commitment to safer streets and deepened funding for waste containerization.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have boosted economic justice and worker protections with support for street vendors and commercial lease legal assistance and expanded capacity for the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection to grow their headcount by 181 heads and $18 million

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: over the next three fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we are improving New Yorkers'health and well-being by supporting survivors of domestic violence, seniors, and access to mental health care.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think we can all agree that these are necessary investments that improve the quality of life for New Yorkers across every borough and community.

chapters.summary: Solomon presents the capital plan totaling $117.1 billion, including $4 billion in additional HPD capital for affordable housing, $500 million for NYCHA renovations, and $256 million for restoring vacant NYCHA units, with $5.6 billion total for NYCHA.
chapters.title: OMB Director Solomon: $117.1 billion five-year capital plan and housing investments

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Turning now to the capital budget, the five-year capital plan totals $117.1 billion, growth of $4.1 billion over the preliminary capital plan.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The affordability crisis is exacerbated by an acute housing crisis, and the administration's block-by-block housing plan addresses the crisis head-on.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That is why we invested an additional $4 billion in the housing, in HPD agency capital funds over the five-year plan, and $500 million more in fiscal year 2031

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: to build and preserve affordable housing across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have also invested an additional $500 million in fiscal year 2028 for comprehensive NYCHA renovations.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: and we're allocating $256 million from this fiscal year through fiscal year 2028, along with funds from the expense budget to restore vacant units so more families can move into safe, stable homes.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The capital plan includes a total of $5.6 billion for NYCHA, the most city capital devoted to NYCHA in recent history.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I look forward to working with you as partners to address the fiscal challenges ahead and identify resources that support our mutual priorities, including

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: keeping New York safe and clean, promoting education and public health, taking on the affordability crisis and making the city livable each and every day for the people who build it.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you again for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I'm happy to answer any questions that you have.

chapters.summary: Speaker Menon raises that OMB took nearly two months to respond to a council follow-up letter sent April 6th, and secures a commitment from Solomon to provide more timely responses going forward.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon questions OMB on response timeline for budget follow-up letter

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, going to turn it over to Speaker Menon first for her questions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, thank you so much for your testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: A number of questions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So first of all, just a process timeline question really coming from our finance team.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So after our preliminary budget hearing on the 25th, the council sent a follow-up letter to OMB requesting additional information regarding the budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That letter was sent on April 6th.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We didn't receive a response until June 2nd, nearly two months later.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we want to get your commitment moving forward that that won't happen again and that we get timely responses to the council's requests.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Yes, we will work on.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Making sure we speed that up for you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Menon asks about costs to expand Fair Fares to 200% FPL ($150-300 million depending on utilization), potential enrollment of over a million riders, and concerns about chronic underspending, with Solomon detailing outreach efforts including subway ads and MTA partnerships.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon questions OMB on Fair Fares expansion costs and utilization

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So moving to the substance.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So fare fares, which, as you know, is one of the top priorities, an expansion of fare fares that the council has put forward.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So some questions on that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: What does the administration estimate it would cost to expand the eligibility of Fair Fares programs to 175%, 200%, and 250% of the federal poverty level?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So let me start, Speaker, by saying we are proud that we were able to fund in the executive budget $25 million that had been put up at the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As a one-shot in the Fair Fares program.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that there are calls to increase the rate, to increase the percentage of federal poverty level.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: If we were to go to 200%, depending on utilization, it can be anywhere between $150 million to $300 million.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And what do you estimate would be the increase in the number of clients enrolled in the program for these expansion scenarios?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have estimates that can be upwards of over a million riders.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Really depends on utilization.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: During the hearing we had on the General Welfare Executive Budget hearing, HRA testified that through March 2026, $62.5 million had been spent in fiscal 2026 for fair fares.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: During the first...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: three quarters of the fiscal year, only about half of the Fair Fares budget had been spent.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So do you anticipate the entire budgeted amount for the program this year will be spent?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If not, why not and how much will be unspent?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And overall, the questioning is that there's been a underspending has been the case for multiple, multiple years, which is concerning.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So what outreach and education efforts will the administration take to address that?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So, you know, I think you've heard the mayor say on numerous occasions.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: A program on paper is of no use to New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We need to be able to get the word out as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: You see ads all over the subways.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: You see ads on New York One.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: You see ads elsewhere.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know as well that the agency participates on a frequent basis.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: With the MTA on outreach events, either in the subways or, you know, wherever they can connect with New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So outreach is key to this program.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And there may be some underutilization that is expected for this fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The goal is to fully utilize this program.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Menon presses OMB on why the administration continues contracting with Bragg's after federal bribery charges, questioning the independent monitor arrangement. Solomon cites a corrective action plan and DOI oversight. Patrick DiStefano confirms no new funding was added for the monitor.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon questions OMB on Bragg's Home Care contract and monitor

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Moving along to a different topic, in April 2026, leaders of the Brooklyn not-for-profit Bragg's Home Care were indicted on federal bribery and embezzlement charges.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Rather than cut ties with Bragg's, the administration has appointed an independent monitor to oversee the group.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Bragg's was awarded two new multi-year contracts.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: multi-year contract.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: which start on July 1st, worth $186 million for shelter and social services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can you explain the administration's decision to continue its relationship with Braggs, considering the recent federal charges?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Well, as you noted, Speaker, we have the vendor has a corrective action plan.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're working with DOI to make sure that there's a monitor and there is, you know, those protocols in place.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we'll stay working closely between the agency and the Department of Investigation to make sure that corrective action is taken according to that corrective action plan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Why not just cease interaction with this organization and select a new provider?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Again, the safeguards are in place with the DOI, with DOI in the mix, overseeing, having a corrective action plan, and a monitor for this provider.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Who is the independent monitor and how are they selected?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I'd have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, if you could get back to us this week on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then also, did the appointment of the monitor require a new contract?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is it additional funding?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If so, how much did the monitor cost?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Have the funds in the budget been allocated to the monitor?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Let me see.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Turn over to Patrick, if you have.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: We did not add new funding for that.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: There's funding allocated within each of the agency budgets for this type of monitoring.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So it was within that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So if you could get that, I do want to express my strong concern with this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: decision.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I don't understand it and I don't know why I knew why continue to contract with this entity.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I want to make sure we are saying that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I do want to say it's not the first time concerns have been raised about a not-for-profit organization contracting with DHS in 2025 for employees at a different shelter provider were indicted for wire fraud, bribery, and money laundering.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what steps is the administration taking to address systemic deficiencies in contract oversight to ensure that public dollars are being used responsibly?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We continue to work with our agencies and we continue to work with, you know, the Department of Investigation to ensure that if there's any

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: contracts that are not being implemented in the appropriate way, that there's appropriate action that's taken.

chapters.summary: Menon raises concerns about DOE's failure to provide contract information, noting DOE blamed technical issues and staff capacity. Solomon highlights $30 million in DOE contract savings ramping to $200 million by FY30, and a summary of $1.25 billion in non-competitively bid contracts provided to the council.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon questions OMB on DOE contract transparency

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, moving along to DOE contracts, which is something I know we've asked a lot of questions about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So in both our preliminary budget follow-up to DOE and to OMB, we requested additional information on DOE contracts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Neither DOE or OMB provided this information in your responses, and then we received a partial response from DOE late last week that was unclear.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yesterday's executive budget hearing on education, DOE blamed technical issues and staff capacity for not being able to provide responses to the council.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I do want to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want to note DOE has the largest agency budget of any agency, so the idea that there's not staff to address what are literally rudimentary questions about providing contracts I find not credible.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We appreciate the fact that the council has surfaced and raised DOE contracts and contracts writ large as an opportunity for the city to realize savings.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That is why the chief savings officers and in our savings plan, we begin with $30 million in savings from DOE contracts in fiscal 27, ramping up to $200 million in fiscal year 2030.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's a start.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know there's more to do.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We want to partner with the council to be able to do that.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: With respect to the information that was provided, there was a summary of contracts that was provided.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know you want the actual contracts, and we'll work with you and the Department of Education.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The summary, I would note as well, has about $1.25 billion in contracts, which is what requested in terms of what are the contracts that haven't been competitively bid.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There are a couple in there like the custodial services and the New York City school bus.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I think that we're willing to obviously take a deeper look with you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: On that, as we perceive our shared goal of being able to find contract services without

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Contract savings without slashing services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So when can we expect to receive the contracts?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We will work with you and the DOE.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that they answered yesterday that it'll take a certain amount of staff time.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We'll look to see how quickly we can work together.

chapters.summary: Menon notes the mayor's campaign pledge to double DCWP's budget but the exec plan only increases it 4.4%, still $53.6 million short. Solomon highlights 181 new positions and ongoing discussions about appropriate resources, but does not directly confirm the doubling commitment.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon questions OMB on DCWP budget doubling commitment

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, moving along to DCWP, which is also a top priority of the council.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: On the campaign trail, the mayor had pledged to double the budget of DCWP, but the fiscal 2027 executive budget proposes only $78.4 million, an increase of just 4.4%

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: from $75.1 million at fiscal 2026 budget adoption.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The fiscal 2030 budget at the end of the plan in the mayor's term is $96.6 million.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That means DCWP still needs $53.6 million to achieve a doubling of its budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how is the administration still committed to doubling DCP's budget?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Could you talk about the funding?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you know, we're pushing for increased funding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the reason we're pushing for increased funding is because this agency has been asked year in and year out.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To be clear by the council, to do additional work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have so many important council bills that were passed in the last few years that add additional work to DCWP.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So as someone who ran that agency, I just want to raise a red flag that I'm very concerned about the agency's ability to do everything that we are asking it to do.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I know you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: know the agency well and we both share the goal of what the agency can do and what it can be.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have a significant investment in this executive budget to bring their headcount when fully ramped up to 181 new positions.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: To address multiple facets of their responsibilities, existing responsibilities and new responsibilities through the local laws that you referenced, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we look forward to having the continued discussion with you over the next few weeks about appropriate resources for DCWP.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so is the administration, though, still committed to doubling the agency's budget?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We want to give DCWP the resources that it needs.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We worked very closely with the commissioner to understand what he needs, both in the short term and in the medium term.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And this executive budget provides a significant, significant start.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're happy to talk to you in the next few weeks about if there's any more that's needed.

chapters.summary: Menon asks about the Office of Mass Engagement's $53 million budget, PEU's role and funding, and safeguards against political activity. Solomon explains OME's community outreach functions and states government offices cannot participate in political activities, with COIB training required.
chapters.title: Speaker Menon questions OMB on Office of Mass Engagement and PEU

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, in January 2026, with Executive Order 7, the mayor established the Office of Mass Engagement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The existing public engagement unit, PEU, budgeted within HRA, was incorporated into this new office.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We would like to get a better understanding of the work of the Office of Mass Engagement and how specifically PEU will now operate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what is the total budget and headcount?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For the Office of Mass Engagement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I want to say that the PEU is an,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: an entity that council members and community boards, I mean, they're sort of on the front lines of dealing with community outreach.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we really under, this is why we really want to better understand these various functions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what is the total budget and headcount for OME in fiscal 2027 and in the out years?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the total budget for the Office of Mass Engagement is $53 million,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: and that includes in this executive budget $2.8 million for new funding for staff in the executive budget in 27 and out.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And as you noted, there are multiple offices that are included.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In there and so the additional funding would fund an additional 20 lines in the in the office.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Did you say 53 million for OME?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Overall budget for OME 53 million.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But how much of OME's budget then is for PEU?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We can provide you a breakdown for all the different offices that are in there.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There's PEU, there's CEAU, there are...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: You know what the other, there, look.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, and what are the new office's responsibilities?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Well, I think that

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The Community Assistance Unit, as has existed, as you know, connects government with communities and being able to inform New Yorkers and all communities about what services government provides, so on and so forth.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In an acute housing crisis as well, there's outreach to various communities to inform them about assistance in their housing needs, for example.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So these are the kinds of things that the office, even before there was the creation of Office of Mass Engagement, has existed to do.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so there's a continuation of those efforts and really a ramp up of those efforts to reach New Yorkers and their communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So in the executive plan, we can see that 10.3 million in dedicated budget codes for PEU within HRA includes the 20 positions that you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: From conversations council finance staff has had with OMB, we understand there is additional funding and headcount for PEU that is not so clearly delineated in the budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Why is that?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So, Patrick, do you have the...

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: Excuse me.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: PEU, I believe, exists within one or two budget structures within HRA.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So it's not that hidden or anything.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: And I think PEU budget is approximately $24 million.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: And we can provide those codes to your staff.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the last question I have on this topic is what safeguards are in place to ensure that OME is not engaging in political work or other outreach?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is not, that would be a conflict of interest.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Government offices cannot participate in political activities.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That is very clear from the very beginning.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so employees are instructed as such.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so whether it's through conflicts of interest board training, DOI training, employees understand what their roles and responsibilities are.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This is a government office, not a political operation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, and so all the employees have taken COIB training?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I know that I will get back to you to confirm who has taken it or not.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, I have more questions, but I'm going to turn it back over to Chair Lee, and I'll go on the second round.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether all state budget assumptions were included in the executive plan. Solomon confirms commitments were met, noting one exception: Tier 6 pension enhancement legislation cost $36 million more than the $110 million initially budgeted.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on state budget assumptions in executive plan

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so starting with the state budget assumptions, the executive plan was released, obviously, as we know, prior to the adoption of the state budget, and now we have the state budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what assumptions does the executive plan include regarding state actions or funding, and were all the assumptions included in the final adopted state budget?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think it's another opportunity to really thank Governor Hochul, Leader Stuart Cousins, and Speaker Hastie.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: What was committed to was provided, and so we are confident in what we put in the executive budget in terms of what the final product was.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and what other actions in the adopted state budget will affect the city's financial plan that were not included in the executive plan?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There was one particular area, Chair, and we had put up in the financial plan $110 million for what we anticipated would be the cost impacts from...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Pension enhancement legislation is the tier six legislation and a couple of other pension bills It turned out in the final analysis that tier six was more costly to the city.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we hadn't booked that extra I believe year one was 36 million dollars more and

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And there were a couple of other pension bills, you know, as well.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So that is something that we'll have to reflect based on those fiscal notes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so that was $110 million total of the...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: $110 million we put in the financial plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But it turned out that Tier 6 was, I think, I believe, $36 million more.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Also, just really quickly, we've been joined by Jay Sanchez, Councilmember Epstein, Councilmember Ressler, Councilmember Farias, and then on Zoom we have...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Tiffany-Cabán, Councilmember Cabán.

chapters.summary: Lee raises the council's call for new units of appropriation to improve transparency. Solomon lists new UofAs created for Office of Mass Engagement, City Council committees, and Office of Community Safety, and expresses willingness to discuss further.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on new units of appropriation and transparency

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so really quickly, moving to units of appropriation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: One of the ways to improve transparency and rebuild trust in the budget is to align the units of appropriation with the programmatic spending areas, especially where there are...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Real issues and cost containment.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In our budget response, we called for the creation of a number of new U of A's to increase transparency.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So does the executive plan include any of the new U of A's?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if so, could you please provide a list of the new ones?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also, what was the rationale behind the creation?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure, there were new units of appropriation in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The Office of Mass Engagement, for example.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There were also new units of appropriation for the City Council in the sense that there were committees, the Committee to Combat Hate, the Committee on Disabilities, etc.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Also, there were new units of appropriation for the Office of Community Safety that was created.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As always, we look forward to discussing with the Council new units of appropriation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about OCS's restructuring and future funding needs. Solomon explains $260 million moved from existing offices into OCS, plus $41 million in new dollars for FY27 bringing the total to $271 million, with additional investments expected in out years.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on Office of Community Safety structure and funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just while you're on that topic with OCS, because I know that this came up in a

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: a few of the city agency hearings that we have throughout the budget process where I think there's just a lot of questions and confusion around OCS and what that's going to look like.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I know that there's a whole restructuring that's happening.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And my understanding, correct me if I'm wrong,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: is that we're sort of taking programs that were under perhaps different city agencies and putting it in.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it's not necessarily new funding that's being allocated, but do you know if there will be increased funding required for OCS in the future?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how does it look like right now in terms of the coordination, staffing, all of those things?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the creation of the Office of Community Safety is really an exciting step for the administration to be able to move forward in this space, be able to create an office in City Hall under a new deputy mayor.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so what we initially were doing was taking $260 million that were from existing offices.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That would come under the umbrella of the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So, you know, the Office of Community Mental Health, the Office of the End...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: GBV.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so that was the 2026 number of $260 million.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: What you have in 2027, there was a drop-off based on grants that did not extend into fiscal 27.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we've invested...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: $41 million in new dollars so that the overall budget as it stands for fiscal 27 is $271 million for the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Clearly, we're going to want to build on that as we move forward, but there was money added to go beyond the $260 that we said we were taking in in fiscal 26.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: Yeah, because my end.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know up until now it's been, you know, just sort of reshuffled funding, but my understanding was that it would eventually require more funding in out years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so next year for FY27, you're saying $41 million.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then do you know in future out years how much that will increase?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In future out years, we are looking at additional investments as the office takes further shape and the vision of Deputy Mayor Francois continues to take hold.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, I took a little curve.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether reserves should be budgeted in dedicated UofAs rather than lumped in miscellaneous budget. Solomon expresses willingness to discuss improving the display of reserve information, noting everyone already knows the amounts down to the penny.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on reserves budgeting in dedicated units of appropriation

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So currently, I'm sorry, going back to U of A, the city's reserves are held in one unit of appropriation, 002, in the miscellaneous budget, which also includes judgments and claims, various transit subsidies, and other citywide fixed costs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So do you think it'd be better and provide more transparency for the city to budget its reserves in dedicated units of appropriation rather than just...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Altogether in a lump sum.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah, I think, look, we're happy to have a conversation with you about it.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think that there's been certainly a greater focus and attention on reserves now.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think everyone knows down to the penny exactly what we have in reserves because there's been so much discussion about it.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But, of course, we're happy to talk to you about ways that we can improve that level of, you know, display of information.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee questions OMB's 2% annual tax revenue growth assumption in out years, below inflation, which would mean less real revenue in FY30 than FY27. Solomon and staff explain they forecast conservatively for out years due to greater uncertainty and asymmetric downside risk.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on conservative out-year tax revenue growth assumptions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then moving to the tax revenue growth in out years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the financial plan shows large out-year gaps.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 7 billion, as you mentioned in your testimony, to nearly 10 billion in FY 2030.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: However, this partly appears to be a function of the assumption of very slow tax revenue, your assumption of very slow tax revenue growth in those years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And OMB expects tax revenues to grow at an average of just 2% a year, which is slower than the forecast for inflation during the same period that you guys had mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: After adjusting for inflation, OMB expects the city to generate less tax revenue in FY 2030 than it will in FY 2027.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So that's something that usually happens only during recessions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wanted to know how you came up with this forecast.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is this a product of just being more conservative on the budgeting?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So first to start, I think we have to look at first what we have in the tax forecast for FY26 and FY27.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So you're looking at forecasted growth in 26, and collections are coming in on plan at 5.1% this year, and then around 4% next year.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We continually monitor, obviously, market conditions, economic conditions, headwinds, as I noted in my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: When you start looking out into the out years, we have basically a forecast that is not

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Too aggressive because we want to make sure that we're not forecasting revenues that don't materialize.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I don't know if you want to add anything.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I would ask what you think is a more realistic outlook then.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think statistically as you go out into the out years, the degree of certainty is smaller, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You have a much larger room for error.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So in terms of how we do it, as the budget director said, we typically

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: try to get the best forecast for the next, the current and next forecast year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then we do have a wider band of variants.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we are on the less aggressive side, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're taking a lot of, there's a lot of risk out there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We want to make sure that we are

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: accounting for it because as you know like the the risk is asymmetric on the revenues right if you if you forecast too aggressively and the money doesn't come in then we're forced to cut right

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hopefully there's room for improvement, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Or increases.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about property tax reform insights and timeline. Solomon says a comprehensive reform proposal building on the Advisory Commission recommendations is being developed for the next state legislative session. The 2026 tax lien sale is paused pending a six-month review.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on property tax reform timeline and tax lien sale

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, property tax increase and reform.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm glad you said it's inequitable.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As we know, the property tax system is inequitable.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the executive plan...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sort of

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm glad also that you are not including it in the executive plan for the property tax increases.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But as you sort of have been thinking through looking at property taxes, right, what insights has this process so far in the budget given in terms of where changes need to take place?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I think we've said before, we'll continue to say, I think it's near universal agreement that the property tax system is inequitable.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that we need reform.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We are working on a reform proposal that largely builds on the recommendations of the Advisory Commission on Property Tax Reform.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Established by former Mayor de Blasio and former Speaker Johnson.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
