utterances.text: So we are making sure that we're looking at not only the structural recommendations that are included in those findings from the commission, but also looking at homeowner relief programs, taking a holistic view.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that, you know, looking at other classes of properties, we're looking at the multifamily properties, large rentals, commercial properties.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we are taking a comprehensive, holistic view.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: On property tax reform because that's what's needed to address the system.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we're working on that proposal to be able to introduce in the state legislative session in the next year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, in the state, okay.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In the next state legislative session.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know that this year in Albany, I think you had mentioned that we

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The proposal would be released in weeks, which was in February, but did you mean for next year?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because we're just trying to get a sense of when the timeline is.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah, I think we're definitely working on the proposal.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Again, we're taking a comprehensive view, and we look forward to being able to have a proposal that is ready for action in Albany next year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And will the tax lien sale remain paused until after the property tax reform gets accomplished?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The tax lien, we paused the 2026 tax lien sale.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As you know, we are initiating, or in the middle of, I should say, a six-month review on tax liens.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: At the same time, we know that per legislation that has been passed earlier this year, there are all kinds of different...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: You know, policy looks, policy changes that could be made, including the utilization of land banks.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so we are looking at that to be able to have an alternative pathway to what we've seen in terms of the lien sale, which is a program that's in need of reform.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so what I'm hearing, if I'm understanding correctly, is that you would pause it until the– do you want it to go hand-in-hand with the property tax reform?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that what you're saying?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Or you would– Not necessarily– I mean, it's–

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's related in the sense that it's both property tax, but the steps that we're taking on both sides, it's not really interrelated in that same way.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the six-month review is what we're engaged in now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In terms of the tax lien sale and then the property tax reform proposal is also being worked on in parallel.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: To the extent that the two will interrelate, we will obviously incorporate what our review lands.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so there is a chance that it will unpause, I guess, for next year.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The six-month review will dictate, and the recommendations from there will dictate how we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee raises agency complaints about slow OMB hiring approvals. Solomon details eliminating the two-for-one hiring restriction, approving 1,400 PARs in recent weeks, and ongoing efforts to track and streamline the PAR process, though background checks are handled outside OMB.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on streamlining hiring approvals and PAR delays

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Really quickly going into streamlining OMB hiring approvals to reduce the staffing delays.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know that one of the things we've heard across a lot of the agencies and just is around the staffing and the report that it does take a while to obviously go through the approval process.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And that it sometimes causes internal bottlenecks to hire folks in the social services.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so what steps have you taken to streamline the hiring and budget approval process so agencies can fill positions in a timely manner and maintain the staffing levels needed to deliver essential services?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: One of the first things...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: that I heard when I came to OMB was that, you know, one of frustration from agencies about the personal action request forms not moving at an expeditious way.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we, you know, immediately went to work to try to figure out where those bottlenecks were.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we've been making significant improvements.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: One of the main things we've done is eliminate the two-for-one hiring restriction.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Under the two-for-one hiring restriction, you would need to wait to get a monthly allotment after we understood what your attrition rate was, right?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So that is effective.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That's eliminated.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so agencies are now able to hire up to their PS budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: What we sometimes encounter when we're looking at personnel action requests is that there is a misalignment between the vacancy and between the appropriate funding, or maybe there are different levels of sources of funding.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That may tie to a particular employee line where we have to go, we'll have a little bit of a back and forth with the agency on that.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But we're committed to streamlining the process as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In fact, we're looking at, and this has been in the works, to look at a system that we can have where we can really look at

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: where a par is across the system to see if there are bottlenecks.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: and looking at if there are particular agencies that have an issue where we have, you know, funding issues because they have more capital funding, for example.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So all of that's on the table.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We are looking to move it quickly.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And in fact, we have moved it quickly.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think between May 1st and May 1st and June 6th, I think we moved forward and approved about 1,400 PARs.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think in fairness, a couple of those have, I mean, a piece of those 1,400 have been for new classes.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: You know, in terms of civil service titles that are coming online, but there have been, you know, agency PARs that have been met.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so we're continually working on that and committed to improvement.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And sometimes I know the delay can happen with the background checks.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And sometimes the background checks can take about upwards of six months to complete.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so how have you factored that part into the sort of streamlining of the hiring process?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is there a way to truncate that sort of timeline?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And do agencies manage the background checks themselves or does DOI manage them?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know some cases external contractors are involved in the background check process as well.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah, so OMB is not involved in the background check process.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so when we get a PAR, we're looking to move it forward as quickly as possible, as I noted.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then just in terms of the same sort of line of thought around streamlining, one of the

chapters.summary: Lee asks about OMB's role in HPD project approvals and whether it's duplicative. First Deputy Director Tara Boirard explains OMB approves certificates to proceed, checking funding eligibility and term sheet compliance, and that the process runs concurrently with HPD's work, not sequentially.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on HPD term sheets and capital project approval delays

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Comments and feedback we've heard from a lot of the HPD-related housing projects is that there's a very arduous process that they have,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: the term sheets that HPD has, and then they have to go through another very arduous, long process with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I know last year the council worked with the administration to update the HPD term sheets that in some cases hadn't been updated for years, and this was critically important because out-of-date term sheets required ad hoc approvals from OMB that added

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: considerable delays.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so what is your role in approving HPD preservation in new construction projects, and is any of OMB's role duplicative of agency-level approvals at HPD?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to Tara Burrard to speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: So as it pertains to HPD projects, we are required to approve the certificate to proceed.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: As part of that analysis, we're looking to make sure that all of the funding is eligible for capital, but we're also looking to ensure that the project is within the term sheets that we agreed upon.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Oftentimes when there is a perceived delay, it's because the project is coming over and then we're working with the agency to make a determination about how to bring the costs down.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Over the years, when we have found those projects over term sheet, that funding does not come back to the general fund or the capital budget, but more than likely stays with the project.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: So what we're trying to do is increase additional affordability or raise the reserve level to make sure that that particular project is more viable in the long term.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So after updating the term sheet, would that not be included there?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because my understanding is that it was updated so that we could sort of move these projects along faster.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And all I know is that from my own minuscule experience with our nonprofit capital project, the CPs can sometimes take like eight, ten years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Not necessarily, and with that, the costs and everything else increase.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so with housing being such an important part of the city's affordability issues, how do we make sure we can speed this up?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The team at OMB that works on the housing CPs is extremely dedicated.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think that, you know, for the most part, you're looking at housers in addition to budget people.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The process that we take on is concurrent.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're not adding additional time to the whole development process for HPD.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, correct me if I'm wrong, because I was told that it's sequential, not concurrent.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I guess that was my question, because that would seem to make the process even longer, but you're saying it happens at the same time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It happens at the same time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I have more questions as well, but I'll save it for round two because I want to make sure that our members can ask questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So first we are going to...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, where did you start?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we'll come back to Deputy Speaker when she comes back.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Majority Leader Abreu.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Abreu raises that DOT confirmed no funding for hardened daylighting despite the mayor's campaign promise. Solomon notes 400 daylighting projects budgeted, bike lane projects may include daylighting, and expresses willingness to discuss additional investment with the council.
chapters.title: CM Abreu questions OMB on lack of daylighting funding

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I just have one question.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: On the campaign trail, the mayor supported the expanded use of hardened daylighting.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: During our executive budget hearing, however, DOT said there is no funding budgeted for daylighting in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Has there been any discussion with DOT about providing funding to expand daylighting?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So there has been obviously a back and forth with DOT about the needs.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think that it is, we know that we have the 400 that has been budgeted.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that when we do bike lane projects, that as part of that, there could be

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: daylighting included as part of those projects.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's something we look forward to discussing with the council in the weeks ahead.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We are also making investments in, you know, street safety overall.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're committed to street safety.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're putting, you know, funding in for Sammy's Law.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We put funding in for more bike lanes.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We put funding in for pedestrian centers on the side of medium duty trucks.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we are committed to street safety and we look forward to having conversations with the council about additional investment.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Yeah, at my executive hearing, I did mention that we are grateful for funding in certain areas, like the ones you've mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: However, I'm disappointed in the lack of funding for daylighting, given that the mayor campaigned on this issue, and it's something that we see no funding for, specifically.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: for.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Advocates are calling for $15 million for daylighting.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: What has your engagement been with the advocates and what is your estimate of the need to meet those goals?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Well, I defer to DOT in terms of engagement with the advocates, but I do, you know, again, I look forward to having the discussions with the council about additional investment in daylighting.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Director.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Deputy Speaker Williams, followed by Majority Whip Hanks.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Williams asks about baselining the remaining $20 million for cultural organizations beyond the $10 million included. Solomon cites the funding envelope constraints and expresses shared commitment to cultural institutions, looking forward to adoption discussions.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions OMB on cultural funding baselining

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just a question about the cultural funding.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So in the council's budget response, we called on the administration to baseline the $30 million that was received in a one-shot last year to adequately fund the ongoing needs of the city's cultural organizations, the exec plan.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thankfully, it included $10 million baseline, but we wanted to know if the administration would be adding the remaining $20 million baseline funding to the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We all value our cultural institutions together.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I know we all agree on that.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We were happy to put up the $10 million into baseline.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I do think that we look forward to having the discussion with you and with the council on additional funding moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Obviously, with the...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: you know, with the overall fiscal constraints in mind and to try to figure out how we can provide more to our cultural institutions.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And just wanted to have a little bit more understanding as to why you all chose the $10 million to include in the exec budget,

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: which I'm sure you know forces a lot of the cultural organizations to continue to operate year to year with a lot of budget uncertainty.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah, it was a matter of just looking at the total funding envelope of the executive budget and with, you know, the commitment to see that we could

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: put up funding for the institutions that we care about and not sort of wait or defer them to adopted budget negotiations.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we had picked.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The 10 million because of the funding envelope.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But we recognize that it's also a significant priority for the council.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We share the fact that we want to invest in our cultural institutions, and we'll look forward to those discussions over the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, just to jump around a bit and tease this out, but I definitely have a ton of more questions on this topic, as well as libraries.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about OMB's racial equity plan tools for agencies. Solomon describes developing a framework to screen new needs against racial equity goals, inspired by climate budgeting, targeting the next budget cycle, with two-way accountability between OMB and agencies.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions OMB on racial equity budgeting tools

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So OMB's racial equity plan states that the agency intends to develop tools by 2027 that would allow city agencies to incorporate equity principles into budget requests and planning for the FY28 preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: What specifically will those tools look like and how?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Farah-N.-Louis is OMB in developing them.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We, as noted in our plan, are committed to having a rubric that OMB uses to systematize, really, how we're looking at

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: making investments that align with the racial equity plan and making sure that we have a way to screen those new needs that are coming in to be able to say, okay, does it meet this need?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Does it meet this need?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we are developing that framework now.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have committed in our plan, which is published in the preliminary racial equity plan, to have that ready for the next budget cycle.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So for the next preliminary budget, it would have been informed by this new framework.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we're happy to be able to talk to you about it in greater detail as it gets fully developed, but we are committed to having some system and framework for being able to assess

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: whether a new need comports with the racial equity goals that we put forward in the plans.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I know that your office created a climate assessment to look at the city's budget.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So would it be similar to that assessment?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Would it look a little different?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think we are looking at climate budgeting as something that could be instructive for how we view the racial equity plans.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As you know, it's something that exists.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's something that screens for a particular goal of government.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: to be able to understand how funds are being invested.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So there are parallels to there, and we will look to that to be instructive in how we develop our plan.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Hanks asks about the $4 million right-to-counsel pilot for small businesses facing commercial lease harassment. Solomon explains it differs from the existing commercial lease assistance program by providing counsel after disputes arise, and that the amount was determined by the overall funding envelope.
chapters.title: Majority Whip Hanks questions OMB on SBS right-to-counsel pilot program

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Majority whip hangs, followed by Councilmember Areola on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: It's good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So my line of questioning is about the right to counsel pilot of the Department of Small Business Services.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: The executive plan includes an additional $4 million in fiscals 2027

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: and 2028 for a right-to-counsel pilot program at SBS designed to provide negotiation and settlement support for small businesses experiencing commercial lease-related harassment.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: My question is, how did the administration arrive at this estimate for the cost, and how will this program differ from the core commercial lease assistance program that SBS currently operates?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the commercial lease assistance program is really designed to be sort of

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: pre, it's like the advice and counsel that we can provide and assistance we can provide to small businesses

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: as they're going into negotiating a lease to make sure that they're best positioned to be able to get the best deal for that particular small business.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The right to counsel

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: comes in

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: after the fact, right?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So it's sort of like where it lands in the process, where right to counsel then will be if there is a dispute and there is a need for counsel at that time by the particular small business.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And I think that we came up with the number, just again, it comes back to the funding envelope overall.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We said, can we make an investment here?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This is a real need to support businesses.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And that is how we arrived at the $4 million.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So does the administration have any estimate of when the RFP for contracted legal services be issued in order to get these services ramped up for fiscal 2027?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We will work closely with SBS to make sure we understand what their timing is.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This is something that the administration believes in.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That's why we're making the investment and making sure that they can access these services because we want small businesses to thrive in our city.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Hanks raises that six months into the administration, EDC still lacks a president. Solomon says the search is ongoing and a priority, but notes EDC continues working on SPARC Kips Bay, Kingsbridge Armory, World Cup, and free grocery store projects.
chapters.title: Majority Whip Hanks questions OMB on EDC president search status

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: One more question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: The New York City's Economic Development Corporation, New York's EDC, is the agency who, you know, their mission is to encourage economic growth in each of the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: We are now six months into the new administration, yet we don't have an economic development corporation.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: They lack a president.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: What is the status of the administration's search for a new president of EDC?

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: And could you speak on why it's taking so long?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The search is ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It is a priority.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: At the same time, we know that the agency continues to work on a number of projects.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: that have been in the pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So whether it's SPARC, SPARC project in Kips Bay, we know the Kingsbridge Armory is another project that continues.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Obviously the work that they're doing to prepare on the World Cup and on the

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: on free grocery stores as well.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So all the work continues in that agency.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Areola on Zoom, followed by Councilmember Shulman.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Ariola asks about the fifth firefighter expansion, EMS pay raises, and firehouse disrepair. Solomon looks forward to discussing the fifth firefighter, notes ongoing labor discussions for EMS, and highlights $24 million in FY31 capital for six firehouse renovations with willingness to explore accelerating funding.
chapters.title: CM Ariola questions OMB on fifth firefighter, EMS pay, and firehouse repairs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Areola, are you there?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yep, I'm not sure if you can hear me.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, because I'm not getting it.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I want to go over a couple of things that

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: that we discussed at the executive budget hearing for fire and emergency management.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So in your testimony, I did not hear anything about the fifth firefighter, that expansion that has been proposed by the speaker and the council, EMS pay raises.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And I would like to know what you plan to do with both of those topics, as well as

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: taking out your money and making it available now for firehouses that are in terrible disrepair so that they can be fixed sooner than later.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you for your questions.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So on the fifth firefighter, we look forward to having that discussion with the Council.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that fire safety is of paramount importance.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know the Council put forward a proposal.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know prior to that, the union had put forward a proposal.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Again, we look forward in the next few weeks to have that discussion.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: On the EMS parity, you know, this is something that, you know, the fire commissioner believes deeply in.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: and has testified to that fact.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think the mayor also has publicly talked about the challenges that are faced in terms of EMS.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We look forward to working with the new union leadership.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There is a new president that was elected for that local.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There are conversations between the Office of Labor Relations and that local, and we look forward to having a collective bargaining discussion.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Where we can hopefully make progress on the last EMS contract round.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And then finally, on your question on firehouses, we were proud to include in the executive budget capital funding to renovate six firehouses.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That will be obviously determined in terms of the most sort of in need of state of good repair.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we're proud that that was a start.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Your suggestion about looking at out-year capital funding to accelerate, we will take a look at.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're constantly looking at our capital plan and seeing what kind of opportunities may exist to advance funding.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But we put up funding for the six firehouses as a start.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we want our firefighters to be in, you know, state-of-the-art facilities as best as we can.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we want to invest the dollars to do so.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And just one last question.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The money that you're appropriating for the six firehouses, is that immediate?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Get money or is that out your money?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That is.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have $24 million for FY31, and we will look at being able to, again, per your suggestion, seeing what can be advanced.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Purina Sanchez, Gail Brewer, and Selvena Brooks-Powers, and we have Shulman, followed by Dinowitz.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks how vacancy reductions at DOHMH avoid impacting services. Solomon explains agencies have flexibility in implementing reductions, and notes added funding for community outreach teams, mobile food vending, disease surveillance, and the new public health lab maintenance.
chapters.title: CM Schulman questions OMB on DOHMH vacancy reductions and public health funding

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The executive plan includes savings associated with long-term vacant positions at DOHMH and reflects ongoing efforts to manage fiscal constraints while maintaining public health services.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How does OMB evaluate whether vacancy reductions can be achieved without impacting service delivery or DOHMH's core public health functions?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have an extensive engagement with the agencies to make sure that this exercise should not in any way result in any reduction to services.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We leave the flexibility to the agency to understand how they would effectuate the 50% vacancy reduction.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I would also note that in other ways we're adding funding to the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene for heads that they need, whether it's

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: the community teams that will do outreach to make sure that folks are signing up for health insurance access and

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: other needs that we've added for mobile food vending, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What funding and staffing requests submitted by DOHMH remain under OMB's review ahead of budget adoption, and what public health investment, if any, does OMB anticipate may be added to the adopted budget?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That's a conversation we look forward to having with the council, the agency.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We had extensive engagement with them in the lead up to the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We were able to meet some needs, including funding disease surveillance.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're also funding maintenance for the new public health lab that's opening up, in addition to the other investments that I noted in the first part of my answer.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I think we're constantly talking to the agency if there's anything that develops over the next few weeks, but clearly as well with the council.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about risks from loss of federal public health funding. Solomon highlights the reversal of Article 6 public health funding cost shifts worth $60 million annually, and the importance of disease surveillance investments given recent outbreaks and pandemic history.
chapters.title: CM Schulman questions OMB on federal funding loss and state support for DOHMH

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The executive plan includes a baseline $17.6 million increase to support disease outbreak resilience and sustain core public health functions previously supported through temporary federal grants.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: As the federal government continues to reduce funding for New York City, has the state included any funding in its enacted budget for any of DOHMH's programs and services?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Well, one of the things that we were especially proud of this year is that we were able to reverse the Article 6 public health funding cost reversal,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: which had existed for many, many years, and we were able to do that, which is worth about $60 million a year to us.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So through our advocacy efforts and through the partnership with the state, we were able to do that.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So that is an example.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: of additional state funding for public health.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What assessment has OMB conducted of the potential risks to disease surveillance and outbreak response resulting from the loss of federal public health funding?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Well, I think that's exactly why we thought it was important to make the investments that we made in the executive budget for the disease surveillance, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I think that it's important that we never lose sight of the fact that we're only a handful of years away from a

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: once-in-a-century pandemic, but we also see outbreaks, whether it's hantavirus, whether it's Ebola.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we need to make sure we're always in a state of readiness, and that is why we made that investment.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Chair, I just have one more question.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sure, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Schulman raises that council districts no longer have assigned CAU liaisons. Solomon offers to take the concern back to Office of Mass Engagement leadership. Schulman also notes for the record her role in pushing for the Article 6 reversal.
chapters.title: CM Schulman questions OMB on community engagement unit (CAU) assignments

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I just want to follow up a little bit on Speaker Menon's question about the mass engagement unit.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Up until recently, every council district was assigned somebody from the community engagement unit, from CAU, and that doesn't really exist anymore.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So what's being done to make sure that that...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Continues to happen because without that it's very difficult to get things done unless unless some I mean some of us have relationships with certain people and IGA and everything else but we were assigned a particular CAU person

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I'm happy to take that back, Council Member, and talk to leadership for the Office of Mass Engagement about that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, and just for the record, Article 6 was something that I...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: came in office and pushed for us, and we were able to do it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We worked with the state.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We appreciate the support of the admin, but I just wanted for the record to state that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for building the foundation for us.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we've been joined by Councilmember Aviles, and next, actually, sorry, Eric, we have Councilmember Caban on Zoom, followed by Dinowitz.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Cabán asks about contingency plans for federal threats to gender-affirming care, particularly for youth. Solomon notes $15 million added for gender-affirming care in prelim budget. Cabán responds that advocates say $60 million is needed, with specific gaps in youth services.
chapters.title: CM Cabán questions OMB on gender-affirming care funding

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I know that we're tight on time, so I'm going to ask both of my questions up front.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They're very different topics, so I apologize for that, but I'm going to start with a question on gender-affirming care.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'd like to know what policies and resources OMB is prioritizing ahead of adoption as a contingency plan because of the federal government threats to further protect gender-affirmative care in the city, particularly for youth.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then my other question has to do with IMT and ACT.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about restoring and baselining $15.5 million for mobile treatment teams and Step Down programs. Solomon says OMB will take back the baseline question but looks forward to adoption discussions on ongoing ACT and IMT funding. Cabán contrasts funding priorities: 500 more cops but no money for trans youth healthcare.
chapters.title: CM Cabán questions OMB on IMT/ACT mental health teams baseline funding

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: In your testimony, you mentioned that the city is improving New Yorkers'health and well-being by supporting access to mental health care.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Now, last year, the council secured an additional funding of $15.5 million to expand the mobile treatment teams and create the Step Down program.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then we were informed a few months ago, very surprisingly, that the funding has to be baseline for DOHMH to implement.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And, you know, I hope that we can agree that the city can't afford to bargain with funding for effective mental health care services.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: This remains a priority for the Progressive Caucus.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can we expect the restoration?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: and baseline of the 15.5 million at adoption.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I just want to clarify that also, you know, the money that we're seeing put in by the city is to address the fiscal cliff left by the federal government and not actually

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: do anything additional to make more headway on the wait list that currently exists.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So thank you, Council Member, for your questions.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Let me first take the first one.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we added in the preliminary budget $15 million over fiscal 26 and fiscal 27 for gender-affirming care.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We look forward to working with the Health and Hospitals Corporation on how that funding will be utilized.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I just will say that's not nearly enough.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Advocates are saying that the need is in the ballpark of more like $60 million, and also there needs to be money earmarked specifically for youth services because gender-affirming care, not every practitioner can provide that at young ages.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So every major medical organization in the country has said that it is integral, life-saving medical care, including children 13 and under, and there's a real gap in being able to service that population.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yes, Councilmember, it is a start.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We hope that within the fiscal constraints that we have, we're able to invest additional resources there.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think that this is a start.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And, of course, it's an important start given the health needs that we know exist.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I think the next piece of your...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Question was related to IMT and to ACT and your point

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: we will take back in terms of the baseline in question but just in terms of the four and a half million and the 11 million in one time ads for ACT and IMT

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: we're looking forward to the discussion as part of ADOPT in terms of ongoing funding for that and so we look forward I know to having those conversations with you and your colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, and just before I pass it back to the chair, I just want to say, you know, in terms of the fiscal challenges, most of the people on Rikers live with a mental health diagnosis, could benefit from an active fact team.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We need this medical care coverage for our transgender youth and transgender neighbors in the city.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We got money for 500 more cops in this executive budget, but we don't have money for health care and trans kids.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think, you know, that's a question of priorities.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And yes, agree.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Fact, IMT, ACT, those teams do incredible work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So whatever we can do to try to uplift them would be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Dinowitz, followed by Councilmember Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz raises that DOE requested approximately $40 million for K-12 special education programs including AIMS, NEST, and Horizon, which were not funded. Solomon notes $150 million invested in special ed pre-K and ongoing discussions about K-12 within the funding envelope.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz questions OMB on K-12 special education funding gap

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yesterday we held our executive budget hearing with the Department of Education, and yesterday

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They testified that they submitted a new needs request for K-12 special education programming, including their AIMS, NEST, and Horizon programs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Notably, this budget does not include increases in K-12 special education.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They testified, additionally, that these funds would reduce due process cases for Carter cases for tuitions at private schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That was their testimony.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And therefore, it would reduce the costs to our city's budget by doing what we're supposed to be doing, which is educating every child.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: First, how much did DOE request for this, and why was this request not funded?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we are obviously continuing to talk to DOE about their new needs overall, and particularly on the due process cases.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As you know, we've made, and as I mentioned in my testimony, we've made investments to the tune of $150 million in special ed pre-K.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In pre-K, I'm speaking specifically of K-12.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: You're talking K through 12.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But I'm just saying that we did make investments, $150 million in special ed pre-K, in making sure that IESP, so that we're having lower costs in terms of children that need services.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're looking at K through 12, and we're looking at the overall funding envelope.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Ultimately, we've made many investments in DOE within our executive budget, so we're looking at the available funding, and we're happy to talk to you in the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, to be clear, how much money did they request?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Liz, do you know exactly what the new need was?

utterances.speaker: Liz Hoffman
utterances.text: I don't have that on me, but I will get it for you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, we can get that today.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's in an email somewhere, or is it in a...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're happy to follow up with you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Today?

utterances.speaker: Liz Hoffman
utterances.text: Yep, I can do it right now.

utterances.speaker: Liz Hoffman
utterances.text: Thank you, Liz.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Liz Hoffman
utterances.text: It was around $40 million.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And to be clear, they testify that for Ames, for example, there are 510 seats, but 2,070 applicants.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That was just for the Ames program.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Fantastic graduation rates, attendance rates, success rates.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz questions cuts to school safety agents and failure to restore $6 million for restorative justice, which DOE testified would impact 25% of schools. Solomon cites 3,801 SSAs with 8% vacancy rate, new dismissals agents, and states restorative justice will be addressed through the Office of Community Safety.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz questions OMB on school safety agent cuts and restorative justice

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Similarly, the executive plan includes a cut to the number of budgeted school safety agents and a failure to restore $6 million in restorative justice program, which is functionally a cut.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And the DOE testified yesterday over 25% of schools would lose restorative justice programming without this funding.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So how are you supporting school safety and why was this funding not included in the executive budget?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the school safety agents have a headcount of about 3,801 as of the executive plan with a vacancy rate of roughly 8%.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The total budget is about $283 million in fiscal 27.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This is a conversation that we want to have with you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We did add dismissal agents to address the busiest times and just hired a class of 100 school safety agents who are anticipated to begin in June.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We, on restorative justice, the executive plan includes funding to create, obviously, the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And, you know, within that, we intend to address restorative justice proposals, programs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, well, I'm not sure how you do that with a cut when the DEWE testifies.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: 250 of their schools of the 900 roughly 960 schools that have this programming would lose it and share if I have one more

chapters.summary: Dinowitz raises Tracy Towers facing 30.6% rent increases over four years, contrary to the mayor's affordability promises. Solomon says OMB is not involved in rent increase oversight but offers to discuss HPD's role in ensuring affordability in city-managed Mitchell-Lama properties.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz questions OMB on Mitchell-Lama rent increases and HPD oversight

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You know the mayor mayor mom Danny ran on a promise of affordability and rent freezes But notably that rent freeze excludes our city managed properties including our city-run Michelamas

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: HPD oversees and manages these these developments over a hundred developments having approval and authority over operations.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: How many HPD asset management staff are dedicated to ensuring the health of city-managed Michelama properties?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And what role does OMB play in ensuring that developments remain affordable and healthy to low- and middle-income New Yorkers and co-op owners in these developments?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I want to highlight Tracy Towers in my district, which over the next four years will face a 30.6% rent increase.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And over the last four years faced a 22% rent increase, which is as about...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Opposite of the mayoral promise that he campaigned on as you can get.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're happy to look into that and have a discussion with you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Can you describe OMB's role in working with HPD to make sure not only the investments that we allocated for the City of Yes funding and the state provided

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: are going towards these buildings, but OMB's role in ensuring that rents don't skyrocket so the residents of Tracy and our other city-run Mitchell Lamas can live affordably and with dignity?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We are not involved in the oversight or review of rent increases.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we will be happy to talk to HPD and look at their oversight and supervision of the program and happy to have a discussion with you about if there's any way that agency services can be focused on that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We'll continue the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you for the extra time, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Lewis followed by Councilmember Ong.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Lewis asks how the $22 billion housing capital plan compares to prior administrations and whether agencies must absorb planning costs for city-owned property development. Solomon points to the LIFT task force for city property opportunities. Boirard explains most funding goes to HPD or DCP for environmental studies.
chapters.title: CM Lewis questions OMB on housing capital plan and city-owned property development

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Two questions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm going to conjoin them together.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The mayor's block-by-block housing plan commits more than $22 billion in housing capital funding over the next five years, with approximately $4 billion in the new HPD capital funding as part of the broader $22 billion housing investment strategy.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So I wanted to know, how does this level of capital funding compare to the prior?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Mera housing plans on an annual basis and what specific portion of these funds represent truly new capital investments versus previously planned commitment?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And my second question is, does the administration's housing capital plan also include funding specifically dedicated to studying and advancing housing development on top of existing city-owned property?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And if so, are agencies expected to absorb planning and redevelopment costs within their own capital budgets, or is OMB establishing centralized funding sources to support feasibility studies, design work, and so on?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure, so let me start and I'm going to turn it over to our first deputy director to address that.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I think in your second part of your question with respect to...

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Agencies.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Right, in terms of on-city property.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have the LIFT task force that is specifically focused on ways that we can seize opportunities to build on city property.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so there's a team together that's constantly looking at ways that we have property across the city to be able to build housing there.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I do want to turn it over to Tara to talk about your first piece of the question in terms of like what our capital, how our capital investments.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: rank in terms of housing plans.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: A few aspects to your question in terms of if the agencies would be anticipated to absorb the cost of that additional development.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Unlikely.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: That's more of a development proposal as opposed to an agency operational need.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: So when that need would be identified, it would either be funded out of HPD's capital budget or if it was an environmental study required, we would look at it within DCP in their EIS budget.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: In terms of how this ranks compared to annual funding from prior administrations, we would have to take a look at that.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: What I can tell you is that the vast majority of funding within the five-year plan is going towards housing.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: So the $22 billion is on a base of $117 billion in total.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Ong is actually not here, so Councilmember Wong, followed by Krishnan.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Wong raises concerns about a net decrease of nine DOB positions and asks whether inspector vacancies will be reduced. Solomon and Boirard explain the vacancy reduction exempts revenue-generating positions and that agencies prioritize inspectors, with 58 current vacancies.
chapters.title: CM Wong questions OMB on DOB inspector position reductions

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have a question about the DOB on cutting off inspectors.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The executive plan includes seven new positions at DOB, but also includes savings as a result of the elimination of 16 positions at the department for a net decrease of nine positions.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: This is extremely concerning as DOB plays a critical role in inspecting building hazards.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My question is how many DOB

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: B, inspector positions were part of the reduction of the executive plan, if any.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And can you confirm that no inspector vacancies will be reduced as a result of the headcount decrease in the executive plan?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I think first thing is that when we did the vacancy reduction exercise, we ensured that there would be flexibility for the agencies to make determinations based on their funded headcount.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: At the same time, we wanted to make sure that revenue generating positions were not also being swept up in the vacancy reduction.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So DOB received seven new positions solely to help them execute the recommendations of the speed report.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so the vacancy reduction exercises again, revenue generating exempt.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So it's up to the agency to make that determination.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Would you add anything?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: It is up to the agency to make that determination.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: But, you know, inspectors is something that the agency does prioritize.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: It would be self-defeating for us to pull inspector vacancies out of the agencies, and they do have 58 vacancies at the moment.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I see.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about implementation timing for DOB sidewalk shed reforms and how many sheds will be removed. Solomon says DOB is in the rulemaking process and has sufficient resources to implement the reforms once rules are promulgated.
chapters.title: CM Wong questions OMB on sidewalk shed reforms timeline

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My second question also regarding DOB on sidewalk sheds.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Earlier this year, DOB announced sidewalk shed reforms to be implemented by this summer, this year.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Reforms would extend the timeline for...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: inspections an extra year, create an abbreviated filing program for lower risk, well-maintained buildings to every 12 years instead of five,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: and mandate sheds only extend to a maximum of 40 feet away from buildings.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My question is,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: When would these new regulations be implemented?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And how many sidewalk sheds does the administration hope to remove this year and in the next four years?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you answer that, please?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the DOB is currently in the rulemaking process to be able to move forward on meaningful reforms on sidewalk sheds.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we know that there is sufficient resources that the agency has to make sure that they implement the rules on sidewalk shed.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we look forward to talking with you more once those rules have been promulgated.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have one question for round two.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We'll add you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Krishnan, followed by Narcis.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Krishnan raises that DOI has only $175,000 in public funds after fixed costs, forcing reliance on drying asset forfeiture funds. Solomon commits to ensuring a well-resourced DOI and is confident progress can be made in the adopted budget, including the $4 million for the 9/11 toxins report.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions OMB on DOI funding and 9/11 toxins investigation

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee and Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Just a few quick questions.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Good to see you as a budget director and the LMB team.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: The Department of Investigation has a detailed history of rooting out corruption, especially in the last administration.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: The last administration also slashed their budget, too, year after year.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: But its mission is both one of independence and also very sacred for the work of city government.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What we heard from the...

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Plenary budget hearings and executive budget hearings is that once you take out the fundamental payments they have to make, rent and things like that, they're only left with $175,000 in public funds for their budget, which is very, very little.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And it forces them to have to look to their asset forfeiture funds to really supplement and boost their core operations, which is also drying up too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So I know we've talked about this before too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: How is OMB thinking about increasing DOIs funding to make sure they have the resources they need for their mission?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So thank you, Councilmember, and for your advocacy here.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I know that, so I've met with the Commissioner on a couple of occasions and her team, and we've gone through their budget in great detail.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I've seen, and I've seen the actual trajectory of their asset forfeiture funds and how they're being used to cover sort of your basic day-to-day.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we know that the past is true, that they've had significant sort of reductions in their budget over the last several years.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so we are committed as an administration to rooting out corruption, waste, fraud, abuse, wherever it exists across government.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We look forward to having the conversations with you and with the council on making sure that we have a well-resourced DOI.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I'm confident that we can get there in this adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm glad to hear that and look forward to the continued conversation.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And similarly, there's a $4 million need that DOI has to be able to do the investment.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: into publish the report on the knowledge that the Giuliani administration, Bloomberg administration had of toxins in the air on 9-11.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So how are we looking at that?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Is OMB able to commit to putting that $4 million in this budget?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We also look forward to having those conversations with the council in the next few weeks and fully recognizing that that is sort of

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: separate from, separate but related, clearly, but separate from their day-to-day needs and what they need for their PS and OTPS budget.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Glad to hear it.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks about increasing parks funding toward a proportionate budget and supporting PEP officer lines expiring June 30th. Solomon highlights $15 million baselined for parks covering rangers, forestry, and maintenance workers, and looks forward to discussing PEP officers.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan questions OMB on Parks Department funding and PEP officers

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And switching gears for a minute, I know we share the same vision for increasing our Parks Department funding and how crucial that is too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: There are still PEP officers, for example, about a third, that don't have the funding in place yet once their funding expires on June 30th.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And there's a larger question of making sure our parks overall get more funding to ultimately get to a place where they have a proportionate city budget, proportionate to the overall city budget, and to other cities across America.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So how do you all see increasing the parks budget in this budget and specifically supporting worker lines that are expiring on June 30th?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we were proud to put forward $15 million and be able to baseline that for parks.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we identified some titles that it would cover.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know urban park rangers, forestry management, parks maintenance workers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that there's been highlight on...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: on PEP officers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We look forward to having that discussion with you and with the council in the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Narcisse, followed by Banks.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Narcisse asks about protecting H&H if federal Medicaid matches shrink and about FEMA COVID reimbursement status. Solomon notes ongoing state conversations on Medicaid and reports H&H has received over $2 billion in FEMA COVID reimbursements with the vast majority of the portfolio closed.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse questions OMB on Health + Hospitals and FEMA reimbursements

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you, Director, and all your leadership.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Go Knicks.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We can win this.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The city's support.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Serious now.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The city's support to health and hospital relies largely on supplemental Medicaid payment that draw directly down a federal match.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: If that match shrinks, does the city have a plan to protect the health care of those who rely on it the most on H&H?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We are grateful to have a health and hospitals corporation that is a safety net for so many New Yorkers who need to access health care.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This is something we are focused on to make sure that the hospital system can continue to thrive.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that we have been in conversation with the state.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Clearly, a lot of the Medicaid funding flows from the federal government to the state and down to us.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we need to make sure that whatever the state's plans may be to address what the impacts of HR1 will be, what the trickle-down effects may be on the city, so we know how to fully sustain our health and hospitals system.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: On FEMA, we are...

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
