utterances.text: The executive plan includes an additional $176.3 million in H&H budget in fiscal 2026 as federal reimbursement for costs incurred during the COVID-19 pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How much FEMA COVID reimbursement funding that has H&H received to date?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How much longer do you anticipate H&H to receive this reimbursement funding for?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is H&H being reimbursed at a sufficient level for the cost it incurred during the pandemic?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Does OMB work directly with FEMA to determine the reimbursement amount H&H is receiving?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the Health and Hospitals Corporation has closed, FEMA has closed really the vast majority of the city's FEMA COVID portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Health and Hospitals has received over $2 billion in reimbursement for eligible costs during or against, I should say, FEMA COVID grants.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I heard the speaker start talking about shelters, but I have one question for you.

chapters.summary: Narcisse questions why homeless services spending is higher now than during the migrant crisis peak. Solomon emphasizes the primary goal is permanent housing through the block-by-block plan targeting 200,000 new affordable units, while acknowledging the legal obligation to serve the unhoused.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse questions OMB on homeless services spending and permanent housing

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How come during the height when we had all the migrants, we were coming in and we have more money for homeless services, we have more money in the budget now for the services for homeless?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: In our city while during the height of the migrant crisis we had less.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So which direction are we going?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Are we putting people in permanent housing or we plan to continue using shelters and to serve the population that we have in New York City?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think as demonstrated in our block-by-block housing plan, we are committed to moving full steam ahead on an ambitious housing plan, 200,000 new affordable

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: units as our goal, 200,000 units, homes preserved as well.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so the plan is chock full of strategies to be able to address that.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So our primary goal is to connect New Yorkers with permanent housing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That is the goal.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That's where we want to be.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We also have an obligation to serve the unhoused, and that is why you see a Department of Homeless Services budget and so on and so forth.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: address what had been, as we discussed, chronic under budgeting in primarily the social services area

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: by adding billions really when you look at shelter, when you look at city FEPS that had been under budgeted, when you look at cash assistance.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Those are three areas among the top six that we talked about in terms of addressing chronic under budgeting, but the goal is

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Permanent housing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That's what we need to focus.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I think that's the best way to go next.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Let's win this.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Let's do it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Nice.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Banks questions how OMB determined $500 million for PACT conversions versus $90 million for Section 9. Boirard explains funding was based on shovel-ready projects and pipeline needs, with $2.7 billion going to PACT/TRUST and $2.8 billion to Section 9 over FY26-30.
chapters.title: CM Banks questions OMB on NYCHA PACT vs Section 9 capital funding balance

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Banks, followed by Morano.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Good to see you, Director.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: When it comes to NYCHA, the executive capital commitment plan includes an additional $500 million in HVD's capital budget for RADPAC NYCHA conversions.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We heard from NYCHA last week that this amount was determined by OMB.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Can you explain how it was determined that $500 million was the appropriate amount for additional funding for PAC?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: The plan includes only $90 million additional capital funding for Section 9.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Compared to the $500 million for PAC, what criteria does OMB use to determine

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: and how to balance investments in NITRA Section 9 versus PAC.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And has the administration considered adding large sums of funding for alternate capital improvement programs such as CompMod, Comprehensive Modernization, and Order Preservation Trusts?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I'll start and then I'll turn it over to...

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And if you can't answer the question fully, please do it in writing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're happy to follow up with you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But actually, why don't I turn it over to Tara Barard to talk you through it.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: So in terms of the funding that went to Section 8 conversions, the $500 million, we looked at which projects were shovel-ready.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: We looked at what the pipeline was in terms of what the agency wanted to do with both PACT as well as TRUST.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Another factor was that the funding for PACT and TRUST kind of falls off in the out years, so we had to make sure to give them another lifeline to keep that pipeline going.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: In terms of the total amount of funding that we have for NYCHA, it's well over $5 billion.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: And in fact, the money that we have going to Section 9 exceeds what we have going to PACT and TRUST.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: So if you look at FY26 to FY30, $2.7 billion going to PACT Trust, $2.8 billion going to NYCHA Section 9.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Well, we're talking about the next fiscal year, which is 2020.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: I'm talking about 26 to 2027.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So in 2027, what is the dollar amount that's going into RADPAC?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: or privatization compared to what is going into Section 9?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: In 2027, there's a billion dollars that's going to Section 8 conversion, but that's split between PACT as well as the trust.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: And then there's an additional...

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Just repeat, what's the total amount of money?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: These are straight questions.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What's the total amount of money that's going into RADPAC for 2027?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Compared to what's going into Section 9?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: As I stated, it's a billion dollars going to Section 8 conversion that is split between PACT and trust.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: So as NYCHA comes to us with...

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And how much is going into Section 9?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: $841 million.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Now, the last part of your question, I believe, had to do with CompMod and that alternative strategy.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: So generally speaking, that's not a model that we see that can be easily replicated because it does not draw down on additional Section 8 federal resources.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: It basically means that NYCHA and the city would have to pick up the full freight of the development cost, and we're trying to do this in a more efficient manner.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Well, the Comp Mod Fund, the city can invest in that fund, am I correct?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Can.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And the city has opted out not to?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: I think that we're looking at a whole series of strategies in order to bring down the P&A cost.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Part of that is PACT, part of that is trust, part of that is updates to Section 9.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: How much funding has been invested in the preservation trust?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: That is within the Section 8 conversion pipeline, so it's going to be a total of $2.8 billion between 26 and 30, combined with PACT.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So how many developments will be converted or get the opportunity to go trust preservation?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: We'll have to get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: NYCHA has to tell us about what's going through the pipeline, but we're in the process of closing.

chapters.summary: Banks presses on why the administration relies on PACT conversions rather than comprehensive modernization, calling it privatization. Boirard explains CompMod doesn't draw down federal Section 8 resources and would require the city to pick up full development costs, which is less efficient.
chapters.title: CM Banks questions OMB on comprehensive modernization vs privatization for NYCHA

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: If you can give us some clearer numbers as to how much is going into each, because obviously you've abandoned comprehensive modernization, which is something that has worked and continues to preserve public housing.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And you've continuously

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: dumped more monies into privatization of public housing, and that is definitely concerning.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And that leads me to my next question, if I may, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: The mayor's housing plan recognizes NYCHA's critical role in addressing New York City's housing crisis, and I've heard that continuously.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Why does the administration continue to rely heavily on private development partnerships and RADPAC conversions rather than the advancing

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: a comprehensive strategy to reinvest and preserve traditional Section 9 public housing?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: It seems contrary to his philosophy.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: I think if you look at the numbers, it tells a clear story.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I've seen the numbers.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Okay, I'm just answering your question.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: If you're looking at the total budget amount, you'll see a total of $22 billion going to housing.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: A full quarter of that amount is going to NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Of that amount, you have over half going to Section 9 preservation.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: And within the balance that's going to PACT and TRUST, a substantial amount will be going to the TRUST, which is fully within NYCHA management control.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And the, just want clarity, the administration continuously uses the word comprehensive or NYCHA renovations.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Is that just a coded word for privatization of public housing?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Of public housing?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: I think that actually refers to the

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: the level of upgrade that's going to be done to the developments as opposed to...

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So in that term that's being used, it's not privatization of public housing.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: I couldn't really speak to where you're seeing the term being used.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: I think in general, if you're talking about...

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: This was in the director's...

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: I believe if you're looking at comprehensive modernization, you're really looking at hard costs within the developments and what it takes to upgrade each of the apartments.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And my last question.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Can I put you in round two?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Is that okay?

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Morano asks about the five largest components of the FY27 gap and whether costs were genuinely unforeseeable or known obligations not budgeted. Solomon lists six under-budgeted areas totaling $7.5 billion and confirms it was a transparency problem, not a forecasting problem.
chapters.title: CM Morano questions OMB on inherited budget gap components and transparency

utterances.speaker: Tara Boirard
utterances.text: Councilmember Morano followed by Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you for your leadership and endurance during these hearings as well.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: It's greatly appreciated by all of us.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony, Director Solomon.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Your testimony stated that this administration inherited a $2.2 billion budget gap in fiscal year 26 and a $10.4 billion budget gap in 27, right?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We talked about a $12 billion gap that we inherited, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So for the benefit of the committee and the public, can you identify the five largest components of that fiscal year 27 gap?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: More importantly, what I'm trying to get at, were those costs genuinely unforeseeable, or were they known obligations that simply hadn't been fully budgeted?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we talked about the six.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: big unbudgeted or under budgeted areas.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We talked about cash assistance, shelter.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We talked about city FEPS, it's in the social services bucket.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We talked about due process cases, judgments and claims, and the city's MTA subsidy.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Those six account for, account it, because we added the funding, $7.5 billion that were unbudgeted.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the administration came in.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We said we need to be transparent.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We need to level with New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We need to be able to budget what are known.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: expenses, known trajectories in programs that we've observed and that when I came in to speak to all these talented professionals told me these are the actual costs and we assessed it.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So it wasn't a forecasting problem, it was a transparency problem.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In our front.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yes, it wasn't put forward.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So I definitely think New Yorkers are eager to know not only what was inherited, but what happened afterwards.

chapters.summary: Morano raises the Comptroller's analysis that FY27 may contain a $1.65 billion gap. Solomon says OMB believes it has accounted for under-budgeting, notes the Comptroller applauded the transparency, and offers to discuss any additional costs the Comptroller identifies as missing.
chapters.title: CM Morano questions OMB on Comptroller's $1.65 billion gap analysis

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Of the fiscal challenges facing the city today, how much is attributable to obligations inherited from the prior administration, and how much is attributable to policy choices made since January 1st?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: What we've talked about is in this executive budget, what you see.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: or in terms of over the course of the preliminary and executive budget, what you see is $17 billion

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: that has been added in expenses, which is a very large amount because a lot of it, again, was the chronic underbudgeting.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And 92% of that.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Is to address the under budgeting and the core government functions that the city needs to run.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That's snow removal, that's IT, that is these programs that are mandated in many cases by either the federal government or state regulation, state law.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So that accounts for that.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So 8%, just 8%.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Of that, $1.39 billion, right, has been added for programmatic investments.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Before I wrap up, Chair, the controller, Mark Levine's budget analysis released this morning concludes that fiscal year 2027

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: may not actually be balanced and instead contains approximately a $1.65 billion gap.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Does OMB disagree with the controller's analysis?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If so, what specific assumptions does the controller make that OMB believes are incorrect?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We believe we've accounted for under-budgeting, and I think the controller actually confirmed that and actually applauded OMB and the administration.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: for making sure that we put up unbudgeted costs in a way to be more transparent.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In terms of additional costs that the controller is highlighting, that they assert are not in the budget, we're happy to have that discussion and see what they're seeing and what they feel as though is not reflected.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we can move forward.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But we have addressed what is effectively chronic under budgeting in our, you know, in the city's budget system.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And for round two, I have some questions about veteran services.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So stay tuned.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember Marte.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Epstein notes an OMB report said DCWP needed 321 lines but the budget only provides 181 over two years, leaving roughly 150 positions short. Solomon says the new commissioner conducted a top-down assessment and the funded lines are sufficient for current legislative mandates.
chapters.title: CM Epstein questions OMB on DCWP staffing shortfall vs OMB's own report

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And next we have Councilmember Epstein, followed by Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you all for your time and patience here.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I want to follow up on what the speaker raised earlier about the DCWP funding.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We saw an OMB report earlier in the year which said they needed 321 lines to be able to match all the additional work the agency is going to be responsible for.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I know we've talked about 77 lines this year.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And a total of over 180 over the next two years.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It does seem like based on what OMB's earlier report said, that would still be close to 150 positions short to keep up with the needs that the agency has.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm wondering how you square those two decisions.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We've, in this administration, worked with the new commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Who has done sort of a top-down assessment along with City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: This has been flagged as a priority.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Obviously for the mayor and the commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We worked with him to understand what his agency needs were and with the legislative mandates that apply to the agency.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And that was the number that we were able to do, which the commissioner also testified was sufficient for being able to carry out the duties by those local laws.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, when we had the hearing with the commissioner, we talked about whether these additional staffing would result in no backlog.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: People not waiting here, he couldn't commit to that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: If we say that there's going to be continued backlog, as OMB committed to trying to get additional staff lines, so New Yorkers who have a

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: wage hour overtime violation doesn't have to wait six months to be able to speak to someone, not even the timeline it would take to actually prosecute a case to get them their money back.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The mayor has been very vocal and is committed to ensuring that there is restitution that is achieved for workers that have been

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: the subject to these actions.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so that is what the department will do.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We'll continue to assess with the department if there are...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Constraints between the heads that they have and between their ability to fulfill the full mandate by the local laws that they've been entrusted to implement.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I noticed there's no money in this budget for people who are advocating for delivery workers or street vendors who are doing the out.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks why no funding was included for nonprofit organizations doing outreach to street vendors and delivery workers in multiple languages. Solomon notes SBS received funding for multilingual outreach to mobile food vending, and that direct nonprofit funding would be a Schedule C conversation for adoption.
chapters.title: CM Epstein questions OMB on street vendor and delivery worker outreach funding

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm wondering why there was no funding put in to ensure that the appropriate outreach and the language capacity by the organizations that are doing that outreach has been funded to support this work.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We will look at, I mean, we did add, I know you mentioned specifically, you said delivery workers.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Delivery workers, street vendors, people who are doing...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Street vendors, we did add over a number of agencies, including SBS to do the outreach in multiple languages.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I was talking about nonprofit organizations who have the relationships already with the street vendors and the delivery workers.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Those are the people who are the critical infrastructure here, and there's no funding in the budget to ensure they have the resources to talk about the additional laws.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Well, the direct funding that would go towards organizations would be a conversation for Schedule C as part of ADOPT as direct to those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But we did make sure that we went through the small business services to make sure that they have the money, they have the funding they need to ensure that they can do outreach in multiple languages to the mobile food vending community.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Just if I can just quickly, Chair, just to...

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is there any capital money?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know Chair Dinnowitz mentioned capital money for Mitchell Lama.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about capital money for struggling Mitchell-Lamas and funding for the animal welfare office. Solomon notes the state budget included $60 million in capital for Mitchell-Lama and offers to follow up on the city's capital plan and the animal welfare office.
chapters.title: CM Epstein questions OMB on Mitchell-Lama capital funding and animal welfare office

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is there capital money put aside to give to Mitchell Lamas who are struggling?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We have a lot of Mitchell Lamas who are facing 20% increases, and it's because of the capital needs.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is there any money in the capital budget for these Mitchell Lamas?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I will note that the state budget included $60 million in capital funding for Mitchell Lama development, but we'll get back to you on our capital plan.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And then animal welfare office in the mayor's office, was there any increase in funding for that office?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I'm happy to have these conversations with you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we'll circle back to Councilmember Ressler, and next we have Councilmember Eldable.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Restler raises that pension re-amortization could cost $7.6 billion over 10 years and asks about next steps, union negotiations, and bond rating risks. Solomon clarifies the $7.6 billion is for all employers, with the city's cost at $5 billion, and reports positive discussions with all four rating agencies.
chapters.title: CM Restler questions OMB on pension amortization risks and bond rating

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I have a question about pension amortization.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The state budget included a provision that will push off or re-amortize some required pension payments over the next five years, the unfunded accrued liability.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: While the maneuver is estimated to generate as much as $2.2 billion in the short term, it could cost an additional $7.6 billion over the next 10 years.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Additionally, changes the state made to improve pensions for workers in Tier 6 is estimated to cost another $1.4 billion.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Over the same period.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What are the administration's next step towards pension amortization and what is left to be negotiated with the municipal unions?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And how will the city, how does the city plan to close bigger gaps in the out years?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Of the financial plan owing to this amortization?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And do you believe that there are any risks to the city's bond rating as a result of the amortization?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure, so thank you for your questions, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Amortization, as I noted in my testimony, is a prudent way to pay down what is a long-term debt obligation by flat level dollar payments.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The figure you cited about the $7.6 billion cost over the plan, that is for all employers in the pension systems.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's not only the city of New York, it's the MTA, it's health and hospitals, it's NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There are different employers that participate.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So the cost over time to the city would be $5 billion.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But the savings as well for the short-term fiscal stability is...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: is also significant as well.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In terms of the status, the state law requires that it be approved by the boards of trustees of each of the systems.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We are pleased to report that the Board of Education Retirement System has approved.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that the NICER's board is coming up on Thursday, and then the other boards will come up for the remainder of the month.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we look forward to seeing those votes approved by the boards of trustees to actually effectuate the proposal.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In terms of the rating agencies, we've been in constant contact with the rating agencies, as we do after we release every plan.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I've met with the four rating agencies last week.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we had a very positive discussion on what this proposal does.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we talked about the fact that it was level dollar, flat dollar.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we talked about the funded ratio for each of the pension systems.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: New York City's five systems at 86% versus the national average at 79%.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Ressler.

chapters.summary: Restler details 25,600 children on a childcare waitlist, delayed mandated voucher take-up creating available funding, and asks about one-year SECC vouchers for FY27. Solomon confirms the maintenance of effort will be met and expresses willingness to discuss the one-year voucher suggestion in coming weeks.
chapters.title: CM Restler questions OMB on childcare voucher waitlist and SECC vouchers

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Good to see you, Director and team.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And firstly, I just want to commend you all for balancing the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It was no easy feat, and I think in particular took

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: impressive skill in navigating Albany and securing significant

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: support up there and that didn't happen by accident and you and the first deputy mayor and others deserve a great deal of credit for for accomplishing it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'd like to try to cover two topics today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Child care vouchers first of all.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we have 25,600 children on a waiting list today who are in desperate need of child care support.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we have not yet heard any commitment from the administration about whether they will begin to invite children on that waiting list to access child care vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There are a couple of things that I'd like to just get clarity on and then see if we can get some support today from you on this.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Firstly, there are 31,000 children who are enrolled in mandated HRA vouchers today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: For some years, we've been getting estimates from HRA on when they anticipate reaching the 63,000 threshold, again, of children on mandated vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: First, we heard that it was going to be in January of 2028, then March of 2028.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Most recently, they testified last week that it's not until September of 2028 that we're going to hit that threshold, which means we have significant resources available for the non-mandated voucher population, low-income vouchers that our families need, these families on the waiting list.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you identify how much savings we have as a result of the delay in the take-up on the mandated vouchers?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Secondly, it was recently reported that the city has only spent $105 of the extra $350 million that the state has allocated for voucher funds.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This is the maintenance of effort funding where when you spend this money, we get matching dollars from the state.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We need to make sure that it's spent down.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you confirm that every...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: penny of that $350 million will be spent by September.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And then thirdly, just to keep it simple, what I've been advocating for with some colleagues in the council and child care advocates is that we spend money this year on SECR vouchers,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: the special child care funding vouchers that are a one-year commitment instead of an in perpetuity commitment until children age out at age 13.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can we get

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is OMB open to working with us on a one-year model to use the extra funding that we have, that we've identified and just, you know, happy for you to confirm exists, to expand child care vouchers for this coming fiscal year?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So thank you, Council Member, and thank you for the kind words.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think that...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So first on vouchers writ large.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So yes, you cite what we were able to accomplish in Albany and the additional funding that we got for the vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The city tax levy funding that you speak of to meet the maintenance of effort will be met.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We will put up that funding.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we intend that we will spend the full amount of what is provided by the state and what is provided in city tax levy to

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: continue to fund vouchers for the eligible population as it exists right now.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Now, you mentioned the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Of course, the wait list, you know, does exist.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We'd like to get there within the existing funding.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We think that we can cover the existing population.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: However,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: However, if there should be attrition that happens in the existing population, or should we see that overall state market rates go down,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: there may be an opportunity for us to, with existing funding, be able to reach into the way that we're going to go down.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Not sure market rates will go down, but the state has delayed implementation of the increase in the market rate, which is helpful.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And the attrition rates we've seen are steady.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we know what the resources we have.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We can make the evaluation today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can we add families for a one-year voucher commitment instead of an imperfect?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, I get that it's a major financial commitment for the city to say we're going to enroll a child in a voucher at age four that they're going to have until they're 13.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But it's less of a commitment to say this year we know we have the money because the mandated take-up is dramatically slower than OMB and HRA and ACS had anticipated.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have extra resources that we can spend in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can we start to offer children on that waiting list, SCCF vouchers, to help connect them to child care for this coming fiscal year?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're happy to talk to you about your suggestion and look at it in the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I have more, but I think I'm round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Round two, yes.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Round two, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Becoming a longer list of round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by P.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sanchez.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Brewer asks whether Solomon's DOI meetings included the $4 million for 9/11 toxins investigation (yes) and whether OMB will fund DOF's $255,000 request to codify their open records portal. Solomon looks forward to discussing both in coming weeks.
chapters.title: CM Brewer questions OMB on DOI 9/11 toxins funding and DOF FOIL portal

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's nice to hear answers for the last four years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We didn't have any answers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'll be honest with you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I say it like it is.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: A couple issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In terms of DOY, I know you have met with the commissioner, but my question is, did that include the $4 million for the 9-11?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Discovery.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It did.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So you're going to pay that?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We are going to look forward to the conversations we're going to have with you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think what we were able to do is, and I met with her on two occasions, to look deeply at where they were in terms of an operational standpoint, and then obviously compliance with the resolution passed by the council on,

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: yes, yes, by you, Councilman, to basically, you know, to fund the effort that's needed for the 9-11 toxins investigation.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We look forward again in the next few weeks to talking about how we have a well-resourced DOI.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's an order of magnitude question, right?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we have to be able to work together and figure out what that order of magnitude will be, given the funding envelope that we have.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, some small issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Doris, I love Doris, and they have the FOIL responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They need $255,000 to codify their open records portal, which will help us with FOIL.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that you will fund?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's something we look forward to discussing with you in the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about $450,000 for COIB staff and attorney, and about EV purchasing costs for 640 vehicles annually. Solomon notes ongoing conversations for COIB and details $4.7 billion in capital for greenhouse gas reduction projects including $28.9 million for medium-duty EVs over three years.
chapters.title: CM Brewer questions OMB on COIB funding and EV vehicle purchasing

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then also COIB, another agency that pays attention to corruption.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They need $450,000.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know that you are paying for new paras to them, terrific, but they need extra to have a better staff salary and to hire an attorney.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that you would find?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think they're all part of the conversation that we intend to have.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then in terms of electrical vehicle,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: purchasing power, DCAS, you probably have the best commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: She's smarter than everybody else, I just want to let you know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No kidding.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So in that case, do you have an estimate of how much additional expense or capital will be needed to purchase 640 vehicles every year to ensure the annual electric vehicle targets are met?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that you're focused on?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have in the five-year capital commitment plan more than $4.7 billion, not just for EV vehicles, but for projects that reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And in there, we also have funding, I believe, for electric vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We can follow up with you on the exact amount.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We do have actually medium-duty electric vehicle purchases, $9.6 million in FY27, FY28, and FY29, for a total of $28.9 million over the next few fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We can have a discussion about whether that includes the vehicles that I'm talking about.

chapters.summary: Brewer raises that DYCD's RFP changes upset every council member and asks about CUNY ASAP/ACE/Reconnect funding. Solomon says it wasn't cost-saving but an RFP issue being reviewed with the commissioner, and highlights $15 million baselined for CUNY plus $50 million in capital for state of good repair.
chapters.title: CM Brewer questions OMB on DYCD after school RFP controversy and CUNY funding

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The other thing is in the recent DYCD, a good commissioner, but she managed to upset everybody, not just one, but every single member with her new ideas about RFP and changing some of the after school programs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that a cost saving issue or just like thinking that you're doing the right thing, but it's really not?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I don't think it was a cost-saving issue.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think it was an issue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't think it was either.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think it was a release of an RFP and the responses to the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that you could look at or that would just not be in your bailiwick?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We are looking at it with the DYCD commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, really it's upset every single person imaginable.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I just would start over.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Suggestion.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I could help you with that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: CUNY, ASAP, ACE, and Reconnect.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know that you're focused on CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We all love CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Those programs need to be fully funded.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that you're focused on also?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We all love CUNY, for sure.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We love it, but we have to fund it.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Well, we did put up the $15 million and baselined it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes, you did.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's great.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we also put up $50 million in capital for state of good repair, the most that they've received for that in over a decade.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So that was an important investment, too.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In terms of the programs which we know work, ASAP, ACE, and CUNY Reconnect, we look forward to having that conversation with you and your colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about planning for Medicaid/Medicare cuts under Trump. Solomon notes close coordination with H&H and the state on Medicaid, and highlights investments in food assistance programs including Community Food Connection, Groceries to Go, and SNAP eligibility specialists, while acknowledging the city can't fully backfill federal cuts.
chapters.title: CM Brewer questions OMB on healthcare and SNAP threats from federal government

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In terms of health care, there's no number, but we're all concerned whatever Trump is doing, Medicare, Medicaid, mostly Medicaid.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How are you thinking about that as a future challenge in terms of people getting cut off?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't know whether you go to New York City Cares.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't know if you just show up at the emergency room.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's obviously going to cost more.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah, I think, as I mentioned, we're working closely with the health and hospitals and with the state because we need to understand what the state intends in terms of Medicaid funding.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: that may be cut from the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: At the same time, we know that SNAP is also a real concern, and that's why we made strategic investments, whether it's the Community Food Connection Program, whether it's groceries to go, whether it's get the good stuff, whether it's investing in SNAP eligibility specialists.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We're committed to making sure that we have food assistance for those who need it, and so it's an ongoing conversation.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think it's been said before that

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The city doesn't have the capacity to backfill the magnitude of those kinds of cuts, but where we can, we will act and make those investments in partnership with the council.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know the commissioner of DYCD is working hard in terms of the after school program.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Not hard enough.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Well, hopefully we'll hear some good news.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we have Pierina Sanchez followed by Brooks Powers.

chapters.summary: Sanchez questions whether a $1.9 billion three-year no-bid contract for homeless hotels is a good investment. Solomon emphasizes the primary goal is permanent housing but the city has legal obligations and capacity constraints requiring hotel use, with active efforts to move families into shelter capacity.
chapters.title: CM Pierina Sanchez questions OMB on $1.9 billion hotel shelter contract

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I hope somebody is passing you snacks, sugar.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I saw a granola bar being eaten by Councilmember Ressler, but he offered some.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Hint, hint.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: You got to restart my...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Somebody get them some sugar.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In March, the New York Post published that Mayor Mamdani signed a $1.9 billion three-year no-bid contract for DSS to house homeless households in New York City hotels on an emergency basis.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Homelessness, we know, leads to community instability, family trauma, more disrupted childhoods, more families in crisis.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Does the administration believe a $1.9 billion contract to house families in hotels over the next three years, homeless families in hotels, is a good investment for the city of New York?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So let me start, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I'll pass it over as well to Patrick DiStefano.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I think, as was mentioned before to Council Member Narcisa's question, our primary goal is to focus on housing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And I know this is an area that's near and dear to your heart.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We need to make sure that is the first approach, that's the first goal.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: At the same time, we have an obligation, legal obligation, to house the homeless.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we know that there has been, over the years, has been capacity constraints that required the reliance on hotels.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We have, as part of our cost containment, looked to move out as many families as possible from those hotels into where the shelter capacity exists.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: There's more to do, clearly, but we also have to fulfill our legal mandates as well.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Patrick, I'm not sure.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm so short on time, but thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Director.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I understand that these funds will be dispersed as they are used.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: If the administration agrees that this kind of sheltering is not optimal, what steps is the administration taking to ensure that we use as little of this?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: contract as possible and I'm just going to sneak in my next question is a clarifying question from general welfare hearing.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks OMB to confirm that CityFEPs houses roughly double the people at half the cost of shelter. Solomon agrees CityFEPs is a lifeline but does not directly confirm the cost comparison, noting shelter census continues rising in certain populations while voucher savings haven't yet materialized on the shelter side.
chapters.title: CM Pierina Sanchez questions OMB on CityFEPs vs shelter cost comparison

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Is this a fair characterization?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: FEPS houses roughly double the number of people, City FEPS houses roughly double the number of people at roughly half the cost as shelter, not accounting for this no bid contract I just mentioned, about 82,000 people

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: with $3.2 billion being spent in shelter contracts and staffing versus 153,000 people, double the number of people, at $1.8 billion for City FEPS.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Is this a fair characterization?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The DSS Commissioner did not answer my question during her hearing, so I'm hoping that you all will.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I think that with respect to the City FEPS program, we know that it's a lifeline for many New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that we want to be able to focus on how we could sustain the actual program.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know that as well, we've seen the shelter census continue to increase in certain populations.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: The families with children began to decline, but we've seen single adults continue to increase.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so we, as the city FEPS voucher program continued to rise and hadn't been budgeted for, as you know, as we discussed, we haven't also seen those savings in the shelter side.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We would like to see that happen, and we're actively managing with all of the new leadership that we have.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: both at the agency level and at City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's something we're constantly focused on.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we want to move out of hotels.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We want to be able to connect people with permanent housing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That is our goal as we look to move forward as an administration.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: FEPS houses double the number of people at half the cost in shelter.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Fair characterization or not?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I would respectfully say that we agree that FEPS is a lifeline for New Yorkers, and we'll continue to work with you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I know you're passionate about this issue, and we're having productive talks.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I don't know if that's a demotion or a promotion.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: These are numbers that you all, the agencies, are putting out, so it would be helpful to make sure we just have the same understanding.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So thank you, Director.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to take a brief pause because, Deputy Speaker, do you want to introduce the special group?

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Williams interrupts the hearing to welcome students from PS 147 in her district, giving a shout-out to the student council members she recently visited and helped arrange a City Hall tour for.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams welcomes PS 147 student council to the hearing

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yes, I'm so excited to interrupt this very important budget hearing to welcome a school from my district.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yes, PS 147.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And I just wanted to send a special shout out to the student council I visited, I think, last week or the week before last.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And they were very excited.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: They said they had tried to get a tour at City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: One person was actually graduating and I was like, let's make it happen.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So shout out to the student council, Holly, Steven.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Trish K, Prince David Pierce, Michael, Farzana, Bryn, Tessa, and Aiden.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair, and of course to our OMB director for allowing me to interrupt.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thanks for coming.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we have three more folks, and then we'll take a quick break after that.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Brooks-Powers asks why the executive budget includes no funding for the Rockaway trauma center despite $50 million previously secured and a publicly owned site. Solomon says he's evaluating cost estimates and funding availability, but cannot provide a commitment, drawing frustration from Brooks-Powers who raised this since preliminary budget.
chapters.title: CM Brooks-Powers questions OMB on Rockaway trauma center funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we have Councilmember Brooks-Powers and then Councilmember Aviles.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and I've already put my name for round two.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So in round one, I will focus on the district.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Round two, I'll focus on my chairmanship position.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So it's good to see you, Director.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: First, I'm actually going to ask you all three questions, and then I can repeat whatever you need to do.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So the Rockaway community for decades has advocated for a trauma center, and we have been able to secure $50 million in capital funding as well as a publicly owned site for the facility.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Rockaway residents are closely watching whether this administration plans to invest in the trauma center.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can this administration explain why there is no funding in the executive budget for the trauma center and whether you intend to rectify the situation in the final budget?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The next one is Senator Sanders has allocated $1 million towards the trauma center, but has been told that he cannot move the state capital funds to health and hospitals for this project.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Health and Hospitals has informed

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: us that OMB is not allowing for the transfer to move forward.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can OMB please explain why the funding that is already secured in the state budget cannot find a home within health and hospital?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And lastly, the council appropriated $10 million for the Brookville Park Recreation Center in fiscal 24.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks why $10 million was removed from the Brookville Park Recreation Center capital plan, leaving $22.5 million of a $32.5 million commitment. Solomon offers to look into the funding change and continue discussions, but Brooks-Powers expresses frustration at the lack of answers after previewing questions the day before.
chapters.title: CM Brooks-Powers questions OMB on Brookville Park Recreation Center funding removal

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Yet that funding was later removed from the capital plan.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Subsequent commitments to restore those funds have only been partially fulfilled, resulting in roughly $22.5 million

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: currently budgeted, despite a combined council and administration commitment of $32.5 million.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Why did OMB remove funding that had already been appropriated?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Why was I not notified before these changes occurred?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And when can the community expect the remaining $10 million in committed funding to be rectified in the capital plan?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I know that we've had a couple of conversations about the trauma center.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Really one yesterday, but...

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Well, also in the cow when we had a little brief conversation.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: But yes, I do agree that this is something that we need to look into.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I know that we spoke about the money that was existing in the budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We talked about what else would be needed.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We talked about whether or not the actual estimate was a correct estimate vis-a-vis.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: what we had said versus what was put out there in terms of the overall dollar cost.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I am evaluating all of that with the team and looking at exactly what the estimates are.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we're talking about what the actual numbers are that we can all agree with.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We need to agree on a number first and then talk about how there's available funding in the funding envelope.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm sorry, Director.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I will say, I'm sorry, one moment.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Because I will say while...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: It's one thing to look at the final dollar tag, which I do not expect the administration will cover.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I would love for you guys to cover the full balance, but I doubt it.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So at the same time, what is the administration's commitment in this fiscal year in terms of funding in the final budget for the trauma hospital?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The previous administration had provided $50 million, not including funding that the council has also funded on the expense side of this.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I want to know what this administration's commitment is going to be.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And that's obviously a conversation that we'll be having with you over the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: My point was that in order to make a determination about, particularly when it talks to capital, because with capital funding, as you know, if a project isn't fully funded, you can't do the certificate to proceed.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Capital project can't.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we need to figure out what the actual, we need to have an agreement on the numbers.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: You had mentioned that H&H said it may be this.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We knew that our team was saying something.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So I'm going to talk to the parties about it and figure out what the actual number is.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Then we need to look at the funding envelope.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's just a matter of funding to see what is actually doable.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I'll look into the Senator Sanders $1 million as well.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: In terms of the rec center, we also discussed that, and we'll be going back and looking at, as part of discussions that happened in the past, what was the change to that funding that went from, I think, $10 to $5 million, you said?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Yeah, it's a balance of $5 million.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Now, Director, we did, to your point, speak about this yesterday, so you had a preview of what I was going to ask today.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So I would imagine that you would have some sort of an answer for me right now under oath in this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I do not, but I'm happy to follow up with you as I continue.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can I ask why you're not prepared when we spoke yesterday and you knew that I was going to ask these questions?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Beginning of a conversation, and I'm happy to continue over the next few weeks with you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But I've been asking this question since the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And there's been no progress and no indication from the administration that we are moving beyond this statement that I'm getting right now.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And we also talked about a provider for the actual facility.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And we spoke about the provider being a part that's happening simultaneously with the securing of the funding.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I don't want this administration to hide behind that as an excuse when we have been successfully securing funding.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: in the last several budgets and prioritizing this from the administration.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Mayor Mamdani has shared with me privately that he understands the need in this community and the fact that health care is a human right, but I'm not seeing that demonstrated in action.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So I do ask that, Chair, if possible, before this hearing is done, that I can get an answer, because I did preview these questions, and I think it's unacceptable to not get an answer to basic questions.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I want to know if the administration is going to commit to some level of funding for the trauma hospital, as well as restoring the $5 million that was removed from the Brookville.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Park Recreation Center.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we'll work with you on hopefully getting some sort of answer on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, I have like 10 papers in front of me.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember Hankerson as well as Mealy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we will hear from Councilmember Aviles, Marte, and then Hankerson.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Avilés asks about restoring $53 million in immigrant legal services funding cut from last year, noting the partnership with OMB on designations. Solomon highlights $33 million baselined in FY27 and $54 million total in FY26, and expresses willingness to continue working together.
chapters.title: CM Avilés questions OMB on immigrant legal services funding restoration

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I want to start with immigrant legal services.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: You know, last year, first, thank you for baselining the $33 million at the preliminary.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Last year, we saw, we had, there was a $53 million that we have not seen the restoration of, of this additional funding for this year.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: A portion of that funding was

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: used to restore, honestly, the lost federal grants that we had seen for legal services in particular.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I have to press that, you know, the funding restoration that happened of those 53 million, really, we had a great partnership with O&B staff around making those designations

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: to service providers, and we would definitely like to continue that partnership.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Can we receive a commitment from the administration that it will continue to fight for that $53 million, particularly

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: at this moment in time where the federal government continues to harm immigrant communities and threaten on a daily basis that they're going to flood New York City?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah, we're happy to continue to work with you on this.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: I know that the immigration legal services in terms of funding is at $54 million fiscal 26, $71 million fiscal 27.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As you noted, we had added $6.7 million in fiscal 26 and $33 million in FY27 and out.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So we did baseline it.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: So it is a significant sort of down payment on what we know is needed, particularly at this time.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We appreciate your advocacy on this issue as always, and we look forward to working with you more in the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And again, it was a great partnership with DSS, OMB, the council to make those designations.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We'd like to continue.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: to do that work together.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Surely.

chapters.summary: Avilés presses for $100 million capital and $70 million expense to address 6,200 vacant NYCHA units. Solomon details $374 million total allocated ($256 million capital, $118 million expense) as historic funding, but Avilés argues it's insufficient and units need to be front-loaded, not stretched to 2030.
chapters.title: CM Avilés questions OMB on NYCHA vacant unit readiness funding

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
