utterances.text: On NYCHA vacant unit readiness, our favorite, one of our favorite topics, thank you for your collaboration in trying to address this profound issue of the 6,200 units that remain vacant throughout NYCHA campuses.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Obviously, we have been pushing to

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: obtain 100 million in capital and 70 million in expense to really be able to meet that full need to obtain the full scope of getting those units ready and online for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Can you tell me

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: how what progress is being made and expanding those commitments to meet that goal?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Sure, we agree.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Obviously, that's why we added funding, the most that we can add, given the funding envelope to the budget.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's also something that is highlighted in the block-by-block housing plan.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: It's something that we really want to lean into.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We've allocated nearly $374 million to the vacant unit and readiness program.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: That's total in terms of talking about $256 million in city capital funding across fiscal years 2026 to 2028 and $118 million in expense funding across fiscal year 2026 and fiscal year 30.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: When you really put this in perspective, it's an historic amount of funding for the program.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: We know there's always more to do, but I think given the backdrop of the fiscal challenges that we have, the fact that we stepped forward with this investment shows meaningful progress.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that investment.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I think we'll continue to push that.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: That New Yorkers and NYCHA residents can't wait till 2030 to be transferred out of an apartment.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: That needs to get front-loaded now, or at least we need to try to maximize, in the short, the amount of units that we are able to turn over.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And we still feel like we haven't hit that right threshold.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We are still far behind.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And not as urgent in this funding.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And again, this would house thousands of New Yorkers immediately.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We don't need to build new buildings.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We don't need acquisition.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: These are New Yorkers that can be housed today within the next two years, not 2030.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We'll continue to have that conversation and keep pushing to maximize that investment in the immediate.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Because when we do the numbers over the long term,

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: It's a bump up, but it's not where we need to be.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Marte, followed by Hankerson.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Marte asks about the updated NYCHA capital needs number. Solomon confirms it's $78 billion as of January 2026, a slight decrease from $78.6 billion. DiStefano explains the decrease reflects progress on PACT, TRUST, and Section 9 renovations bending the cost curve.
chapters.title: CM Marte questions OMB on NYCHA physical needs assessment update

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I want to plus one what Councilmember Avila said about NYCHA and continue on that front.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And the mayor's block-by-block housing plan notes that the capital investment in NYCHA since 2018

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: 2023 has begun to slow the growth of NYCHA's P&A for the first time in recent history.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: The most cited number of the authority total needs of approximately $78 billion from 2023.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Is there a more updated number than this 2023?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Or is there a number going to come out soon that's more recent to make a better assessment?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: As of January 2026, the estimated P&A is at $78 billion.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And so it's a slight decrease from the 2025 estimate of $78.6 billion.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And how did you determine that?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: What factors led to the slowdown?

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So the physical needs assessment is done by NYCHA, not by OMB, but in terms of how they're attributing to the decrease, it's really the progress that's been made on the developments.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So everything that's happening with, will be happening with the trust, with the pact, with the renovations to Section 9, helps to bring down the cost and bend the curve.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Marte asks about the OCC budget ($7.5 million in FY27-28, dropping to $2.5 million in FY29-30) and why CRI funding decreases in out years. Staff explain the first two years include one-time costs and funding will be reevaluated annually.
chapters.title: CM Marte questions OMB on Ombudsman Call Center (OCC) and CRI funding

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: You know, we were excited about the news about the CRI, which is based off the Ombudsman Call Center, but just wanted to know what's the total fiscal 2027 budget for OCC?

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: For fiscal 27, it's $7.5 million.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: Fiscal 28, $7.5 million.

utterances.speaker: Sharif Solomon
utterances.text: And obviously, we'll see how the out years go with respect to funding.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And I have a question about the out years because we see a decrease.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: increase for CRI as well.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: You know, I think they're funded 7.5 million for fiscal year 2027, 2028, but then it drops to 2.5 million in 2029 and 2030.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Why does this funding decrease in those outer years?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so that's the one that we just referenced, but in terms of the funding dropping in fiscal 29?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think the first two years there are some one-time costs that are being picked up, and then we'll be reevaluating on a year-by-year basis.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Hankerson, followed by Councilmember Mealy, and then we'll take a break.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry about that.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Hankerson asks about baselining remaining one-shot parks positions including PEP officers and the plan to reach 1% of the budget for parks within the mayor's term. Solomon highlights $15 million baselined and the 1% commitment, but cannot provide a specific timeline, stating it depends on continued fiscal stability.
chapters.title: CM Hankerson questions OMB on Parks baseline funding and 1% goal timeline

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Director.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: The Council has, for many years, called on the administration to baseline one-shots for a variety of initiatives within the Parks Department, specifically for headcount, including...

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: PEP officers, forestry workers, green thumb workers.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: We were happy to see the executive plan included baseline funding for some of these.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Does the administration plan to baseline the remaining one-shot positions in the budget, which would include PEP officers, which are desperately needed right now, as well as

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: green thumb programming and tree stump removal programming, which is near and dear for my district in Southeast Queens?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember, for your question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We were proud to put up the $15 million for parks and baseline it and not wait until adopted budget negotiation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: to be able to deal with that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's going to fund urban park rangers, going to fund forestry management and other parks workers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know that it's been highlighted that PEP officers also need funding, and so we look forward to discussing that with you and with the council in the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: From your estimate, do you believe that the administration will baseline?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are happy to talk about more investments in parks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, the mayor has and has always talked about more investments in parks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know we have a 1% goal that the mayor has committed to during his mayoralty, before the end of his mayoralty.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we'll continue to talk about ways to invest in this great public amenity.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Well, I'm glad that you brought that up.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: So, given the mayor did make a commitment to getting parks to the 1%, what is the administration's plan to get there within the term?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We obviously came into office, we faced a staggering $12 billion gap.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As mentioned in my testimony, we worked really hard to make sure that we were able to stabilize the city's finances.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Now that we've done that, we've done that without...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Without increasing the property tax.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've done that without rating reserves.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've done that without slashing services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So as we become more fiscally stable, we can talk about the investments that we need to make.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've still carved out of all the expenses that we've added this funding for parks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've carved out money for libraries, for cultural, things that would typically have waited for adopt.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we baselined it because we believe in those investments in our institution.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: And not to cut you off.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'll get there.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: I believe that.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: What exactly is the plan?

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: You spoke about what you all have done, which I think is amazing.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: But given that you've been able to do that, I'm sure that there's some plan to be able to get to where we need to be with parks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This is the beginning of putting up the $15 million in the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As we move forward, the plan in terms of overall budget, continuing to pursue savings and opportunities, and then looking where we can free up funding to make those investments.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: If I may just one final question, if that be the case.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: How can you say that we'll get there within the term if there's no plan to get there within the term right now?

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: If you're saying we need to see where we can pull from, but the commitment has already been made now twice, once that we would get to 1% and now that we'll get to the 1% within the term, but there's no plan, I'm kind of lost here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Our goal is to meet that 1% before the end of the mayoralty.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: At the same time, the twin goal is to make sure the city is financially stable, to be able to make the kind of investments that both you and I

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and I agree need to be made in our parks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it's an ongoing effort, and we're going to work with the council as partners on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Mealy?

chapters.summary: Councilmember Mealy raises concerns about SNAP work requirements now applying to adults 60-64 under HR1, with June being the first month people could lose benefits. Solomon notes investments in SNAP eligibility specialists, Community Food Connection, and other food programs, but cannot provide specific waiver estimates.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OMB on SNAP work requirements for older adults

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Older adults and SNAP benefits.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Many low-income older adults rely on

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: healthy, heavily on SNAP benefits, HR1 expanded SNAP work requirements, and now adults up to 64 years old

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: who are deemed able to and subject to the same work rules as younger recipients.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: People between the ages of 60 to 64 who did not previously have these requirements could lose their SNAP benefits.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: If they are not working, this June is the first month where people will lose their SNAP benefits if they do not meet the work requirements or obtain a waiver.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: What is the administration doing to address the potential impact of the work requirements on older adults between the ages 60 to 64 years old?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So this is something that is a focus of ours.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know that through our deputy mayor for health and human services, she's looking at ways that we could mitigate the impacts of what is to come based on the federal government's actions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've invested in more SNAP eligibility specialists.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've been, Patrick, you have.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: On top of your head on the SNAP eligibility specialist.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You've had it?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'll get that for you as I continue to give my answer.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Should we, it's a demographic of people that need.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Do you know what this would happen to 60 and 65?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: We have to know this already before this budget passed.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: In order to have our seniors have to get a job to get SNAP benefits, that's life and death.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So it's no plan.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: You don't know how much you're going to put extra right now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've invested in the Community Food Connection program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've invested in the Groceries to Go program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've invested in Get the Good Stuff program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we are investing in food assistance.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So how many people you feel are going to get a waiver to not work?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Did you estimate that into play?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is something we're working on with our deputy mayor of health and human services and our agencies.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: That's kind of unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What I would also add is I don't know the number of SNAP eligibility specialists off the top of my head, but in this plan, we added nearly $70 million for HRA staff.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So in addition to doing away with the two-for-one, I mean, they are hiring up rapidly for these benefit access frontline critical positions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They've also identified a specific phone number for SNAP so you can call to make sure you're getting your benefits.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's a lot of outreach.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So they're doing a lot in this field.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Well, I'm going to follow up with you.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: We're about the home-delivered meals.

chapters.summary: Mealy raises that CBOs are reimbursed $14.78 per meal when the true cost is $16.17, asking how the gap will be addressed. Solomon notes the per-meal rate was increased by $1 and that OMB continues discussing appropriate costs with the agency, while Mealy emphasizes CBOs are getting shortchanged.
chapters.title: CM Mealy questions OMB on home-delivered meals reimbursement gap

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Is the administration considering adding funds to New York City agent budget to support high reimbursement rate?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: For home delivered meals to cover the true cost.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: About right now, you're saying it's $14.78.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: But when the administrations get reimbursed, it's really $16.17.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: How would you...

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Reimburse them with that gap.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So as of the executive plan, we've programs contracted budgets $81.1 million, supporting 4.8 million meals for older New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we increased the per meal rate by $1.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We regularly address the change in cost as appropriate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Patrick, I'm going to go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So what is the appropriate cost now?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We continue to discuss that with the agency to make sure that we're covering

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What is appropriate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know that there's outreach in this area.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're working with agency on it.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: But you know the CBOs, they are getting reimbursed half of what they really need.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: That's not fair to the CBOs just as well.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee announces a five-minute break before resuming with second round questions from council members.
chapters.title: Chair Lee calls a five-minute break before round two

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we're going to take a five-minute break, and then we are going to resume with round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can we find our seats?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can we find our seats?

chapters.summary: Lee raises the $111 million SNAP cost shift from HR1 not included in the executive plan or state budget. Solomon confirms no state funding was provided and no confirmation from the state that the reduction will be passed to the city, but if confirmed in a future plan, OMB would have to reflect it.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on SNAP cost shifts from HR1 and state budget

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we want to make sure you guys don't stay until dinner time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we want to get you guys out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we're going to go right into second round questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, where do I begin?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm going to start with the HRA SNAP state and federal funding cost shifts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So at the General Welfare Committee's executive budget hearing, I brought up the snap cost shifts resulting from HR1.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We had hoped that the state would step up and include this funding in its enacted budget rather than shifting the burden to localities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The first and most imminent cost shift is $111 million.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: for the city's share of the state's new SNAP costs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The executive plan does not appear to include this funding in HRA's budget to support this.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: At the general welfare hearing, HRA testified that this funding will likely need to be added in a future financial plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So can you confirm that no funding was included in the enacted state budget to support this?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There was no funding provided, but at the same time, we do not, we have not heard from or had a confirmation from the state that this

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: $111 million would be passed along as a reduction to the city.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So not in the budget, no confirmation from the state that that will be passed along.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if not, have there been, so you just mentioned there have been conversations with the state about, did they say anything about appropriation of funding towards this in the future?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They didn't.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We obviously, you know, in the lead up to the enacted budget had several conversations and the result, of course, as we talked about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So but this wasn't something where they indicated or confirmed that this was going to be

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: a reduction to the city's budget in the same context that we were talking about ways that we were going to receive additional state aid to help us with gap closing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and do you think that we'll need to be?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Adding this into the FY27 adopted budget?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, I have to wait and see what, if there's confirmation from the state, there was nothing, again, in the enacted budget, so we don't have anything confirmed for the state for us to act on in our budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But if it comes to pass in a future plan, then we'll have to reflect it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We would obviously want to make sure we're engaging with the state to be able to convey what the hardship would be with that $111 million if we had to see that reduction.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, because this is definitely going to be a huge impact to the community in New York.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got to figure this one out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: One of the many conversations we'll be having in the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Additionally, starting in October 2027, there will be an additional cost shift to states that could result in a 15% increase in the cost to states.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The rate will depend on the state's error processing rate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And HRA testified that the state noted that this cost shift would likely impact HRA's budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does OMB have an update on how this cost shift would affect the city's budget?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're continuing to look at that with the agency and also with our, you know, administration colleagues that are looking at all the actions coming out of the federal government.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we don't have any confirmed, you know, information to act upon at this moment.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is there any possibility that implementation of this would be delayed?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Not that we know.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the Office of Healthcare Accountability created by the Speaker's legislation. Solomon reports it's fully funded at $2 million per year within DOHMH, fully staffed, published a report last year, and is working on the next iteration.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on Office of Healthcare Accountability budget

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so a couple questions on the healthcare accountability, which the speaker, as you know, had created an office for.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In 2023, through the passage of bipartisan legislation,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: that the Speaker championed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Council made it easier for New Yorkers to understand their health care costs with the creation of the nation's first municipal...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, sorry, I thought I was going crazy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Made it easier for New Yorkers to understand their health care costs with the creation of the nation's first municipal office of health care accountability.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: OHA would examine health care costs and hospital pricing practices, and the law would revolutionize health care in New York City by bringing transparency to prices and empowering New Yorkers with the information they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as someone who is on the provider side, I can definitely tell you that this is totally true in terms of the higher costs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what does the current budget and headcount for the Office of Healthcare Accountability?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So first, I mean, it is an important function of this office in terms of introducing transparency in terms of hospital pricing and other ways that the consumer can be more informed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The budget has been established within the health department, and it's fully funded at $2 million per year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's fully staffed at the moment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's a report that was published last year by the administration after significant sector collaboration and assessment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we're proud of their work in this area in terms of Department of Health and Mental Hygiene.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And they're working on the next iteration of the report.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As we speak.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and when is the next report due again?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We can come back to you on an exact timeline.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Lee raises CUNY's $6 million request for early childhood education training and certification programs. Solomon notes CUNY's role in workforce development, the $40 million for childcare provider rate increases, and ongoing evaluation of the request, while Lee also raises pay parity between CBO and school-based providers.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on childcare workforce development and CUNY training

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then childcare workforce development.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: With the expansion of 2K, 3K, there is a growing need for trained and certified early childhood educators.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: To support the growth, CUNY has requested that the administration allocate an additional $6 million for various early childhood education training and certification programs, including an apprenticeship program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what investments has or will the administration make in supporting the training and certification of early childhood educators in anticipation of the ECE expansion?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're examining further.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, I think CUNY as an engine of upward mobility also plays an important role in terms of workforce development.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know this is a big priority for the Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: My time there, certainly, I've heard him talk about this a lot.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so CUNY can really be a catalyst with...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This kind of work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we're evaluating their requests.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We look forward to having those discussions with you in the next few weeks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I would also note just in general, I know this is not the same issue, but it's related in the sense that we were happy to put up $40 million in the executive budget to increase child care provider rates.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know that between that, between the other investments that we've made to stabilize and expand

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 3K, and then the launch of 2K in the four districts so far, we know that this is an important piece of the puzzle that we look forward to working on.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because you need a workforce to be able to accomplish the goals that this administration set forward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that, again, to my earlier question, I think the process takes a while in terms of the background checks and all of those things.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I think that'll be in helping us to streamline and get more folks in the pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So whatever you all can look at to speed that up, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that we were also talking yesterday at the DOE hearing about pay parity across the board with our early childhood programs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so if you could work with DOE to make sure that we look at that holistically.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Whether you're a CBO provider versus a school-based provider, I think there's differences in the contract amounts there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So that would also be great if you could look at those numbers as well with Dewey.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $70 million for city grocery stores, whether they'll be contractor-run, and workforce plans. Solomon says EDC is identifying sites including La Marqueta and Hunts Point, optimal program design is under consideration, and more information will be shared as progress is made.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on city-supported grocery stores development

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then lastly on the citywide grocery stores, FY27 Executive Capital Commitment Plan includes $70 million for the development of city-supported grocery stores.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As we've heard, two of the five proposed sites have been identified already, one at La Marqueta in East Harlem and the other at Hunts Point in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If the city plans to open up city grocery stores, is it going to be run by contractors or what are some of the other options that you're looking at?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In terms of the sites and spaces, we are looking at, you know, the EDC is looking at

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: spaces that make sense to house these sites across the city.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We obviously will have specifics at some point about any lease negotiations and specifics in terms of where they will exist.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I would say that optimal program design is still under consideration, but we are making sure that

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're looking at the parameters of store operations that EDC is looking at and to make sure that it also comports with what the vision is that was initially set forth.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're making sure that we're committed to economic justice addressing the affordability.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Obviously, worker dignity and neighborhood visibility.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we hope to make more progress and share more information soon.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That'd be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if you could just let us know as things progress, you know, who's going to be selected, the type of workforce that you're going to be engaging in, all of those things would be great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Certainly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, just another PSA.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're moving the next two hearings into the committee room next door at 3.30 for those in chambers and listening via live stream.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just wanted to let you guys all know that at some point we are going to have to get kicked out of this room and we'll move to the committee room to prepare for an event here tonight.

chapters.summary: Williams asks how OMB determined library capital funding levels and how much remains unfunded. Solomon cites $50 million spread across the three systems with discussions ahead for additional investment. Williams also raises PICA as an unfunded mandate for libraries; Solomon says they're looking at parity funding numbers.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions OMB on library capital funding and PICA costs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, I'm going to move on to Deputy Speaker Williams.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just going back to libraries, given the substantial backlog of capital needs across three library systems, including aging facilities, HVAC, and roof repairs, ADA upgrades, and other state of good repair,

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: projects, how did OMB determine the level of capital funding included in this year's exec plan and how much capital needs remain unfunded?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so I think, Deputy Speaker, this was an, you know, we had to look at this in terms of available capital funding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That we had the submissions from the libraries in terms of what they were looking

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think what we tried to do here was to say $50 million that's spread over one project for each of the library systems, understanding that moving forward in the next few weeks, we'll have a discussion about

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: potentially additional investment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Based on the overall funding envelope that we have.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, and another issue that the library systems raised was the PICA program.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So we know it was negotiated as a mandatory city health benefit.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Obviously, it's an unfunded mandate, and so the library systems have to pick up the cost.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So we just wanted to understand why these costs went unfunded.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And why do the independent library systems have to absorb the cost?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're looking at the numbers now with our team, and we'll be able to communicate soon about the parity funding that comes out of the health funding piece.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Obviously, we all want individuals to have access to all the things that PICA comes with, but it does feel like an unfunded mandate for them to have to absorb that cost.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thankful for the baseline funding, but this, of course, creates additional needs for the library systems.

chapters.summary: Williams asks whether a framework comparable to climate budgeting exists for racial equity. Solomon says they're developing one for the next budget cycle, but in the meantime are already investing in disadvantaged communities through Community Parks Initiative, NYC Her Future, 2K siting, and the housing plan.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions OMB on racial equity framework and climate budgeting parallel

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, so more questions on my favorite topic, the racial equity.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, so OMB has developed a fairly robust climate budgeting framework that evaluates capital investments through the lens of emissions reduction, flood resiliency, and heat resiliency.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Is there currently a comparable framework within OMB for evaluating racial equity or environmental justice impacts when budget decisions are made?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I know you're working on it, but wanting to understand, again, if anything exists currently.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I think that we, so we are, yes, we are developing the plan that we have in our preliminary plan just to get there for the next budget cycle.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But the sort of the absence of a particular specific rubric or framework doesn't mean that we aren't being attentive to investing

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: with respect to new needs in disadvantaged communities in areas where we know have been targeted for underinvestment over the years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And there are

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: areas in this budget where that sort of bears out.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There was an announcement earlier this year about Community Parks Initiative, for example.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know that we have funded in this budget the New York City Her Future program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's just another example.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know, for example, in the 2K siting, and really looking at where those districts will be.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We look at the housing plan and really how we can make sure

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that the block-by-block comprehensive plan, the 200,000 new affordable homes, the 200,000 preservation, where is that investment happening, and how can we integrate an equity lens into that plan?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So all of that means that we are focused on it, but we're going to have something that is structural and formal to say, let's go through this, perhaps in the way that climate budgeting, as you noted, actually does.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'll look to be informed by existing models to be able to improve what we have.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about accountability for agencies failing to incorporate equity considerations. Solomon describes a two-way street where OMB's framework and agencies' submission process must align, with the racial equity plan charging all agencies to think this way from the inception of new needs.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions OMB on racial equity accountability mechanisms

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yeah, and on that note, it's my understanding that OMB works really closely with the Mayor's Office of Climate and Environmental Justice to integrate the climate considerations into the annual budget process.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Again, already existing, you have this formal structure.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So wanting to understand if that coordination process, a similar coordination process, would exist with the Mayor's Office of Equity and Racial Justice.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, I mean, I think it's in the same way that the climate budgeting is inextricably linked to the administration's environmental justice work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think it's sort of similar, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We want to make sure that our budget is representative of the decisions and the choices and the investments that we make.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To tackle a systemic issue, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we're making sure that we're looking at in the same way that we would look in environmental justice or investment in fossil fuel.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: reduction or decarbonization or the various tools that exist there, what are the tools that we can employ here vis-a-vis investment for where we know there's been a paucity of resources that have been devoted to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: either services or amenities or public institutions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that is what our focus will be.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, so the plan establishes a goal of having agencies utilize equity budgeting tools and strategies during budget submissions.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Will there be any accountability mechanisms for agencies that fail to meaningfully incorporate equity considerations into their requests?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, it's something that we are attentive to, attuned to, and making sure that it's a two-way street, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, that's the goal here is that OMB clearly is going to do its part in the framework, but we want to make sure, and again, the racial equity plan, it really charges all the agencies with thinking in this way as well,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: which is to say if a new need is going to be submitted at its inception.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How does it comport with the racial equity plan?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How does it advance those goals?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It will make the process much more aligned and compatible if we know the front end is also matching up with the back end.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're committed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know agencies are committed as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we will have a good product when we're working together in a two-way street.

chapters.summary: Williams asks whether OMB has identified disparities in its own workforce and what corrective actions are considered. Solomon discusses professional development, the OMB Institute for analyst advancement, and commitment to diverse leadership, while Williams asks if the agency's leadership reflects the city's diversity.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions OMB on internal workforce diversity and pay equity

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, just a few more questions because one of the great things

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: about the racial equity plans here.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: You have like a dual role because you have this role because you manage the budget to sort of hold agencies accountable, but you also are an agency.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And so just some questions specifically about your agency and how you are going to advance racial equity just in the functions of your agency.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So the agency's long-term racial equity goals, including advancing pay equity and incorporating the city's true cost of

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: living measure into pay parity analysis, has OMB identified any existing disparities within your agency's workforce and what corrective actions are being considered?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are looking at ways to achieve, I mean, the pay equity piece is also a citywide effort.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With our agency ourselves, we're looking internally to be able to say, how could we offer more opportunities, either promotional opportunities or hiring opportunities, to make sure that we have

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: an effective and diverse cross-section of employees across our agency.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We spend a lot of time in professional development as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know my first deputy director has been big on that long before I even got to OMB.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Having an OMB Institute to basically make sure that there's a pathway, an upward pathway for OMB analysts to be able to develop in the agency and get promoted.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're committed to that work and making sure that the agency makeup is representative of the diversity we'd like to see that exists, frankly.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So do you, to that point, do you believe that your agency, its leadership structure reflects the diversity of the city it serves currently?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Do you believe that your agency reflects that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think we have a diverse leadership in the agency.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that we look for the best and the brightest, but we also look to have, again, a leadership structure that is representative of the diversity that we see in our daily lives.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I'm committed to that, and the opportunities that may exist will foster and make sure that we continue to build a solid OMB team that is also diverse.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Majority whip hangs, followed by Councilmember Brooks-Powers.

chapters.summary: Hanks asks whether studies have been conducted on how community grocery stores will impact bodegas and local stores. Solomon says EDC is attuned to the issue, the program relates to affordability, and it doesn't have to come at the expense of small businesses. Hanks also asks about engaging council members in siting.
chapters.title: Majority Whip Hanks questions OMB on community grocery store impact on small businesses

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: To kind of piggyback off of Chair Lee's questionings regarding the community grocery stores, I don't know if this question's been asked, has the administration conducted any studies on how the community grocery stores will impact small businesses,

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: particularly like bodegas or local grocery stores?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are attuned to those issues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know in speaking with EDC, they are also attuned to that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We also know that through our small business services, we are investing in as much as we can to help small businesses.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This is this program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is something that directly relates to the affordability agenda.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the time now is even more salient when we see costs going up and the fact that the SNAP eligibility looms large.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And there are so many factors that are playing into the need for grocery stores in this proposal.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it doesn't have to come at the expense of small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So does the administration have a plan on how they'll engage council members whose these grocery stores will reside?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're happy to take that back to our intergovernmental teams and our leadership.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Please do.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Okay, one more question.

chapters.summary: Hanks raises that the $4 million DOE Immigrant Family Communication and Outreach Initiative is not included in FY27 or out years. Solomon cites the overall funding envelope and foundation aid supporting ELL programs, but acknowledges language access is critical and discussions will continue at adoption.
chapters.title: Majority Whip Hanks questions OMB on DOE immigrant family communication funding

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So over 40% of New York City public school families speak a language other than English at home.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: The DOE's Immigrant Family Communication and Outreach Initiative is a $4 million program that helps immigrant families get important information about their child's education through strategies such as sending paper notices, calling, texting families, and collaborating with immigrant

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: facing CBOs.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Does the executive plan, pardon me, does not...

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Include this funding in the fiscal 2027 or in the out years.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Can you speak to how this funding will be used this school year?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We will definitely be discussing that over the next few weeks with you and the council and also with the Department of Education to see how we can support these services.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: And what would the programmatic results be if this $4 million in funding is not restored in FY27?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, it is an important service.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have to see how we can talk about it in the next few weeks in terms of the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Having language access is critically important, and so we'll work with our team on New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To see how that goes forward.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But Liz, I don't know if you have anything to add on that piece.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, I can just say we'll continue to look towards this for adoption, and we're happy to continue conversations with you.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Okay, so one more.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So if the DOE, they supported this program, have you given...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: any indication why this funding wasn't included in the executive plan?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think it's just a question of the overall funding envelope.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We made a lot of investments in DOE.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We also know that there was foundation aid that was supported by the state that also funded ELL programs, et cetera, in terms of the weights.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So from our perspective, this is the fact that

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We need to have a public school system that can communicate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's important.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we have to talk about that in the next few weeks on how the funding envelope can support that.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Brooks-Powers, followed by Jay Sanchez.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about incorporating the Independent Rikers Commission's recommendations, funding 380 remaining JISH units, and increasing ATI funding. Solomon details 120 current JISH units with a goal of 350+, an active RFP, and says DOC technology including visitation software has been self-funded by the department.
chapters.title: CM Brooks-Powers questions OMB on Rikers closure, JISH, and ATI funding

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm going to again ask a couple questions back to back just in the interest of time.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But this is focused on the space of criminal justice.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Regarding the Rikers Island closure, the Independent Rikers Commission has issued its blueprint to close Rikers Island, which lays out numerous recommendations for investments that would assist in lowering the incarcerated population.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: These programmatic recommendations are a critical component of the borough-based jails plan, but despite the council's advocacy, didn't receive adequate attention in the prior administration.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How have these recommendations been incorporated into this plan?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: One example of a

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: of a program that hasn't been sufficiently funded is Justice Involved Supportive Housing, also known as JISH.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In 2019, the city agreed to add 380 more units, bringing the total to 500.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Currently, there are only 250 units online.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Half of which are supplemented with funding from the city council to stabilize the reimbursement rates.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: When does the administration plan to fund and bring the remaining 380 promised units online at the higher reimbursement rate?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Also, alternatives to incarceration operate at

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: a fraction of the per person cost of Rikers and have been shown to be effective.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But the executive budget does not increase the funding to ATIs, can you explain?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And then lastly, in terms of Department of Corrections technology, at the hearing on

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The Department of Corrections executive budget, Commissioner Richards testified that it is a priority of the Department of Corrections to modernize the agency.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Is there money in the department's budget to fund new technology?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Would this technology be capital?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Capitally eligible and one of the council's priorities is the development and implementation of an online scheduling system to facilitate visits to incarcerated

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: individuals per local law 43 of 2025.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: OMB originally estimated that developing this software would cost $2 million.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Have those funds been included in the department's capital budget as this technology would be transferable to

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: borough-based jails and therefore meet the five-year lifespan requirement.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I can repeat any question you need me to.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sure.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers presses for when the administration will fund the remaining 380 JISH units at higher reimbursement rates. DiStefano clarifies that $4.8 million in baseline funding exists and units are filled as applicants come in through a rolling RFP. Brooks-Powers also asks about ATI funding increases, with Solomon deferring to adoption discussions.
chapters.title: CM Brooks-Powers presses OMB on JISH funding timeline and RFP status

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So let me start first writ large about Rikers in terms of

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Riker's closure and the borough-based jails projects and we're committed to those and we know there are various locations that are in progress in terms of the different sites.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: At the same time, we made sure that we made investments in Riker's now in terms of maintenance.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we invested in cell doors, for example, to make sure that they're workable and other maintenance that is required at the facilities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With respect to justice-involved supportive housing,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you noted, we have currently 120 units, and we hope to bring the city's total number to more than 350.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know that the health department has updated an RFP for the new units, and we urge providers to be able to apply.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so our goal is to basically

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: increase the number of units that are there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And again, we encourage providers to apply.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With respect to DOC technology...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm sorry, Commissioner, before you move on, the question was when does the administration, because I know the administration has been

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: verbally supportive, but when does the administration plan to fund and bring the remaining 380 promised units online for the reimbursement rate, at the higher reimbursement rate?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sure, we're going to stay close with the health department because that RFP is out there, and so we need to understand what the responses are and be able then to plot a plan forward based on those responses to say when will these contracts be in place and when we can move forward with funding.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So will the funding be included in this fiscal year budget?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: At all?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It depends on what the outcome is with the health department and the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: When is the RFP done to you?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The RFP is...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Patrick, do you know what it is?

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: The RFP was put out already last year.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: When does it do that?

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: $4.8 million was funding.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: I believe it's rolling, so you can apply as...

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: As the applications come in, you fill all the units and the dollars available.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So if it's rolling, it's into perpetuity, so there's no timeline in terms of the funding, and we're in the fiscal year right now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, it's been, my understanding is in the past, there's been RFP on the street, but there haven't been responses.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we need to basically find out how we can invite people.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And please, with your assistance and partnership, please get out the word using, of course,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: your committee to basically say, look, we need providers to be able to respond to this RFP, and then we can get there faster.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I don't want to compare you to the last administration because, you know, this is a new administration.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So, again, if you're saying that the funding is going to be tied to the RFP, but the RFP does not have a deadline,

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: There's no commitment for the funding.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm trying to get an understanding as to when the administration plans to fund and bring the remaining 380 promise units online at a higher reimbursement rate.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So the funding was added in the baseline.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So the funding exists.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: As the applicants come in and are awarded,

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: They will be awarded such till we reach the funding limit.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So the money is there, it's just a matter of when the RFP...

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: When applicants come in.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: When they come in, then you'll release the funding.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: Yes, when they sign a contract.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you for that clarification.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Sorry, Director.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Technology.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No worries.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the DOC technology, we're assessing the funding availability and the necessary upgrades.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've been in conversations with DOC.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that during the executive plan, the department is able to self-fund a new project ID for visitation software for the request of $2.1 million.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're glad that that's going forward.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we've also approved certificates to proceed for various technology projects, like the offender and jail management system to manage PICs from intake through discharge as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then we also also have a new system for the

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: have a workday software platform which replaces the old agency legacy system for HR and payroll system.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're working with DOC on their technology needs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've made some improvements.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: More to do.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And then the ATI funding, do you anticipate more money by the final budget?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're happy to talk to you about that in the next few weeks as we look forward to the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so we have Councilmember Wong followed by Jay Sanchez.

chapters.summary: Wong challenges that the $7.07 billion FY28 gap represents kicking the can down the road, asking what recurring spending was permanently aligned with recurring revenue. Solomon cites $17 billion in expenses from chronic under-budgeting, recurring state aid, and savings plans, noting the FY28 gap is in line with historical averages.
chapters.title: CM Wong questions OMB on out-year gaps and recurring vs one-time savings

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have a question about the executive budget over here.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Your own budget package says FY27 is balanced.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But the out-year gap jumps to $7.07 billion.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: In the year after that, $9.07 billion.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What recurring spending did OMB permanently align with recurring revenue instead of just pushing the problem into FY28?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because as I see it, this is kicking the can down the road.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you answer that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, I think, you know, it's the number one, I think, when we look at over prelim and exec and what we've added, $17 billion in expenses because of chronic underbudgeting, and you see how that propagates out through the system.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then when you look at sort of the revenue forecast, as my colleague talked about, which is more of a conservative estimate, you know, in the out years, there are a lot of recurring savings in here and recurring state aid from the road.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: reversal of cuts and cost shifts that actually go into the out years and help us.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there's a lot that's been baselined.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But you see in the fiscal 28 gap, that gap is more in line with historical numbers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know that there is continued work to do on savings in the out years that we'll do through the chief savings officers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And revenue generating strategies that we need to continue to do so we can fund the affordability agenda.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Let me squeeze in one more question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Water revenue versus rental payments.

chapters.summary: Wong questions why ratepayers are charged $4.7 billion over two years while $300+ million annually is diverted from the water system for general budget use. Solomon says the rental payment was already in the revenue forecast and removing it would create a $300 million hole, with no impact on system maintenance.
chapters.title: CM Wong questions OMB on water rental payments diverted to general fund

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Your financial plan projects total water and sewer charges of $2.318 billion in FY26 and $2.39 billion in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Separate from that, the city is also extracting a rental payment of about $303 million in FY26

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: and $312 million in FY27 from the water system for general budget use.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How do you justify asking ratepayers for more than $4.7 billion in charges over two years while still diverting hundreds of millions of dollars away from the system?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Our world-class water system needs to be invested in.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's why we have the Water Authority, obviously, and rates go to fund the improvements in maintenance that...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: make sure the system reaches its intended result, which is water access and delivery that we critically depend on.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that with respect to the rental payment, the rental payment was already included in the revenue forecast.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If we were to back out the rental payment, that would be a $300 million hole in the budget that we'd have to make up elsewhere.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yeah, but over here is for general budget use.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It doesn't say that money's been taken out for maintenance and repair of the system.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's why it's concerning to me.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The rental payment has existed and is part of the revenue forecast that has no impact on what the overall maintenance of the system will be.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And it was already put into the forecast.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And if to take it out would mean that we would have to make up that funding elsewhere.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you have anything to add?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: That's all right.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I'll follow up with you on this.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Council Member Jay Sanchez, followed by Lewis.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Sanchez asks whether truck procurement aligns with planned garage capacity for containerization. Solomon explains DSNY is also reducing rear-loader trucks to offset space needs, funding is distributed by fiscal year based on schedules, and they're evaluating options to advance garage replacement funding.
chapters.title: CM Justin Sanchez questions OMB on sanitation garage capacity and containerization

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: First, I wanted to start off with sanitation garages.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So there is extensive infrastructure being built out for containerization, yet a lot of the garage infrastructure that is much needed to house our trucks, do all the things that we need to do to run our system,

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: are being pushed out into the out years.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Has

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Has there been any conversation on whether the pace of the truck procurement will be aligned and available to deal with planned garage capacity?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've had conversations with the sanitation department about this to make sure that their replacement projects are, we understood the timing that they would come online for those projects to be able to move forward.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've distributed by fiscal year based on the schedules and the cash flow of what we anticipate with the agency.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are basically not just expanding the fleet of the side loader trucks as related to containerization.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're also reducing the fleet on our rear loader trucks that offset the need for the additional space.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're evaluating our options where space obviously presents a challenge, but we're seeing, working with the department, understanding the timing and the cash flow of when those projects need to happen.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Now, funding for the garage replacement itself, I think that we need to understand how within the capital project envelope, and this has been mentioned in other areas before, where there's capital funding that may exist

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: in outer years that could be advanced as a possibility or in other areas where we can repurpose.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Some of the conditions that we see in the garages clearly need capital investment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's no doubt about that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we're committed to working with the agency and with you as we go forward in the next few weeks to see what.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What's in the funding envelope that could be provided?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And while, you know, containerization is arguably a really great thing for the city, I want to make sure we're not putting the cart before the horse

chapters.summary: Sanchez raises maintaining 21 sanitation workers for Third Avenue Hub given new charter schools and the opioid crisis, and asks about the Hunts Point Produce Market contract deadline.
