Solomon offers to work with DSNY on Hub staffing. DiStefano reports $130 million in city capital and $505 million in grants for the produce market.
chapters.title: CM Justin Sanchez questions OMB on 21 sanitation workers for the Hub and Hunts Point Produce Market

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: and making sure that we have the actual tools to make sure that once we're fully in containerization, that New Yorkers are then not just bombarded with sanitation trucks all over their streets because there's no place to put them.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Speaking of sanitation workers, the previous administration had put in a one-time funding for 21 sanitation workers in the heart of the hub, Third Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: It is the second most visited place in the city of New York after Times Square on a daily basis.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: The current administration just approved a third charter school within three blocks, adding over 1,000 new students.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And so really understanding where the conversation is with maintaining these 21 sanitation workers to ensure that as we're continuing to build out the hub, that we actually have the ability to

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: clean the hub, given that in addition to all of that, it is the center of the opioid crisis in the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so I appreciate that, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that we'll work with the department to figure out what the overall resources are that are devoted.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We understand that that was a particular area of focus that the department had in the past.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'll understand fourth levels and understand how we could work towards that goal.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: That's super important.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Do we have time for one more question?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Quick, quick.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And finally, the Huntsville Produce Market.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This administration has made a concerted effort to really talk about food insecurity and food access with the expansion of the community grocery stores.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: However, the produce market, which feeds much of the eastern seaboard, is in the middle of a contract negotiation in which if they don't get it signed with the city by June, we lose our produce market.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So has there been any conversation with adding more additional funds to the produce market and being able to make sure that we don't lose a vital critical resource for our city?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm happy to look into that further and talk to EDC about that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, okay.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: There's $130 million in city capital in the Huntsville produce market, and we're expecting

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: $505 million in other grant resources, including $230 million from USDOT.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Lewis followed by Brewer.

chapters.summary: Lewis asks about the Office of Deed Theft Prevention's interaction with HPD and existing programs, and about capital commitment rate falling from 78.3% to 67.7%. Solomon details DOF's holistic approach to deed theft and outlines capital process reforms including streamlined CPs, fewer questions, and blanket eligibility.
chapters.title: CM Lewis questions OMB on deed theft prevention and capital delivery reforms

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm back again.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'll make it quick.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The mayor has taken major action on the deep theft crisis by establishing the Office of Deep Theft Prevention to protect vulnerable homeowners in central Brooklyn and southeast Queens.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Part of my question is for DOF, but I wanted to know from an OMB perspective, how does this office interact with

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: HPD's new mortgage assistance program, if at all, and how does the office differ from the enhanced existing services provided through citywide council initiatives, such as the estate planning

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: and resolution initiative and foreclosure prevention programs?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And is the administration committed to working with the council to scale up and design the Office of Deep Threat Prevention?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: My last question, the city has historically struggled to fully execute its capital program with the capital commitment rate falling from 78.3%

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: in FY24 to 67.7% in FY25.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: As OMB continues to expand the size of the capital plan, what reforms are being implemented to ensure that agencies can actually deliver projects on schedule?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And has OMB considered increasing appropriations without improving?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Without improving the

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: delivery capacity and will continue that will continue to inflate projects costs and delay community benefits that's all thank you so

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: you're reaching back into my prior lives on these two questions it's a good thing yeah so d theft when I was commissioner was a big focus as well at the Department of Finance it was

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: really a holistic view and I have no doubt that you'll hear from Commissioner Lee when he testifies that it's a holistic approach.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Of course, we'll work with other agencies, HPD, if they have information that can be brought to light that helps us combat d-theft.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know that working with the Sheriff's Office under new leadership as well, we can look at that, the Land Records Division within the Department of Finance.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can we see whether there are

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is there technology that could be used to be able to see the land records and patterns that are being, when a deed sort of transfers over?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There was, I think, a proof of concept to look at things like blockchain technology, to look at that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there are a number of things that could be brought in, including creative technological tools, but then also working with other agencies and clearly law enforcement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For those investigations and how they're supported.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it is a commitment, and I have no doubt that Commissioner Lee will talk about the broad strokes of how they're going to approach it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With respect to your second question, I mean, I think that this is something we're continually improving on, delivery of capital projects.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I would

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I oversaw a capital process reform task force that brought together leaders from the industry, MWBEs, all.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: stakeholders'labor in our capital construction universe and being able to say, how can we streamline certificates to proceed?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How can we have greater visibility into where the blockages or logjams are in those submissions?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's a new system that OMB has where we communicate regularly with agencies and say, hey, this CP is outstanding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You owe us some information on XYZ.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Let's figure out how to get that and move that along.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've shortened the number of questions and time period to get to those points.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We looked at things like blanket, you know, capital eligibility and so on and so forth.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there are a lot of things that are going on to improve, clearly more to do, but the word is out with the agencies that we could, you know, speed things along.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And OMB as well, it's our responsibility too to make sure that when a certificate to proceed is on my desk, we're moving it as fast as possible.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right, so we look forward to that.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for your response.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Banks, and then Murano.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about capital funding for courthouses in disrepair, particularly Bronx Family Court. Solomon says meetings with OCA and DCAS have occurred. Brewer also asks about $4.7 billion in decarbonization; Solomon confirms the number and details $7.5 billion for flooding, $1 billion for extreme heat, and a comprehensive climate action plan coming next spring.
chapters.title: CM Brewer questions OMB on courthouse renovations and decarbonization investments

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So a couple issues about buildings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We toured some of the courthouses, particularly the one in the Bronx, Family Court, and there's leaks everywhere.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, what funding, if at all, is allocated towards the courthouses in terms of renovations?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Maybe not all of them, but is there any thought towards the courthouses?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And capital funding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've met with the Office of Court Administration recently with our Corporation Council, and I know that we've had

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: visits, and I know that our friend, the DCAS Commissioner, Yume, has also been with us working there, and she's towards some of the facilities as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we need to have the facilities of our justice system in a state of good repair.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We will see within our capital envelope what we can produce to be able to invest in there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I can follow up with you about what's exactly in the capital plan for that, but just to let you know that we are having those conversations and we're attuned to those needs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, I think a little bit before, this is about decarbonization, how much is allocated?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think it's $4.7 billion in the capital budget.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that for schools and hospitals and public buildings specifically?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And later on, could you break it down?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think your number is correct.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, $4.7 billion for decarbonization and basically nearly $25 billion for projects with elements of supporting our net zero goals.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so you look at the five-year capital plan, for example, $7.5 billion to address flooding and projects totaling over $1 billion to address extreme heat.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Capital plan also includes $41 billion for resiliency elements.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are also...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Develop

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: a comprehensive climate action plan that will release in the spring of next year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there'll be more on that soon.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But we're also looking at reconstructing docks at the city's asphalt plants.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's $26.4 million in FY27.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We talked about the EV purchases before, heat pumps, clean energy at NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there is quite a bunch of investments here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's clearly more to do, but we do have some solid investments.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have more on that, but different topics.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks for largest examples of agency efficiencies and when the next assessments are due. Deputy Director Gustorf details the five savings categories and confirms ongoing tracking. Brewer also raises that school water fountains can't be fixed standalone without a larger project, which Solomon offers to discuss.
chapters.title: CM Brewer questions OMB on Chief Savings Officer examples and water fountains in schools

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So Executive Order 12 required the Chief Savings Officer, CSO, to focus on recurring savings and efficiencies, as you know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They will provide updated assessments every six months.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can you provide some of the largest examples of agency efficiencies?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: When are the next round of assessments due?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Should the council anticipate seeing them, either the adopted or November?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And do any of them include improving agency performances?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I hope so.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So during the break, Deputy Director Nathan Gustorf was very sad that no questions came his way.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And he was overseeing the chief saving officer's exercise.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I would love to turn it to him to be able to address that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Glad I'm not.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: Sitting up here for nothing.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So just to start out, because as you noted, Council Member, the CSO exercise is ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: We have five categories that we've used to frame CSO efforts that will continue to operate throughout the process.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: You've probably seen these numbers, but in the current $1.77 billion in savings across the two fiscal years, we have $24 million in savings

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: from agencies giving up unused space, consolidating leases, and other leasing savings.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: $28 million in savings from IT upgrades, renegotiating software licenses, and other related IT savings.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: I think of particular interest for everyone here, $94 million in savings from pursuing better contract rates and insourcing functions rather than relying on outside consultants.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: $368 million from increasing the efficiency of public services overall, including things like overtime management, phasing out unused or redundant programs and other programmatic improvements, and $947 million

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: from improved financial management, better estimates, and revenue improvements such as better grant claiming.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, and as far as you feel strongly that in terms of the, how are we going to hear about all of this?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We'll see the savings coming up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is this what it's going to be?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that what you're saying?

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So, yeah, we continue to track all of these savings to ensure that they will be realized.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: Some of that is simply a matter of making sure that our estimates are accurate.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: Some of them do require implementation, and OMB is overseeing that in partnership with the Office of the First Deputy Mayor.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: And then in terms of the ongoing nature of the exercise, the executive order does require six-month check-ins, but the reality is that we are in touch much more frequently than that in order to continue working on all of these savings prongs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And you think the agency performance will improve as a result?

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: Yes, it has been such a priority to avoid service reductions and find efficiencies that we should see better agency service go hand in hand with the savings because they are targeting efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Quick question on water fountains.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know this is minutia, but the water fountains in the schools, they often don't work, and you can't get them fixed because you can't fix a standalone.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can you change that?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No, you have to be part of some stupid big project, which isn't necessary, in order to fix a water fountain.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's stupid.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We look forward to talking to you about working with water fountains for sure.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then also...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, Council Member, I got to...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because we got to move on.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Time's constraints.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm so sorry.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But we're going to follow up with all the members'questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then if we can just make the questions and responses brief.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Banks raises that 4,000 NYCHA households face uncertain futures with EHV funding expiring in November, asking about the $124 million annual cost. Solomon says OMB reallocated federal HOME tenant-based rental assistance for a two-year transition period and will look at other voucher programs for any remaining gaps.
chapters.title: CM Banks questions OMB on emergency housing vouchers for NYCHA residents

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Council Member Brinks, followed by Morano.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I know the emergency housing vouchers has been something that's been of concern.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And obviously, due to the fact the impact it may have on close to 5,200 NYCHA residents and some of the most vulnerable residents of public housing.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: During the last week's hearing, we heard that NYCHA is conducting outreach to the 5,200 EHV holders.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And approximately, we believe, 1,200 had actually been signed up for the Section 9 wait list.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: This leaves approximately 4,000 households with an uncertain future with the EHV funding, which is set to expire in November.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Is OMB aware of any state funding or alternative housing voucher programs that would help cover the $124 million,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: which is the annual cost for these households?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And is the city prepared to cover any shortfall that might arise to ensure that these residents are able to retain their housing?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We knew that this was an important issue that we had to confront in the face of the federal government cuts here in this area.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what we will do is we will

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: been able to do is work with our agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've looked at reallocating alternative federal funding, notably the home tenant-based rental assistance program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we were able to do that so that we can use basically

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: this rental assistance program to assist the households that are affected over a two-year transition period.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, you know, I think the long-term outlook we have to really focus on still as well because the problem will come right back.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We need to make sure that we're addressing it head on, but we went to work to find that alternative, as you noted, that will work for the short term.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And are you looking at any vouchers?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Are these residents eligible for FEPS?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Or I know NYCHA has been known to use the housing voucher for a lot of residents who are on Project Bay Section 8.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: If they choose to leave or opt out of Project Bay Section 8, they've given NYCHA residents the housing choice voucher.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Is that an option as well?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I think we take a holistic approach if we can assist households in any way.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If you don't match up with one particular voucher and we need assistance, then our goal is to try to help as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So you will cover the shortfall.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'll look at it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're covering the shortfall in terms of what we're doing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Talking about for that specific, the EHV loss, right, the emergency housing voucher loss through the tenant-based assistance program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But if there are any other gaps to fill that wouldn't be covered by what we've talked about, we'll look at other voucher programs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We just don't know if there's a match.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Just want to make sure these 5,200 or the remaining 14,000, 4,000 households are covered and protected.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'll make sure we work with NYCHA to make sure that we can do what we can.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And one last question.

chapters.summary: Banks asks what impact PACT conversions have had on the $78 billion capital needs assessment, noting NYCHA testified that conversions don't impact capital needs. Solomon and DiStefano explain the 2026 PNA shows PACT reduced needs by $3.2 billion, but new needs and inflation offset the decrease, keeping the number stagnant.
chapters.title: CM Banks questions OMB on NYCHA capital needs impact of PACT conversions

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: 20 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Yes, one last question.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: NYCHA's capital needs has long been estimated at $80 billion, and I know it was lowered.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: You had mentioned that it was at $78 billion.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Given that significant number of developments have been already converted over to the RADPAC program, I believe

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: close to somewhere between 27,000 and 30,000 units have been converted, and there's about 62,000 units that are in queue to be converted with this new RFP or expression RFP that's been released by NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What impact is that having on the capital needs assessment or the $78 billion of capital needs that exists?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Has there been an impact?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And is conversion really–

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Is it decreasing the capital needs of NITRA?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This goes back to the physical needs assessment.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There was a 2026 update, and it shows, as we talked about before, a slight decrease from what we saw last time of 0.8%.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It showed that PAC conversion reduced it by $3.2 billion, and then other completed projects was a reduction of about $500 million.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But then there were other new needs and inflation offsets, which kind of kept the number pretty stagnant.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And the reason why I want clarity on this is because NYCHA gave testimony to my committee.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: That when a development is converted over, it doesn't impact the capital needs.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So, but you're giving a different, you know, it's contradicting what they're giving testimony to.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: This is outlined.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What they've given testimony to.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The technical report for the physical needs assessment is outlined on NYCHA's website, so you can see the complete breakdown in detail in terms of how they arrive at that number and how it changes over time.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Well, I look forward to hopefully getting some clarity, and hopefully the administration and NYCHA can be on the same page when it comes to what the actual impact is.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Rad Pack is having on the capital needs.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Murano versus, sorry, not versus, sorry, I'm thinking tennis and sports.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Murano versus wrestlers, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: My money's on Lincoln in that matchup.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Murano and then wrestlers.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Morano, chair of the Veterans Committee, asks how OMB determined savings for DVS's $6.6 million budget. Solomon explains the only savings was $60,000 from a planned parade cancellation, DVS was exempt from vacancy alignment, and no veteran services were cut. He guarantees no reduced services or longer wait times.
chapters.title: CM Morano questions OMB on veterans services savings and impact

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Director Solomon.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I have the honor.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: of serving as the chair of the Veterans Committee.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: The Department of Veterans Services represents only a tiny fraction of the city's overall budget at $6.6 million.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How did OMB determine where savings could be achieved without affecting services to veterans?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What specific metrics or performance indicators were used to make that determination?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the first thing is that we really looked at, and you just heard this from Deputy Director Gustav, which is that

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: we made sure we insisted to agencies, don't submit anything that's going to be a service cut.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And if they did submit something that was a service cut, we would...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We would explore deeper as to how the agency felt it would be not a service cut burn efficiency or program effectiveness or whatever other category it fit into.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In this particular case, there was $60,000 that was the only proposal in the savings book

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that dealt with a planned parade that was supposed to take place.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that was the sole savings initiative that was accepted.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There is, I believe there was one time funded that was added last year at ADOPT.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's a separate issue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That wasn't an issue related to the chief savings officer.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: or exercise.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So based on that, we had confirmed that it was an event.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It was not anything related to cuts to veteran services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And in fact, we look forward to discussing with you moving forward, not only in ADOP, but overall, long term, how do we enhance services to our veterans?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's the least we can do, and we look forward to the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: As do I.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So that $60,000 you alluded to, the executive plan includes $60,000 in baseline savings for DVS.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So did DVS meet the same savings target imposed on other agencies, or was there a different standard applied because of the circumstances that you just alluded to with the parade?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, for example, so that was the sole piece.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There was another, there was, in terms of the vacancy alignment exercise, they really didn't have vacancies that, or funded vacancies to be able to take.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So they were exempt from that particular exercise.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They had maybe two, was that, was anyone?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It was a very small amount where it wouldn't have made sense for us to apply the vacancy alignment exercise.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that was something where they were exempt.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So really, we felt as though there wasn't an action that was taken as part of the savings exercise that would have any real impact on the department.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So just to reiterate and be clear, can you guarantee

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: that no veteran seeking assistance with benefits, housing, mental health services, employment assistance, or discharge upgrades will experience longer wait times or reduced service as a result of these savings.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The Chief Savings Officer exercise does not impact any of that.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thanks very much.

chapters.summary: Restler raises that NYCHA vacant units grew from 486 to over 6,000 under the prior administration, plus 1,157 vacant supportive housing units, totaling 7,200+ vacant units. He asks what investment would make the greatest difference. Solomon cites the $374 million investment as historic but acknowledges more to do, and agrees to continue discussions.
chapters.title: CM Restler questions OMB on vacant NYCHA and supportive housing units

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee, and I'm very happy to not be competing with Councilmember Murano.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I wanted to focus on the Progressive Caucus's campaign for rentals within reach.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: As you know well, Director, we saw a disgraceful 12-fold increase in vacant NYCHA units under Eric Adams'administration, from just 486 when he came into office at the end of the de Blasio administration to well over 6,000

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: when Mayor Mamdani came in four years later.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Similarly, we've seen the number of vacant supportive housing units that are in the city's control, DOHMH and HRA units, increase dramatically over these recent years.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There are 1,157 vacant HRA and DOHMH-funded supportive housing units today, as of a report from a month ago.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So combined, we're looking at close to 7,200-plus vacant units that the city funds, that are in our control, that are for the poorest New Yorkers who need housing the most.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We want the city council to champion putting more resources.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: in to activate these units, what do you think is the best way for us to fill, to renovate and get activated these NYCHA units and these supportive housing units?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is it if we give additional funding to NYCHA to hire additional staff to fix up the units, is that the best approach?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: De Blasio had a shelter repair squad a decade ago that fixed up shelters that were in substandard conditions.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Should we be bringing resources together from other agencies to fix up NYCHA apartments?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: How can we break through and not ensure that it's just the same number of vacant units a year from now?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So thank you for your leadership on this issue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, I know that you also have focused on this for many, many years as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know it's a priority of the caucus, and we know that from our perspective, what we added in the executive budget, we think it makes some meaningful progress.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We know there's more to do.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We want to be as ambitious as we can be.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The block-by-block housing plan outlines how ambitious we want to be in there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: A vacant unit doesn't serve anyone.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We need to be able to, and we invested in ways that we can bring more apartments on and repair and maintenance in terms of mold and leaks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have the OCC, you know, call center as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But the vacant unit piece, again, we talked about the numbers a little bit earlier.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know that, again, it will make meaningful progress.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And when you look at sort of the historic and recent history, it's probably the most combination between capital and expense.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know that you're talking about more like strike forces or squads that can come in and do it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What's the kind of blitz that we can do that can make a difference this year?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Because when we hear from NYCHA, when we've talked to them, they've said with the additional funding that the city's putting in, which we're grateful for, all we're going to do is stop the bleeding, and it's going to be the same number of vacant units a year from now.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It won't improve because it's getting worse every year.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And that just, being at status quo, having 6,000 vacant NYCHA apartments a year from now isn't a win.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we want to hear from OMB, what's the investment that we can make that will make a difference?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If the council is willing to lead on this, and I can't speak for the speaker, but if that were to happen.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What's the investment in vacant NYCHA apartments and what's the investment in vacant supportive housing that you believe and your team believes would make the greatest difference so that we could house potentially 20,000 New Yorkers who are desperately in need of affordable housing this year if we renovated these units?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I think it's a conversation we'll have over the next few weeks, and we can tie sort of the investment into working with NYCHA to understand for every dollar spent, what is it by us in terms of units brought online?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have not had the easiest time scheduling some of these follow-up meetings, so we would love to, it's good to hear that you want to continue those conversations.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Hopefully that will make it a little easier to get things on the books.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I was going to make a joke, but I won't.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Pierian Sanchez and then Mercedes.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks how OMB worked with DSS to develop the $543.5 million FY27 cost containment plan and what indicators were used. Solomon details centralized support, rent reasonableness comparisons, reducing broker fees, and using arrears as alternatives, all without cutting vouchers. DiStefano adds investments in right to counsel and self-sufficiency programs.
chapters.title: CM Pierina Sanchez questions OMB on CityFEPs cost containment plan development

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Hello, we're still here.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So continuing my questions around the FEPS program, at the General Welfare Executive Budget Hearing last week, DSS gave us some

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: insight into the cost containment savings in the executive plan for DHS and HRA.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The plan includes savings of $543.5 million in fiscal 27, $280 million in 28, and $336 million in fiscal year 29.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And $381 million in fiscal 2030 across.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: HRA and DHS from city, state, and federal sources.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: These savings will be generated through a cost containment plan for the FEPS program and DHS shelters, where DSS aims to more effectively maximize the use of non-city funded housing subsidy programs, ensure long-term shelter residents

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: obtain subsidized exits, and improve shelter diversion and intake.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Can you walk us through how OMB worked with DSS to develop the cost containment plan?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: What specific indicators did OMB and DSS use to estimate the projected savings starting in fiscal 27?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And if I have time, I'll ask one.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Let me start, and then I'll turn it over to Patrick DiStefano to continue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we worked on, and I alluded to some of this in my testimony, but we looked at certain things in terms of centralizing central support, in terms of operational support, and finding other efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it was a few things that we talked about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Rent reasonableness, for example.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We talked about comparing legal rents against the division of homes and community renewal legal rent.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: data list.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We talked about reducing broker's fees.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We talked about alternative ways that we can potentially use arrears, for example, rent arrears through one-shot funding to be able to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: find a different pathway for families that may be facing eviction.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we talked about those and all of these things.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What was the North Star here was without cutting vouchers, without cutting vouchers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: At the same time, we looked at ways that

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And this is a one-time savings in terms of fiscal 27, which is can we, and this goes to your earlier question, council member, where we can look at ways to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: vacate some of the hotels and into shelter capacity where it exists, and that achieves savings in FY27.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Patrick, I don't know if you want to add anything to that.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: All the things the director said fit into this very holistic view of what we're doing here.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: I mean, even before that, you know, before you get to the front door, we've invested more in right to counsel, $20 million or $40 million more for that.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So we're trying to prevent evictions.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: We want to get every step of the process, people from not entering shelter to when they get into shelter, if they get a voucher, they have education and training opportunities to be more self-sufficient.

utterances.speaker: Patrick DiStefano
utterances.text: So holistically, we looked at every aspect of it, and that's how we arrived at these conclusions.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm going to say a statement instead of a question.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: To respect time, if the chair will allow.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I think it is fair to say that settlement negotiations around the FEPS expansion, there needs to be a number in the budget this year in this negotiation.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And so I'm hoping that with the controller and the council's more rosy projections for revenue in this year with the savings that are being identified, maybe more savings that are being identified, that we really get there

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: in terms of being able to expand vouchers for families that need it.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I think we can do so much more.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And just on my question before, I do want to make sure that we get on the same page that FEPS is cheaper on a per person basis than shelter is because I just because I believe you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I believe that you want to focus you and your colleagues in this administration want to focus on permanent housing.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I believe that you mean that and I believe that this administration and this council can think big together and that we can be the first sort of administration to pivot the city from fulfilling our right to shelter mandate through shelter

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: and do that instead in housing.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We can be the beginning of a title shift.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I just really wanted to emphasize that and thank you chair for the extra time.

chapters.summary: Narcisse voices support for the Rockaway trauma center, noting 100,000 people rely on one hospital and a police officer was lost due to response time. She also asks for construction dollars for a Canarsie rec center for youth, noting she has $500,000 secured and emphasizing prevention over crisis response.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse questions OMB on Rockaway trauma center and Canarsie rec center

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And we can be the catalyst for this process and make sure it happens.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And you're so alert.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Usually people get tired at a certain time.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But as chair of hospitals committee and ER nurse for many years in level one trauma center,

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I want to really put my support behind the trauma center we're talking about on the Rockbury Peninsula.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Peninsula needed for a long time, and I hope that we can address this.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: More than 100,000 people live in the peninsula, and they only rely on one single hospital for service.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We lost a police officer because of a time-consuming, and you know when you're in an emergency, it's a tick-tock moment.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Anytime you can be gone.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And we don't want that to happen, so I'm hoping that you look into this.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: This is very important.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Now, coming back to my District 46, Canarsie has lived through a lot.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: When we're talking about gun violence, our youth and our kids really want to have a rec center, and they've been asking for it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You know, I play basketball in the court with them, and that's all they ask me, when we're going to have a rec center.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We don't have no swimming pool, and I think that's a great opportunity for you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: for us to address those.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: This is a prevention.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You know, as a nurse, we do preventive, you know, we have to prevent things from happening.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So I hope that we can work together on this.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I already have a steady money, half a million dollars, and I'm hoping that if anything, anywhere in your budget, you can put some construction dollars to help us out to save our kids.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So thank you for raising it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Happy to talk to you more about it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And, you know, it's the next generation of Knicks players on that basketball court.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm a mom.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What can I tell you?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I grew up with them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Now I'm still playing basketball with them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I don't want them to die.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I'm sure you don't want that, too.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You seem you're a smart man and you know prevention is better than curing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I hope we can sit down and discuss that and find a way for both projects.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Very important.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Maloney asks about net business closures, struggling industries, and how the budget supports small businesses. Solomon cites SBS investments including BEST program, commercial lease assistance, and mobile food vending, plus job creation through the housing plan. Maloney also asks about RACE/REAP incentive analysis, which Solomon says is underway.
chapters.title: CM Maloney questions OMB on business formation decline and tax incentive analysis

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilwoman Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I have some questions around economic development.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The EDC's economic snapshot from May 29th was generally upbeat, but net business formation was down 4,000 year over year and down 1,000 from the previous quarter.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And more businesses have closed than new businesses were formed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In your assessment, what is causing the net business closure in New York?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I'll do a rapid fire for time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Are there specific industries that are struggling more than others?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And how does the administration's priorities supporting small businesses and economic growth are incorporated in this budget plan?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And overall, I mean, I think we're...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Clearly, we're going to work with and have been working with EDC to drill down on the challenges related to the business community.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think that when you look at the executive budget and what we've been able to do with small business services,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: providing the support there, whether it's the best B-E-S-T for the acronym that we have for the engagement with the small businesses

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: for services to be provided that we invested in the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We also invested for small business services now in terms of the commercial lease assistance, as we talked about.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We talked about the right to counsel.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So, and obviously funding them for their efforts on mobile food vending.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we are being able to focus on small businesses writ large.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, I think that overall we are looking at

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, the investments that we have in the universal child care space.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We are looking at the affordability agenda, the housing plan, the block-by-block housing plan, where you're talking about the creation of 30,000 construction jobs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You're talking about the creation of permanent 12,700 jobs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In terms of operations of housing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So there are a number of ways that we can support job creation and businesses within the city through our affordability agenda and our focus there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Has OMB conducted any analysis on the efficacy of incentive programs that we have for business relocation taxes like RACE or REAP and what that has done for revenue of the city?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Have you considered expanding?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Forming or replacing any of those models?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do we have a sense of what businesses have been bringing revenue to the city?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Just that line of questioning.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, we are looking at those.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think it's prudent to look at those, to take a fresh look, you know, coming in.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I know that many of these incentive programs have existed for many, many years, and they are extended at, you know, four years, you know, in four-year intervals of late at least.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we need to look at sort of the original sort of the inception and the

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: value proposition when they were first created, what they have produced for economic development, what kind of industries have thrived under those or which industries have not, which industries need the incentive.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And where across the city does it make sense?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we are looking at that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We will look at that with the Economic Development Corporation and with the Department of Finance as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And which CSO, if any, is in charge of reviewing savings and how we structure those tax breaks for economic development but also for housing?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So on the economic development piece, well, obviously, you know, there is a chief savings officer with respect to EDC.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But when we talk about the effort to look at the tax incentives, it's cross-cutting across many different portfolios.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so the Office of the First Deputy Mayor.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: and OMB with the respective agencies that this may impact are all looking at these together.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You could have situations as well where there is an incentive that impacts a particular industry, whether it's manufacturing, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we have to look at where other portfolios will invite them in to be able to look at those as well and get that perspective.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So it is a multitude of different parties that will come to this conversation.

chapters.summary: Maloney raises that NYC tourism is 10 million visitors short of projections yet funding decreases from $28 million to $23 million in FY27. Solomon notes World Cup investments in international advertising and openness to discussing NYC Tourism's budget request. Maloney notes smaller cities spend 2-3x more on tourism marketing.
chapters.title: CM Maloney questions OMB on tourism funding cuts and marketing investment

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: My last question, thank you, Chair, is around the revenue driver of tourism.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So New York City tourism was projected to reach 76 million visitors by 2026, but the city is roughly 10 million short of that projection.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right now the plan has 28 million in...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 28 million in the current year, which is decreasing from 23 million in FY27.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So why is the city decreasing support for NYC tourism and conventions, especially in this economic environment?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And can you commit to at least increasing the budget to prior year's levels?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, just so we can speak to the administration's support for increasing tourism to help drive tourism revenue.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we are in receipt of the request from NYC Tourism for

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: for additional funds.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We look forward to discussing that with you and the council in the weeks ahead.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Of course, we want to make sure that we have more tourists that are coming here.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, obviously, a mix of federal policy and other reasons maybe aren't, you know, leading to

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: you know, not exceeding what previous levels have been.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have been investing, or we have invested, obviously, it's not on a permanent thing, but with the World Cup, we've invested more in tourism that is being used for advertisement, particularly in international

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: destinations to make New York a tourist attraction.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Obviously, looking at more permanent investments and how we can bring tourism here on a regular basis in order to the benefit of the city because of obviously the tax revenue, so on.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we want more tourists to come, and we'll be looking at that budget request along with you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The last point I'll make is that cities that are much smaller than New York with far fewer hotel rooms are spending two to three X on their marketing budgets for tourism.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think it's something we should definitely have more conversations about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the three-year $1.86 billion hotel trade group contract for shelters. DiStefano confirms it's a renewal of an existing umbrella contract terminable with a month's notice, paying only for rooms used. Lee asks about competitive bidding and plans to reduce hotel shelter use, with Solomon citing cost containment efforts.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on $1.86 billion hotel shelter contract renewal

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, really quick last round questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In March 2026, the admin signed a $1.86 billion contract with the city's hotel trade group for shelters over the next three years, which is a little concerning.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We know that hotel shelters come at a higher cost to the city compared to purpose-built.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: shelters and can you verify that the city entered into such a contract if so was this a new contract or renewal of the of an existing one

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: sure chair let me turn it over to Patrick DiStefano to address that so this is a renewal of an existing contract so it renews the term

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's a three-year contract.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It can be terminated, I think, with a month's notice.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So you're just paying for the rooms that you have, and it streamlines the process when there's a shortage of rooms in the shelter system.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Obviously, we want to get out of the hotel, but in the short term, this allows us to have sites.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so after the three years, is there an option for extension?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If so, for how many?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't believe there's an option for an extension after this one, but we can double check.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I will say that some of these I know were supposed to come to an end in certain areas, and so if we could re-look at some of these and look at the purpose-built shelters, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And was this contract competitively bid?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If not, why not and how was it procured?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would have to get back to you on that specifically.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Since it's a renewal, it was a continuation, but I'm not sure the origin of it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then also before you renewed it, what was that process like?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Did you look at if there were any complaints or if there were any issues with the contracts?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, because this is sort of a master agreement or an umbrella contract, it's really so that DHS can quickly get in and out of sites.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And it also eases the burden for the providers because they're not negotiating directly when they have to jump to a new site.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't know that there would be another parallel entity that could provide this service.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what are you doing to reduce the use of hotel shelters in the coming years?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So this is part of the cost containment plan as well, but obviously we've seen the family population decrease slightly over time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're doing a number of different things like we spoke about front door, making sure people don't get evicted, increasing rates of counsel.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Funding, you know, all the steps to make sure that we are providing every aspect for families and individuals so that they don't enter their shelter system.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how OMB worked with DSS to develop the cost containment plan and calculate savings. Solomon describes convening new DSS leadership, Corporation Counsel, and the Deputy Mayor to identify controllable measures, then using DSS data to make reasonable assumptions about results, producing recurring savings without cutting vouchers.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions OMB on DSS cost containment plan development and savings calculation

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then really quickly with the DSS cost containment, which you were just talking about, how did OMB work with DSS to develop the cost containment plan and how are the savings amounts calculated?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, we, and I'll kick it back to Patrick as well, but we convened really the new leadership, the new commissioner at DSS.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: had really all of the players across the board in terms of the Corporation Council, you know, the Deputy Mayor for Health and Human Services, and we really just took a look at what we can do across the board

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: that was within our own control.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We knew that we had a state agenda.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We knew that we had things that we couldn't do on our own, that we needed the state to intervene in, but what are the things we're able to do?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And once they were identified, along with the housing team, and again, Health and Human Services, Corp Council, City Hall,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We then started to estimate, just like we would estimate any cost containment proposal.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've looked at sort of what the trajectory would be if we made this change using available data from the Department of Social Services, for example.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And then we use that data to then make reasonable assumptions about what the results would be.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we're confident they are real savings, they're recurring savings.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, the program itself, in terms of city FEPS, lifeline for many New Yorkers, we think that this will strengthen it while also making some reasonable operational efficiency adjustments.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, are there any concerns that the strategy may slow down some of the application review processes and do you anticipate more overhead support will be needed?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: No, when we've worked with the agency on this, we think that the level of administrative support, overhead, things like that are in place.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Like I said before, we did add a significant amount of funding to DSS for staffing in this plan so that they're able to hire up.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The two-for-one's gone.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Some of the IT measures they're putting into place should improve the process.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we're confident that it's not going to make it longer.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Hopefully it's the other direction.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think that's it, actually.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just want to plug that Councilmember Brooks-Powers did mention she wants us to look at the $5 million in the budget, as well as Senator Sanders'$1 million towards the trauma center.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if we can get a timeline, I think she wanted it by today if possible, but if we can get a timeline on that, that'd be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But I just want to thank you so much, Director Solomon and your team, and also thank you to our controller in the back for waiting so patiently on your team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But thank you for giving us the extra time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We really, really, really appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee concludes the OMB questioning segment, thanks Director Solomon and his team, and transitions to the next portion of the hearing with the Comptroller. A break and room move occurs before the Comptroller segment begins.
chapters.title: Transition from OMB to Comptroller testimony segment

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we're going to transition right away.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, I think we're ready to go.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee reconvenes the hearing in the committee room, introduces colleagues present, welcomes Comptroller Mark Levine and his team, and reminds the public about tomorrow's 9:30 AM public testimony session before swearing in the witnesses.
chapters.title: Chair Lee reconvenes hearing for Comptroller Levine testimony

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I want to make sure I get everybody.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so we're going to continue on.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So good afternoon and welcome to the final day of hearings on FY27 Executive Budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Linda Lee and excited to be here with my colleagues, our Deputy Speaker Williams, Murano, Brewer, Wong.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hanks, majority whip Hanks, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think that's it for now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome to our awesome comptroller and dear friend, my dear friend, Mark Levine and your team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's, as a reminder, oh, this is what I have to do every single year, not every single year, before every testimony.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, I'm losing my language right now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's getting towards the end.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But we're having public hearings all day tomorrow starting at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So make sure that you all sign up, please, because we want to hear from the public and hear your feedback.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please sign up.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I think we're going to get straight into it and turn it over to our committee council to swear in our comptroller and the witnesses.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Comptroller Levine begins testimony by highlighting contradictions: a strong economy with near-record stock market and 6% tax collection growth, but also halted private sector job creation, 4.6% inflation, declining real wages for lower-paid workers, and greater economic uncertainty than any time in recent memory.
chapters.title: Comptroller Levine: Economic contradictions and strong but uncertain outlook

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee and members of the Finance Committee and City Council.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I know it's been a long day.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I've been there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I promise I'll keep it energetic.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm pleased that I'm joined today by Executive Deputy Comptroller for Finance and Economics, Francesco Brindisi, and Deputy Comptroller for Budget, Krista Olson.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I really appreciate this opportunity to share my office's assessment of the fiscal year 2027 executive budget and the May financial plan for fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is a time of enormous contradictions in New York City's economy and budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the one hand, our city's economy is strong today by most measures.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the other hand, we are now heading into a period of greater economic uncertainty than any time in recent memory.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the one hand, the stock market is near an all-time high.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the other hand, private sector job creation in the city has essentially come to a halt.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the one hand, our budget is being bolstered by a significant increase in tax revenue.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the other hand, we face extremely large out-year deficits.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: How can all those things be true at once?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I will attempt to square these contradictions in my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'll also assess the extent to which the executive budget addresses our most pressing fiscal challenges.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I'll suggest measures we can and should take now to prepare us for the uncertainty ahead.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: First, the state of our economy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Wall Street has just continued to show record strength.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This has helped power our tax collections, which through April are 6% over last year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: My office was thus able to revise our tax forecast upward by $927 million in fiscal year 26 and $1.1 billion in FY27 since March.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: After the blowout first quarter, Wall Street's profits in calendar year 2026 are now projected at $54 billion, below last year's record $65 billion, but still quite high by historical standards.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Our jobs picture is more complicated.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We are in what some are calling a low-hire, low-fire economy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thanks to the relatively low pace of layoffs, the employment population ratio in New York City is actually at an all-time high.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But due to limited hiring, the private sector here, excluding educational and health services, has shed nearly 6,000 jobs so far in April.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Inflation in New York City is rising at a worrying pace, hitting 4.6% year over year in April, the steepest in three years.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Gas here was up 34%, food up 5%, electricity in the Northeast regions up 12%, all putting an even greater affordability burden on New Yorkers who are struggling.

chapters.summary: Levine expresses relief that the executive budget no longer calls for property tax increases or rainy day drawdowns, and praises baselined funding for DOE, right to counsel, libraries, and Fair Fares. However, he warns the budget relies on $6.1 billion in one-time measures including pension re-amortization, accounting adjustments, and delayed class size implementation.
chapters.title: Comptroller Levine: Relief on property tax and baselined programs, but gap closed with one-shots

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Wage growth here has been strong, but here again, there are two stories.
