utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The highest paid industries, where workers make an average of $225,000 annually, saw wages grow by 5.7% over the past year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For the two-thirds of New York City private sector workers in lower-paying jobs, real wages have declined.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: What does all this mean for our budget?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm relieved that today we're reviewing an executive budget which no longer calls for a property tax increase and no longer drains our rainy day fund.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm also pleased that, after years of uncertainty, the plan baselines funding for a variety of important programs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This includes 269 baseline for DOE and FY27, for technology, the Learning to Work program, IESP support, and a partial fix to school custodial budgets.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Additional baseline items include right to counsel, library subsidies, and foster care contracts, as well as an additional $25 million for fare fares.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: How is it that given the dire warnings about

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: our budget gap that I and others issued in January, we've been able to make so much progress in recent months.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: By far the biggest driver is the surge in city revenues, both collected and forecasted, which totaled nearly $7 billion since the November plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We reached a much needed increase in funding from Albany.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Which we are grateful to the governor and legislature for providing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the mayor, to his credit, has committed to a number of efficiencies and savings measures.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But all this was not enough to fully close the gap.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The budget is in fact balanced with a great deal of temporary fixes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The plan relies on a slew of one-time measures and short-term savings, which altogether total $6.1 million in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: These are measures which, by definition, will not be available next year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They include $2.3 billion from the re-amortization of the unfunded pension liability.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: $1.6 billion in accounting adjustments to prior year accrued expenses, in addition to the $500 million already budgeted in the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Nearly $1 billion from the more gradual timeline to achieve the class size mandate and from the clawback of H&H's share of pension re-amortization savings.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: $200 million in one-time lower subsidy to the MTA, in addition to the $500 million already included in the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Beyond the one-time measures, the executive

chapters.summary: Levine warns that reduced prepayments and prior year expense re-estimates mean spending $4.4 billion more than revenue this year, with the General Reserve at just $100 million. He projects an $8.8 billion FY28 gap, noting the city won't have one-shot measures available next year and faces this without assuming a recession or AI disruption.
chapters.title: Comptroller Levine: $8.8 billion FY28 gap and structural imbalance warnings

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: budget plan also dramatically draws down prepayment of next year's expenses from $3.8 billion in FY25 to roughly $1 billion in FY26, a 72% decline year over year, the largest single drop

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: since 2002, right after the 9-11 attacks.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Taking the reduced prepayment and the re-estimate of prior year expenses together means that we are spending $4.4 billion more than we're bringing in in this fiscal year, continuing a worrisome trend.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And next year, the General Reserve stands at just $100 million, and we're already assuming reductions in prior year expenses, setting a new precedent for using these types of measures even before the start of the fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I also have to note that some of the savings in the plan are really just targets at this point.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The plan still carries roughly $600 million in unallocated efficiencies from various agencies, plus $1.97 billion over four years in three new cost containment targets.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: City FEPS, DHS shelter, and DOE special education due process cases, all still lacking implementation plans.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In plain English, this all means that we are kicking a very big can into next year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Just how big is that can?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The executive budget projects a gap of no less than $7.1 million in FY28 and rising steadily.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Now, my office, of course, runs our own calculations of out-year gaps.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: When we plug in our tax forecast and our estimates of under-budgeted costs and unmet program needs,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: including overtime and child care vouchers, the FY28 gap widens even further to a whopping $8.8 billion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We will face that gap without the option of the many one-shot measures that we've used up this year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We won't be able to re-amortize our pension again.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're unlikely to be able to further delay class size implementation, etc., etc.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And all of this is predicated on a strong economy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Our baseline forecast does not assume a recession.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It does not assume an AI-driven labor dislocation or the popping of an AI investment bubble.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Outcomes which, according to our recently released analysis, could lower revenues relative to baseline by between $9 billion and $14 billion over the period of the financial plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This plan assumes continued strength in the securities industry, in the pool of bonus payments, in stock markets, the volatile foundations of our personal and business income tax collections.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The city's bottom line in FY27 depends, in a real sense, on the bull market continuing for another year.

chapters.summary: Levine highlights that the $2 billion rainy day fund ranks NYC ninth of the ten largest cities in reserves relative to revenue, ahead only of Chicago. He notes Moody's, Fitch, and Kroll lowered the city's credit outlook to negative, warning that structural gaps, low reserves, and repeated one-shots increase downgrade risk.
chapters.title: Comptroller Levine: Inadequate reserves and credit rating downgrade risk

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Any of those assumptions can break.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If we do hit turbulence ahead, our reserves are nowhere near.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: year adequate.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The city will finish this year with just $2 billion in the Revenue Stabilization Fund, aka our rainy day fund.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This leaves us with an extremely thin financial cushion relative to the scale of our budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Even if you count the $5.2 billion in our Retiree Health Benefit Trust, which I'll remind you is an offset to roughly $100 billion long-term liabilities in health care for retirees, though it's been used as a de facto rainy day reserve,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: we're still far below the amount we need to weather a typical recession.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And among the 10 largest cities in the nation, our reserves relative to revenue rank us ninth, ahead only of Chicago.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Looming over all of this are our credit rating agencies.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Moody's, Fitch, and Kroll sent us a warning sign when they lowered the outlook for the city's credit from stable to negative.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't expect any immediate further actions from them, but the risk of a downgrade in the months ahead remains.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The structural gap, the low level of reserves, and the repeated reliance on one-shot measures all increase the risk of future credit rating downgrades.

chapters.summary: Levine proposes formal rules for the rainy day fund: a 16% of tax revenue target, 10% floor, deposit formula when revenues exceed trend, and narrow withdrawal criteria limited to recessions or catastrophes. He notes adherence would mean adding $854 million this year, not leaving it flat, and urges the council to avoid adding more one-shots.
chapters.title: Comptroller Levine: Proposed rainy day fund rules and charter amendment

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We should do everything we can to avoid this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: One critical step we can take is to establish formal rules for our rainy day fund.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In April, I laid out a plan to establish a target balance for the fund of 16% of tax revenue.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: with the 10% floor, a deposit formula when revenues exceed trend, and clear, narrow criteria for withdrawals.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In other words, rules that require we put money in.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: in when times are good and only take money out when times are tough.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'll be proposing a charter amendment to establish these rules in the coming days and hope you'll join me in this effort.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If we were to adhere to that funding for a rainy day for

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: to that formula for our rainy day fund this year, a year in which the economy is strong and tax revenues are up significantly, we would be adding $854 million to the fund, not leaving it flat as the current plan does.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I urge you to find ways to add to the rainy day fund as your work to finalize the budget in the coming weeks takes place.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I know that you will be pushing hard to fund the Council's priorities and initiatives in the final budget, as well you should.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I fought for many of these investments when I was in the Council.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: My ask is that you avoid adding even more one-shots to the budget as you find ways to offset the additional expenses you're fighting for.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: New York City has many strengths to draw on as we navigate these challenges.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Our economy is strong.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Our workforce is supremely talented.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Our tax base is the envy of other cities.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, we face real economic uncertainty ahead.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, we face a structural imbalance in our budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we have all the raw ingredients we need to solve this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we still have time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Let's act decisively.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Let's seize this moment.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you, and I look forward to your questions.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about DOE's $122 million estimate for 70% class size compliance. Levine estimates ultimate cost of $1 billion annually by 2030 for expense side, plus $14-18 billion in capital needs not currently in the plan. Staff estimate $25 million for proposed differential payments to teachers in exempted classes.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on class size compliance costs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by my council member Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so really quickly, I just have a few questions on class size compliance.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the administration, do you agree with the administration's $122 million estimate for Dewey's cost to meet 70% class size compliance next year?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we estimate that ultimately this will cost us a billion a year on the expense side by 2030.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think at that point they have it in there for $800 million.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we do see a shortfall at that point.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For FY27, I also believe we have a slightly higher estimate than they do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, sorry, but was the billion for 100%?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I believe that we peak at a billion for 2030, correct?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We assume it's going to be what they originally said of a billion dollars in 2030.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For fiscal year 27, the $122 million should be sufficient to hire the 1,000 teachers that they've said they need.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we are waiting for their class size report that comes out later this month.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: To confirm.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you have an estimate for the cost of the proposed differential payments to the teachers in exempted classes?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't think we have an estimate because we're waiting for the class size update, which I think could happen any day.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's supposed to come in June.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I think it would be on the order of high single-digit millions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Am I in the right range?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For the differential, we calculated it just based on...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Those exempted classes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Last year, or in the current school year, and found it to be about $25 million.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But again, as the controller said, we're waiting for the updated class size report and the number of exempted classes going forward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with an extended timeline, do you have a new estimate on the cost to meet 100%?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, that's the $1 billion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's the $1.30 number, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That'll be a billion a year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: By the way, the capital cost as well, right, because we have to build out for some schools.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Under the administration, they originally projected that as an $18 billion capital expense.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Now, I believe in November, they offered an update saying they found some more efficient use of space that would run at about $14 billion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have nothing like that currently in the capital plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I believe it's about a billion and a half in there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So by any measure, on the capital side, we're going to need to put more money in.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about lessons from past technology shifts and AI's potential revenue impact. Levine describes five scenarios ranging from very positive to very negative, including potential loss of 250,000 jobs and $9-14 billion in revenue losses if the AI bubble pops, urging reserve buildup as preparation.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on AI report scenarios and revenue impact

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then moving on to AI, I know that your office just released a report on AI and New York City's fiscal future.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The issues related to AI have multiplied over the last few years as AI's capability, sorry, has increased dramatically.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As with many new technologies, government is often years behind in learning how to deal with the new advances.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what lessons should we have learned from the past in how we deal with AI industry?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And can you summarize your AI scenarios presented in your report and how they may affect the city's revenues?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: What a good way to frame the question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This city has been...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: reshaped again and again by technology from steam to electricity to railroads to automobiles to container shipping to the internet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Literally rebuilt the physical structure of the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And in some ways, sometimes we manage that well and sometimes we fail to.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We ignored the arrival of container shipping, and we no longer have major ports in the five boroughs of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We lost that to other places who adapted quicker, just to give you one example.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think the Internet on the whole has probably made us stronger as a global financial hub and hub for knowledge work.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think that AI is going to be by far the biggest transformation yet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I think we should assume it will change the city in powerful ways, change the economy in powerful ways, potentially very good, and also potentially very bad.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's hard to exactly predict where we're headed because the experts themselves don't agree.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The technologists don't agree with the economists, and the economists don't agree with each other.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we decide to embrace that uncertainty, and we developed a forecast that included five different scenarios.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we ascribed a percentage likelihood to each.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They range from...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Very positive, where we could see an uptick in the market because of AI and net increase in jobs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And they range all the way to very negative scenarios, which include potentially a rapid loss of a quarter of a million jobs in a matter of months.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We also forecast these potentially could take a hit to the budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For example, if the AI bubble pops, an estimate of between $9 and $14 billion, just talking about the budget hit.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think we need to prepare for these eventualities, both good and bad.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: How do you prepare for uncertainty ahead?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: How does a family prepare?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: How does a small business prepare?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: How does the government prepare?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We need to build up our reserves, our savings, as a family would say.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we just have very, very little, as I outlined in our plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have such a small amount relative to the scale of the budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so we believe this should add to our urgency of building the reserves now, so that if we do see the bubble pop, we can avoid cutting services.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If we do see people unemployed, we can help support them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's what we call for in our analysis.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about proposed rainy day fund rules. Levine details a 16% target ($13.5 billion), 10% floor, deposit formula of 20% of revenue above 3% growth plus a six-year lookback, and withdrawal limited to recessions (two consecutive quarters of job loss) or catastrophes including AI disruption, requiring state legislation.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on rainy day fund rules and deposit formula

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's funny.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So that's the perfect segue to my next question, which is about the rainy day funds.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the administration's preliminary plan included a drawdown of the city's rainy day fund, and the city council and comptroller were in agreement that such an action would be detrimental to the city's fiscal stability.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The new charter commission created by Mayor Mamdani is considering the proposals that would provide more guidance.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I know you had spoken about this, but what rules would you suggest for the usage of the rainy day fund?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And do you have any recommendations as to how deposits into the reserve fund should be managed?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Big picture here, we're coming off a very long run of

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: A successful economy of New York.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Other than COVID, essentially going back to the financial crisis, we've had a 16-year bull market, and we basically have nothing to show for it in our reserves.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have a rainy day fund of $2 billion against a city budget of $125.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So it's just a crying shame that we've had all those good years go by, and we didn't save enough.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the first thing that the formula we're calling for would do would set targets so that when you have a good year in the economy, when tax revenue is growing, that you are required to put more money in or deposit into the fund.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We call for 20% of what's over a 3% growth to go into the fund, plus a look back, an average over the last six years, 40% from the look back.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So that we're putting money in every year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And to give you a sense of the scale, that would mean this year, when our economy, as at the moment, very strong, we would have to put about $830 million in, give or take a little.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But there also needs to be rules about when you can take out.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Because we were about to take out now when the economy is good, which is not the purpose of a rainy day fund.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is not a rainy day.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: From an economic perspective, the sun is shining.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the condition should be either a recession, which is a little bit hard to measure in real time, but we define it as two consecutive quarters of job loss, or some

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Sort of catastrophe.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: A natural disaster, a pandemic, God forbid, a massive terrorist attack.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Now, what we called for in our report is we should add major AI disruption to the list, and that probably requires a change to the state legislation.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: How big do we want it to be?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we believe it should be 16% of our annual tax revenue.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's not an arbitrary number.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's the amount that we would need to cover in a normal recession so we didn't have to cut services.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Right, that's our goal.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If we enter a recession, we want to protect services.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If we have a reserve fund of 16% of our tax receipts, that would allow us to avoid, and I think that's...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: About $12 billion now, $13 billion?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: $13.5 by today's budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then in terms of the floor, of the minimum amount that the city should be...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 10% would be our floor.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, we're at $2 billion in the rainy day fund, which is, I don't know, 1.7% or something, so we're way below the target.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 10% would give us essentially the first half of the recession, the first two years, so it would give us some time to cushion the blow.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about revised pied-à-terre tax revenue now that final legislation is public. Levine says the office is now confident the $500 million target will be met, potentially exceeding it, with a no-behavior-change estimate of $1 billion. He notes watching for impacts on sale prices and real estate market effects.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on pied-à-terre tax revenue estimates

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, moving to pied-a-terre tax.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On April 15th, when the pied-a-terre tax was announced by the governor and mayor, the claim was this would raise $500 million for the city each year, starting in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think your office had put out a report finding that the revenues could actually be substantially less than that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the exact contours are now...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Public based on the legislation passed as part of the final state budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So now that we have the true outline of the new tax, can you tell us how much money you think it will raise next year?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So, Madam Chair, this is a moving target because you can define the rates.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we don't know yet how many people will seek exemptions, how many people will contest their assessment, how many people will change their behavior.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Maybe they have a relative move into their home so that they're no longer considered a pied-à-terre.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And certainly if we look at other cities that have implemented this, that has happened.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Which is why when the initial proposal was released, we gave a slightly lower ask.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: estimate for what we thought would come in because we assumed some of these factors.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But once the final language was printed in the bill, we can say with a little more certainty that we are actually quite confident that we will at least meet our baseline of 500 million and that potentially it could be much more than that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If you assume no behavior change, we'd be at a billion, which we don't expect.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we're confident enough that in, you heard me express doubts on some revenue in the budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're actually not putting uncertainty on that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We think that we're pretty confident that even with the change in behavior and people contesting, that we'll still hit the $500 million target.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know some have raised concerns.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: To your point, behavior changes, that this tax could negatively impact the real estate market and property values.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so how much of this are you concerned with or what sort of is your thoughts on that?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Look, we don't know yet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we're going to have to watch what happens to sale prices and the number of sales.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think it's...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It is logical to assume that you could have an impact on sale price if you have an additional cost, calling it a surcharge now, technically not a tax.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we really won't know yet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that could have an economic impact.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we certainly will be watching for that in the comptroller's office.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the cost of holding school budgets harmless amid declining enrollment. Levine estimates approximately $260 million currently, warns of growing mismatches between budget and enrollment, suggests phasing in changes, and raises that 200 schools have under 200 students, suggesting consolidation could save money.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on school budget hold harmless policy costs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Moving on to the school budgets and the hold harmless.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I know on Sunday the DOE chancellor announced that DOE

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We would hold initial school budget allocations harmless for 26-27 school year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The city has been holding initial school budgets harmless in recent years as enrollment has declined citywide.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I wanted to know if your office has an estimated cost for holding initial school budgets harmless, and will this funding be added at adoption?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, to remind people of the big picture here, we've had a very large drop in enrollment in New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're down probably a quarter of a million students.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the budget has gone up every year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And part of the reason for that mismatch is a policy that was put in place during the pandemic for understandable reasons at the time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That even if an individual school lost enrollment, you didn't want to cut the budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And this was COVID.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There was so much stress on the system that we just wanted to protect schools.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we're now five or six years after that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And some of these schools are pretty far off their pre-pandemic enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But you have schools that are 10 or 20 or 30 or 40 or even 50% off.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so, wow, the mismatch is getting quite big.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And in effect, it means it's a lot of money per kid that a heavily enrolled school that's overcrowded, which you might think would want more help, is actually getting less per child.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I believe that we are going to eventually have to resolve this and, frankly, do it pretty soon because of all the strains in our budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Not in a way that would shock these schools from one day to the next.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You don't want them to endure catastrophic cuts.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This could be phased in in a fair way that's predictable.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You want to protect services to kids first and foremost.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we believe that today it's about a $260 million difference.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, because we're waiting for this all-important assessment of enrollment that's coming soon, we don't actually have an exact fix for you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the number I just offered is really just a guess at this point.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then to your point about phasing it in, what do you think would be a fiscally responsible way in terms of timeline to phase that?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm not sure I would get something specific.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would be happy with almost any plan to phase it in.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Right now we're just on this indefinite path of allowing greater and greater and greater differences between budget and enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I'd be comfortable with any plan that just got people on track.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And, you know, I also have to say, Madam Chair, that we have now 200 schools with under 200 kids in their schools in such a big city with 8 million plus residents.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that also is a big budget cost there because obviously you have a lot of fixed costs for a school.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Some of these schools are so small, it's not even good for kids.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They can't even field a sports team.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so I do think that as part of our effort to find savings, we should look at combining more schools.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's tough.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It has to be done sensitively.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Parents need to be part of the process.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But this, too, is also weighing on the...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: system as again we had this mismatch between a growing budget and declining enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether $149 million in FY27 due process savings is realistic. Levine notes Carter cases tripled from $500 million to $1.5 billion in six years, supports the mayor's strategy of offering more public school services, but notes it's hard to assess exact savings since additional service spending may precede savings.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on due process case cost containment realism

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then as we know the due process case cases cost quite a bit.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have you conducted an analysis of the due process case cost containment savings item included in the executive plan and DOE's budget?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if so, do you believe $149 million in savings in FY27 is realistic?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, first, big picture, we've had such a rapid rise in this line.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In 2019, we were spending $500 million on so-called Carter cases.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's going to hit $1.5 billion, so a tripling in six or seven years.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You don't really see that in government very often.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's got to be contained, and I do applaud the mayor for articulating a plan to do that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It has not really been defined yet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think the vision is that we'll offer more services in schools so that people, kids can receive what they need in the public schools and not have to go to the private schools.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That is definitely a good strategy, which I totally support.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But it's a little bit hard to be exact on the savings because you have to account for additional money we spend if we need to spend on additional services.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And it could be that we spend money on services today and don't get the savings right away.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So it's a little hard for us to assess this exactly.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Though, again, I think the mayor is absolutely right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That we need more services for children with learning differences in the public schools so they don't need to go to the private system.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks on the Speaker's behalf how the FY28 gap changed from $13.9 billion to $8.8 billion. Levine explains the change is mostly explained by the property tax increase reversal and pension re-amortization, notes his office is slightly more conservative than OMB on revenue, and that monitors are clustered within about a billion of each other.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on FY28 gap variance and forecasting differences

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then before I hand it off, I know the speaker wanted to be here but could not come back, and so I just wanted to ask a few questions on her behalf.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Of course.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so the FY28 gap in your budget analysis of the preliminary budget would be $13.9 billion, assuming that the property tax increase did not happen, which thankfully it didn't.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So now the updated executive analysis shows a gap of $8.8 billion in FY28.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's a $5 billion change, a large change even if you account for the pension amortization savings of about $1.6 billion in that year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how did your outlook change, the delta change so much?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then with such large variances in the forecast, how can we be sure that it doesn't change so drastically?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what measures are you taking to sort of look at that?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, very good question.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Very important question.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You know, Madam Chair, forecasting turns out to be a little challenging because it's hard to predict the future.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we had no choice but to do our best.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we came out with a forecast of about, I think, just under $88 billion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Excuse me, just over $88 billion in...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Give me the exact number, please.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: What's our total tax revenue forecast for next year?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: vis-a-vis

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 27, yeah, 27, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Right, so we have $87.8 billion as revenue for the coming year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That is a little lower than the mayor's projection, so we're just a tad bit more cautious.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Now, there are other fiscal monitors that also have given assessments, including the Independent Budget Office, which is about another half billion below us.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There is the state comptroller, which I think was right about where the mayor was.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And then there is the financial control board, which is about where we were.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we're clustered within about a billion of each other.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The mayor in the group is the most optimistic.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Council came out with this forecast today, which I'm excited to review.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think you all are about a billion and a half above the mayor's projection.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Any of us could be right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I hope the highest possible projection is right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It really depends on what you think will happen on Wall Street.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That is kind of the core assumption.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mentioned that Wall Street had its best year on record, the most profitable year on record, the biggest bonus season on record.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And what we assumed is that next year Wall Street has a good year, but a little bit off the top.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's how we played it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's possible that we break the record again, and I hope we do.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But as you heard in my testimony, I see some reasons to be cautious out there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I will also add that while we really commend the mayor for ending this era of drastic under-budgeting of major expense lines that we knew we were going to incur,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: we still have a difference of opinion on a few budget lines.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're nowhere near as drastic as prior years.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But if you consider both, we're slightly less optimistic on the revenue and that we have some slightly higher numbers on the expenses.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We go up from, I think the mayor had it at about $7.1 billion for the fiscal year 28 out year gap.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have it at about $8.8 billion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That explains why we are just a little more concerned about the scale of the gap.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks why the Comptroller projects tax revenue growth falling to 2.2% in FY28 and whether a recession is projected. Levine says no recession is assumed but they're being cautious given Wall Street uncertainty, AI risks, and geopolitical factors, with economists putting recession odds at 35-40% for 2027.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on conservative revenue growth assumptions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I think your tax forecast assumes that revenue growth will fall from a 5.5% growth rate this year for FY26 to just 2.2% in FY28, which is around the rate of inflation, if not below.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what leads you to believe that tax revenue growth will slow down so much, and are you projecting a recession?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We are not projecting a recession.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think we are assuming a more typical year in tax growth.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, we are not assuming a repeat of the record profits on Wall Street in the past year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, that could be achieved, but if the AI bubble bursts, there'll be an impact.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If the impact of the war and oil prices and higher interest rates are felt in the city, it could also lead to a slowdown.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I think most economists do put the chance of a recession in 2027 at about 35%.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Some that I trust would probably say closer to 40%.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So for all those reasons, we were a little more cautious.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I will ask our deputy comptroller if he'd like to weigh in on the growth versus inflation question.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, I don't know what the council estimates in terms of inflation going forward.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We see that historically after years of steep growth, then tax revenues tend to moderate, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Overall, our growth rate is 3.3% over the entire forecast horizon.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So, you know, there can be fluctuations year to year, but essentially we are on a slightly less optimistic growth trajectory as the council, which is 4.3%.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And, yeah, so as to the gap, I mean, it was $3.7 billion, the property tax, and then there is $1.7 billion from the re-amortization, so that total is about $5.5 billion, which is about how our 2028

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: gap sort of swings.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So most of it is explained by those two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just, sorry, your last question.

chapters.summary: Lee asks why the Comptroller's outlook is more conservative than OMB's and about upside risk. Levine notes they're within 1% of OMB's projection, explains benefits of caution, and states the city is under more fiscal strain than any time in his 12 years in government despite a currently strong economy.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Comptroller on conservative budgeting and upside risk

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: OMB has had a tradition of conservative budgeting, and based on your analysis, it appears that your office has a more conservative outlook on the city's budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Why is that, and how much upside risk is there that the budget gaps will not be as big as you say?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, you're talking about very large numbers, and so we are within less than 1% of OMB's projection.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As I said, we are about $500 million below on an 88%

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: billion dollar base and we have reasons to be cautious.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Either of us could be right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There are benefits to being cautious though, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Because if the cautious estimate is wrong, then you end up with a little more cash at the end of the year that you can put to vital programs or put into the reserves.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If the more aggressive estimate is wrong,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Then at the end of the year, you're in a hole, and then you have to make very tough decisions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So this is a balancing act, and we're doing our best to get it right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Look, I'll just say that we're, as long as I've been in government, we've had a very strong economy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I've been in government 12 years.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: With the exception of COVID, where we had so much federal money come in that from a city budget perspective, we didn't really endure the pain.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I think this year is different.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The economy today is strong.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I do believe that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we're under more fiscal strain than we have been at any time in my 12 years in government.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I personally see more economic uncertainty on the horizon than at any time in recent memory.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So for all those reasons, we're landing on the slightly more cautious side.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, I totally get it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I will say I've got to give a special shout-out to our finance team and our economists because I know that IBO, the mayor's office, your office, as well as the council, we have different sort of projections that we usually have.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But I'm proud.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: To say that they've been pretty good, so it's great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But hopefully we'll see what happens, obviously.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But please take a look at the report and let us know.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We'd love to have more conversations with you about that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm going to, I have more questions, but I'm passing it off to my colleagues so they can go.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about her bill requiring an AI task force and what steps to ensure AI gains are broadly shared. Levine expresses worry about rapid job disruption, supports exploring new economic models and ownership stakes, and calls for building reserves to support potentially unemployed workers, noting nothing is currently in the budget for this.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions Comptroller on AI task force and economic disruption

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So first up, we have our deputy speaker, followed by our majority whip.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Nice to see you again.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I actually passed a bill that required the city to establish a task force to review and report on AI's impact.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do not know the status.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I literally have texted in our compliance division to follow up because I've been asking them for the status of this bill because it would essentially, it tasked the city to create a task force to really look into a whole wide range

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: of things, workforce impacts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All of the things.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How is AI going to impact the city?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So your report warns that AI would significantly impact employment wages and city tax revenues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what specific steps do you think we should take?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To ensure that AI-driven productivity gains translate into broadly shared economic benefits rather than deeper inequality.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you for leading and issuing that legislation.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's an important report.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We'd love to help make sure that

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: that the task force comes together and love to be part of it if appropriate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I was going to say, you should be a part of it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I don't know if the, I have to check to see if the bill requires, you know, these task forces, sometimes we require different citywide to appoint someone.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we'll have to see.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm really worried about the economic disruption.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm worried about the job disruption.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The chair asked about a historical perspective, and the city lost a million jobs in deindustrialization.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It happened over 40 years, though.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And as painful as it was, there was more time to adapt.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And while we don't know precisely where we're headed on AI, we could lose jobs much more quickly.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we need to be there to support people for that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's hard as a city because so much of the leadership we need has to come from the national government.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we're not getting that right now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I would absolutely support plans to give the American people ownership stakes in some of the companies that stand to make trillions of dollars from this technology.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I support ways to ensure that no one's left behind right now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we'll potentially need new economic models to do that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I've asked what can we do as a city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we have some ability to support people who lose their jobs, maybe by some limited income assistance, perhaps by retraining.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have none of that in the budget right now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: when I see that we're already running a gap and we have very little money in our reserves, if this hits in six months or 18 months, I'm worried that we're not going to be ready to respond quickly enough.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So in the report, what I called for is let's start building up our reserves.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Let's add AI disruption in addition to natural disasters and other things to the list of uses and be ready so that if we have 100,000 people unemployed, we can help support them economically, we can help retrain them, and so much more.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, I love that response.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And if my memory serves me correctly, DCAS had testified and said that they're like,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: somewhat working on this it wasn't clear so I'm still very interested in what internal assessments they're doing to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: make a determination maybe some city employees need to be reskilled or upskilled to maintain their job and that just not hasn't been clear to me so your report

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: also suggests that the city, as you mentioned, should roughly double its reserves.

chapters.summary: Williams asks when accumulating reserves becomes less valuable than investing in workforce development, and about the Comptroller's role in measuring racial equity spending. Levine says building reserves isn't a choice given uncertainty, supports rethinking K-12 and CUNY AI curriculum, and wants his office to be a resource for equity accountability.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions Comptroller on reserves vs investment trade-offs and racial equity

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What objective measures or triggers should we look at to determine when accumulating additional reserves

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: becomes less valuable than investing those dollars into various things that we care about, like workforce development or digital literacy or other interventions?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that could reduce AIs related economic and workforce disruption in the first place?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, it's a fair question.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I think considering the uncertainty ahead, building the reserves really isn't a choice.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's why I want an actual formula there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I am all in for rethinking our retraining in the city, including from K-12 and CUNY, where we're giving people very limited education in the kind of emerging AI skills that employers are asking for.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I talk to employers all the time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I talk to CEOs all the time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's part of my job.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And they're not so much telling me yet that they are slowing down hiring, although I feel that may be beginning, and I can talk about that from anecdotal evidence.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But they are saying to me unambiguously they're hiring people with AI skills now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I look at what we're doing in K-12 and what we're doing at CUNY, and I feel like we are way behind in pivoting the curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think we need to move now quickly, and I would love to work with you on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, just two quick more questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Quick more quick questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So as you know, New York City agencies are now required to develop and implement racial equity plans that are supposed to be attuned to the budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yet one of the persistent challenges is ensuring that those plans are reflected in the actual budget decisions and resource allocation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As the city's chief fiscal watchdog, what role do you believe your office should play in measuring where

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Are their agency spending aligned with their racial equity commitments?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And are there additional tools, reporting requirements, or accountability mechanisms you believe are needed to ensure these plans result in tangible outcomes rather than simply compliance exercises?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You've been leading on this, Deputy Speaker, and I really am grateful for that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The city has a very, very long and shameful history of underinvesting in communities in need, in communities of color.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is well documented, and we don't want that to happen on our watch.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so we do need to ask very serious questions about where and how our money is spent and who is benefiting, and is there equity, geographic equity, racial equity, and more.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I would love for my office to be a resource here.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've talked some about this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would love to find ways to help make sure that this information is accounted for and public so we can do the right thing here.

chapters.summary: Williams raises flooding claims in Southeast Queens and asks if the Comptroller's office has resources to help residents file claims. Levine explains his office can't help fill out claims due to adjudication conflicts, but is launching an effort to partner with every council member to educate residents and improve the process.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions Comptroller on Southeast Queens flooding claims process

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, last but very final question about just Southeast Queens flooding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So one of the things, and I haven't really spoken to you about this, I should have added that to my list of things that I spoke to you about when we met, is the claims process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I know it's tricky because it's a legal document, but just wanted to know if your office has done anything to advance on making sure communities who are seeking to file complaints for like flooding related issues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: have the proper resources to do that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And if you think that the city's capital investment adequately prioritizes communities.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, this is people, not everyone knows that our office manages claims.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And you obviously know if you live in Southeast Queens, and this is something I really want to partner with city council members on.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Because technically my office is not allowed to help people fill out their claim because we are adjudicating it, so it would be a conflict.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: What we want to do is empower you and every council member to help provide that assistance in your offices, and we're actually launching an effort now to go to every council member.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: My wonderful team, led by Deputy Comptroller Alana Cantillo, is already working on this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We really would like to partner with the whole body so that you know how this works, you can help educate people, and also give us feedback if we can make the process easier.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That we can do for sure.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I really want to work with you on this.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Awesome.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, I don't know if I said we've been joined by Councilmember Maloney as well.

chapters.summary: Hanks asks which agencies present the greatest fiscal accountability concerns and what capital delivery reforms are needed. Levine cites DOE's self-registration of contracts under $25,000 as a blind spot with 150,000+ annual uses, and calls capital costs like $5 million parks bathrooms and $80-100 million subway elevators an absolute scandal requiring reform.
chapters.title: Majority Whip Hanks questions Comptroller on fiscal accountability concerns and capital delivery

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So next we have Majority Whip Hanks, followed by Councilmember Narcisse.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Hi, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Oh, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So my questions.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: The Comptroller's Office regularly identifies risks and inefficiencies in city spending through audits and oversight reports.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Based on your office's recent finding, which agencies or areas of city government present the greatest concerns regarding fiscal accountability?

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Or operational inefficiency.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Councilwoman, you say, what is my greatest concern for operational efficiency?

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: What areas of city government present the greatest concerns regarding fiscal accountability or operational inefficiency?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: My office oversees the contracting process.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As you know, we review and certify contracts.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's a special case at the Department of Ed, which has authority for contracts under $25,000 that actually, they don't have to come to us for registration.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They can self-register there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that creates a little bit of a blind spot for us.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They do eventually show up in Passport.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I say eventually because 95% of the contracts start before they're registered.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we don't often have...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: real-time eyes on them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And in December, a report came out from the Dewey's investigator that there are some challenges there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we're looking into this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There are a shocking number.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think it's over 150,000 times a year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: These small contracts are used.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I was surprised when I heard that number, but again, these are under 25,000.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's not as much control, and so this is something we're looking at.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: I used to run a not-for-profit, so I completely understand that.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: The city's capital process continues to face delays in moving projects from funding to actual construction.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: From the comptroller's perspective, what reforms are most urgently needed to improve capital project delivery and ensure neighborhoods with longstanding infrastructure needs are not continually left behind?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It is an absolute scandal that it costs $5 million on average to build a parks bathroom, that it costs $80 to $100 million to install elevators and ramps in a subway station.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is limiting our city's ability to thrive and to build for the future.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And there are generations of policies which have accumulated to make this happen.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It is all of them well-intentioned.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It is concerns about environmental protection.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: about community input, about safety, about design criteria, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The accumulation is costs that are just out of control.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we've got to rein it in.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We made a big step forward, thanks to the governor and the state legislature, on reining in SECRA.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In the budget that was just passed.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's going to help expedite review.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But there's just a myriad other obstacles that have to be cleared away that I would love to partner with you on.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We could do an entire hearing on this topic, but it's an urgent priority.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Narcisse, followed by Wong.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks whether the city is getting value from shelter providers, what the H&H pension clawback means for an already-cut system, and whether $100 million General Reserve is adequate. Levine says hotels drive high shelter costs, the H&H pension savings pass back to the city, and $100 million is an unprecedented low that is not adequate.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse questions Comptroller on homeless spending, H&H pension clawback, and reserves

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, Controller, and your team for being here to answer our question.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: On the formal rules for our rainy days, fine.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I will join you definitely.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And don't forget to add epidemic because with crack era and all the things that's hit us in New York City, sometimes we have to take money out, especially the black and brown communities been suffering.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And when we need to address that, I will say we can add that because epidemic is a big deal too.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The homeless service budget is now higher than at the peak of the migrant crisis.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: As the office that audits city contracts, are we getting vouchers?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: for what we pay when we pay the shelter providers?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: That's one.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You noted the budget clause back health hospitals, right?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Share of the pension savings.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I have to ask, what does that do to a system already facing hundreds of million in federal cuts?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The General Reserve next year sit at $100 million.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is that adequate for a big city like New York City?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: All really good questions, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the homeless services budget, it's gone up a lot for...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: First, because we have more people in the shelter system than ever.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're at over 90,000, I believe, at this point, or close to it, maybe high 80s a night.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's helped increase the budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But also we're spending more per person.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Now, that is in part because we've been relying on hotels as kind of an emergency stopgap because we don't have enough purpose-built shelters.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And hotels are no one's preferred solution.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're also very expensive.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is part of the cost.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But maybe implied in your question is I do think we need to scrutinize the contracts with providers and make sure that we have performance metrics.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that we're getting good value for our money.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is something that I would like us to focus on and would love for you to work on as well.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You asked a question about

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: H&H.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So there are some savings in here related to the fact that the pension re-amortization also applies to H&H.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's to use a technical term, they're an obligor.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They also pay into the pension system because they have workers there who are part of it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so essentially the idea is that, I believe, the money that they would be saving from the re-amortization would be passed back to the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Is that an accurate statement?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The amount that the city is assuming in the plan is...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For the first two years, 26 and 27, and it equals health and hospitals pension savings over the course of the life of the plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And just very quick on your question about $100 million, this is the in-year reserve.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, this is not the long-term.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Rainy Day Fund, we hope we never used it, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I hope the Rainy Day Fund sits there for a long time and we have no more pandemics.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But the in-year reserve could be used any day and goes up and down a lot.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we're going to start the year with only $100 million.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That is an unprecedented low number.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so the answer to your question is no, I do not think that is adequate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Wong asks the Comptroller to audit water rental payments diverted to the general fund, which Levine agrees to work on. Wong also asks what single budget assumption worries Levine most; Levine identifies CityFEPs' exponential growth without a budget limit as unsustainable, recommending it be incorporated with a fixed price tag.
chapters.title: CM Wong questions Comptroller on water rental payments and most worrying budget assumption

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Comptroller Levine.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Good to see you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My question goes back to the water rental payments.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The city continues to pull hundreds of millions of dollars out of the water system for the general fund.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: They use it even though the original debt rationale ended years ago.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Will you audit the water rental payment and publicly show how those funds have been used?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think it's a very important topic and I would love to work with you on it.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: How about we meet on this and figure out a good plan together?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have time for another question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You praise parts of the executive budget, but also warn it still depends on one-shots and does not solve the structural gap.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What single assumption in this budget worries you the most?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Child care?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: City Phelps, peer-to-peer revenue, or something else?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Look, you had a robust conversation on CityFEP.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
