utterances.text: And I want to say this is an incredibly important program.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm very proud that New York City has the only essentially municipal Section 8 in the country.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's something like 70,000 families being supported on it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I do not want to cut that program.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But it's growing at a very, very fast pace, Councilmember, and it's not currently accounted for in the budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's not sustainable.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so I think by simply bending the curve on the growth, we can both protect families who need it, but also get a handle on the budget impact.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I'm going to identify that as a fast-growing budget area that we need to get a fix on this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It needs to be properly budgeted.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And there's probably going to have to be some restraints on the pace of growth.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because right now, like...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is growing exponentially.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, it is.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: There's no end in sight in that, right?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Morano.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about retiree healthcare status, the commercial rent tax, and credit ratings. Levine says Advantage is gone, retiree healthcare is not in jeopardy, he'd support lifting the CRT trigger point further, and three rating agencies lowered the outlook to negative though recent good news may help avoid a downgrade.
chapters.title: CM Brewer questions Comptroller on retiree healthcare, commercial rent tax, and credit ratings

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I just was wondering where you think we are with the retirees'health care, a very complicated topic.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And secondly, I know when you were in the council, we all fight to get rid of the CRT, the commercial rent tax in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I didn't know if you thought, given the good news about the revenue, that might be something to consider for those small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then also, I just wanted to get updated where we are with the credit ratings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: One thing about the schools I just want to say, knowing the schools well as you do, students need more help than they have in the past.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So even though the number has gone down, each student has a whole myriad of challenges.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I just, I don't think it's apples to apples.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You got X.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: X number and you only need X dollars, I think you need more dollars in a sense because every child needs more support.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I just want to throw that out.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So look, I'm going to answer the retiree health question from the perspective of the retirees themselves, which is I know what you care about, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And first of all, advantage is gone and not coming back.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I get asked about it constantly still, so I want to reassure people there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We still have some work to do to achieve some of the savings that we have hoped for in previous agreements, but I want to emphasize that that does not

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: put the health care of individual retirees in jeopardy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is a problem for us to work out, to make sure our budget squares, but that is not a problem for retirees.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the commercial rent tax,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's true, I have felt it's unfair that it's only one part of the city, Manhattan South to 96th Street, where this is applied, and it's hard enough to run a small business right now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I have generally focused on kind of lifting the trigger point so that...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: 500 now, I think.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would probably be in favor of lifting it further.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm a little more cautious on our broader fiscal situation, Council Member, so I'd like a chance to think about the question of completely abolishing it, but I would certainly support finding ways to exempt more small business.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: businesses.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then credit rating?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Sorry, credit rating, yes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Look, we got a warning shot from three of the four, from Moody's, from Fitch, and from Kroll.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And they told us by lowering their outlook that there's a chance they would give us a downgrade.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's not a foregone conclusion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We could still avoid it, but I think that that is still an ongoing threat that in the months ahead and through the fall, particularly as we close out the annual budget in October, we need to be very wary of.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think that the goodness we've had in Wall Street earnings.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Helps our standing with the credit agencies.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Some of the additional money that we got from the state will help our standing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The fact that the current budget plan does not draw down the rainy day fund, I think also will improve our standing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think we're in a better place for those reasons.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we should still be cautious.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I think this is still hanging over us.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we should still do everything we can in the months ahead to avoid the risk of a downgrade.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about the status of using pension funds for housing. Levine describes a $4 billion plan to invest city pension funds in deeply affordable housing, workforce housing, preservation, and office-to-residential conversion, emphasizing the pension fund will maintain investment criteria while seeking win-win outcomes in NYC.
chapters.title: CM Brewer questions Comptroller on pension-funded housing investments

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, and just finally on housing, much discussion.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You have said that you're going to use some of the pension money to assist in the effort.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I didn't know where you are with that and just generally allocation for housing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How are you feeling that we're going in the right direction or not?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Look, we've done so much good work on rezoning, City of Yes, and the charter amendments.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's still work to do.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I know the council's all over that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But to me, the big fight right now is the financing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have vacant properties all over the city where the land's ready to go, the zoning's set, the developer's set, it's all good, they're waiting for financing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You see it probably in your district, lots that are waiting one year, two years, three years, four years, and it's often financing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's a long wait for low-income housing tax credit, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we're trying to solve that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've announced a plan to invest $4 billion of city pension funds in financing housing in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Deeply affordable housing, workforce housing, preservation, conversion of offices to residential.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is going to impact tens of thousands of units, and this is going to be good for the pension fund.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're not going to lower our investment criteria.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We never do.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We turn down deals all the time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're going to find that win-win where we get good returns, but provide financing that would have been missing in these deals.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm very, very excited about this plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Murano, followed by Maloney.

chapters.summary: Morano asks what OMB is getting wrong on pension re-amortization and what Levine would have done instead. Levine acknowledges the accounting justification for smoothing but would have preferred using the windfall to build reserves rather than plug the gap, noting the decision rests with pension trustees where labor unions have dominant votes.
chapters.title: CM Morano questions Comptroller on pension re-amortization vs reserve building

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Sir, for the record, comptroller or controller?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Mark is just fine, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: But in general, if you're setting the tone for the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't want to be put on the record on this because I don't want to upset one of the constituencies.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Fair enough.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Ask me when I'm not under oath.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: OMB testified.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: morning that the pension re-amortization is a responsible way to smooth payments over time and improve predictability.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Your testimony characterizes that same action as one of the major reasons where, as you said, kicking a very big can into next year.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What specifically do you believe OMB is getting wrong?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If this pension restructuring is such a concern, what would you have done instead?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I really appreciate the question.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And this is a once in a lifetime opportunity here.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think there is an argument to smooth out the payment schedule from an accounting perspective.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would prefer that given this is a once in a lifetime windfall that we use it to build up reserves.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Not to simply plug this year's gap.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's an opinion I've expressed repeatedly.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is ultimately a decision of the trustees, where the labor unions themselves have the dominant vote.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And those votes are now rolling out.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The first very small pension fund, Board of Education and Retirement Services, did vote to approve last week, but this is probably only about

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 3% of our total assets.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The big funds are still scheduled for the weeks ahead.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So stay tuned for that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But again, my feeling is there is a justification for smoothing this from an accounting perspective.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I just want to make sure that we are also using this very unique opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: to build our resilience for the long term.

chapters.summary: Morano asks where specifically the Comptroller would find $854 million for a rainy day deposit. Levine points to combining small schools, phasing out hold harmless ($250 million), and bending the CityFEPs curve, emphasizing the purpose of reserves is to avoid cutting services when tough times hit.
chapters.title: CM Morano questions Comptroller on finding $854 million for rainy day deposit

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Your testimony says the city would be adding approximately $854 million to the rainy day fund if your proposed reserve policy were already in place.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Given the competing demands for public safety, sanitation, education, housing, everything we've all talked to you about already,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: and other essential services, where specifically would you find that $854 million?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What spending would you reduce, defer, or eliminate in order to make that deposit?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, first of all, the point of having a rainy day fund is so that you do not have to cut services when the tough times hit.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The impact of not having prepared is that every kind of service that you and your constituents need could be cut.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I've already spoken about a few ways I think we could save money in this year's budget by...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Combining small schools so you avoid having to have duplicate two principals and two assistant principals and two school secretaries, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think that benefits kids ultimately so that they have critical mass to field sports teams.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think we are going to have to look at the whole harmless structure.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That alone I put at a quarter billion dollars this year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I say with great sensitivity, I do think we can bend the curb on city FEPS and save money there as well.

chapters.summary: Morano asks whether Levine agrees with OMB's characterization that the $12 billion gap was closed without raising taxes, drawing reserves, or cutting services. Levine says the mayor presented a balanced budget but it relied on Wall Street strength, state aid, savings, and one-shot measures that won't be available next year, with an $8.8 billion FY28 gap ahead.
chapters.title: CM Morano questions Comptroller on whether budget is truly balanced without cuts

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And finally, if I may, Chair, OMBT.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Testified earlier that the administration closed what it described as a $12 billion budget challenge without raising property taxes, drawing down reserves, or cutting services.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Do you agree with that characterization, or do you believe taxpayers should understand this budget differently?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, the mayor presented a balanced budget, as the charter requires.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think it's fair to ask what's happened in the last five months to make that possible.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the most important contribution to our progress has been Wall Street has had a very, very strong run.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so we have all revised our tax estimates up.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have, the mayor has, we had a very good bonus season on Wall Street.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Secondly, we got about $4 billion in additional support from Albany, credit to the governor and the state legislature on that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Then there were some savings and efficiencies that the mayor identified, credit to him on that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But as I mentioned, that was not enough.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that still left us with the need to use some one-shot measures that are going to get us through another 12 months, but they're not going to be there for the year after.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the proof of this dynamic is that the out-year gap, the FY28 gap, which we're going to start talking about in like six months, is $8.8 billion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's a big number.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's a very big number.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're not used to seeing a number that big.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And to confront that where re-amortization is off.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: the table.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't think we're going to delay class size again.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's other things that I don't think we'll count on for yet another year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think that should give us a little bit of concern, and it's why I think we should do everything we can in this budget to solve some of these structural problems.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And next we have Councilmember Maloney, followed by Councilmember Aviles.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about NYC's role as an applied AI leader and ensuring AI companies grow here. Levine notes AI firms are already driving commercial real estate growth and the city should be the capital of applied AI. Maloney also raises tourism underfunding; Levine agrees NYC is way underfunding tourism relative to peer cities like Las Vegas.
chapters.title: CM Maloney questions Comptroller on AI economy leadership and tourism funding

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And thank you to the comptroller, that's my guess, for testifying.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Completely agree with you on the risks and opportunities that are presented with the shift to AI and the need for workforce development to start early in all sectors.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And we all need to be getting more comfortable with AI tools, including in nonprofit and government.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So we have some work to do there.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And I also want to mention the other side of this coin, which is the...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Role that New York City plays in the AI economy as an applied AI leader and what we can be doing to ensure that AI companies are founded in New York, grow in New York, and that we reap from those economic benefits, if you could speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, first of all, I'm very grateful for your leadership for economic development.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's extremely helpful to have someone who knows technology in that role, and you're a very good partner for me on that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And amidst all the potential disruption on jobs that we fear might be coming from AI, there is one way that we could get

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: a little more of the upside, which is to have some of the jobs building these tools located right here in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we've had that benefit so far.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've actually, we have gotten a fair amount of the upside so far.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Also because we're financing this boom.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the firms that are financing the data center build out, et cetera, acknowledging all the complications with that infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But that is one reason why our budget is in a good position today.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: One reason why we have closed the $12 billion gap that we were worried about in January is because

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The financing of the AI boom has run through New York City.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I know you're well aware that we've had a very good run on renting of office space.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Commercial real estate is on a tear right now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Who saw that coming six years ago in the pandemic?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And one of the major drivers of that is AI firms.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Which are renting space at a very rapid clip.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Firms we know and startup firms, very large ecosystem.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So what can we do to support that?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, this should be the capital of applied AI.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: AI for healthcare, AI for research, for biotech, AI for financial services, for law,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We should be the capital of that industry, and we should do everything we can to grow that ecosystem.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We want those jobs here.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would certainly rather the people building those tools be New Yorkers who live in a diverse city and

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: ride the subway every morning than someone in Silicon Valley who's living in a bubble.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's my belief.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'd rather that New York be the place where these new tools are built.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I agree with you on that and having New Yorkers take part of the upside.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And I wanted to ask one quick question on the revenue side with tourism.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I asked this to the OMB director prior, but I'm concerned about the lower funding for NYC tourism in fiscal year 2027 when we are seeing that projections are 10 million visitors shorter

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: than what we, or reality is 10 million shorter than the projections.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So do you agree with the lower funding and what assessment have you made at the Comptroller's Office about what the marginal impact of adding funding to be comparable to other cities in our country

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: on increased tax revenue from tourists coming and using our businesses and our services?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you for fighting on this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We are way underfunding.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: NYC tourism.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The amount we're spending is a pittance relative to peer cities.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think Las Vegas is 10x more in what the local government gives for tourism promotion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we're behind our goals.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is a huge economic engine for New York City.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We need to be out there telling the story.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we're not able to do it adequately.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I absolutely support your fight to get more funding for tourism promotion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think you've asked for an analysis of the impact of every additional dollar.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I don't know if that's been done, but it's something I would love to work with you on.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And the last thing I'd add to that is an assessment of how all boroughs benefit from that increased...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Look, it is unequal right now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And, I mean, every tourist wants to go to Times Square, which, you know, if you're watching from home, it's so crowded, you'd probably be better off going to, like, Prospect Park or somewhere a little more open.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We do want to see tourism shared more equally also in uptown Manhattan and in communities of color more broadly.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And our tourism promotion can help that by uplifting the stories of the diverse communities of the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: One more reason we need more funding for NYC tourism.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

chapters.summary: Avilés raises concerns about unregulated AI in public schools and digital inequities, asks for recommendations on bending the CityFEPs curve while maintaining housing access, and questions guardrails on pension-funded housing to ensure benefits for the lowest-income New Yorkers. Levine supports a fixed CityFEPs budget with trade-offs and emphasizes mixed-income housing across all income levels.
chapters.title: CM Avilés questions Comptroller on AI in schools, CityFEPs curve-bending, and pension housing guardrails

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Revelez.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Hi, Mark.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Hi, Alexa.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I'm not going to engage in the other conversation, but thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I want to jump around on a couple of things.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Interestingly enough, I was sitting next to a hedge fund individual who was really expounding the excitement around AI, and I said, so do you think kindergartners should be using AI?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And he said, absolutely not.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: It does beg a question here that I think our cities should certainly lean into those, but there is a growing movement of profound concern with how AI is

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: unregulated in our public school systems that doesn't acknowledge the bias of AI,

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: digital inequities across the city, and how some students have the tools and others do not, and how that will exacerbate inequity in our city.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So I guess just as a general matter, I think we really need to look very, very seriously at the use of AI in our public schools and develop some real guardrails

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: because ed tech companies are staying with our dollars.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And I'm not sure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: they're harming our children more than they're supporting them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that is not my line of questioning, but I couldn't help but to say that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In terms of city faps, you mentioned a couple of times bending the curb.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we know that the growth has been exponential.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The crisis has been exponential.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And this has much been your life work as any others, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Affordable housing, truly affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But so has the growth of shelter costs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The council has been trying to find a, meet us in the middle.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you have a recommendation of how we can bend the curve but still continue to provide

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the much needed housing to this community of folks that continue to grow every year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I agree this is an essential program and it's definitely kept families out of homelessness and I don't want any family to lose their voucher.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I really don't.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't think we need to even consider that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But Section 8

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's an annual budget for that, the federal government has, and you can't spend beyond that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Now, unfortunately, that can mean there's a waiting list.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But the federal government can print money, literally.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The federal government can run a deficit.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think it's very hard for the city to have a multibillion-dollar program.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That has no budget limit.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I would like to see this

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Incorporated into the budget with a fixed price tag.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's what I mean by putting in some guardrails on the growth.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's going to entail some trade-offs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I get that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But to me, that would be the way to make this sustainable for the long term.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think we want a number that we can work with.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's not the big number we'd love, but I think we need to get to a point where there's actual negotiation around a number.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In terms of the pension funds that you noted in...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To finance projects.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sounds eerily familiar to public bank, which we love.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I was curious if that commitment includes a commitment to very low income

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: housing, or is it just like use our financing money to do a lot of market rate and a little

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: actual affordable housing?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Like what kind of guardrails are you going to be using for those pension financing funds?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Look, we absolutely need housing for people at the lowest rung of the economic ladder, including people who are at risk of homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we do have some good programs like that in the city, like ELA, which should get more funding so we can build more units.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But also, we have an affordability crisis at pretty much every rung.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: A bus driver in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Is having a very hard time finding housing, a social worker, a construction worker,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: a nurse even, are finding it very difficult to secure housing they can afford, and we're losing those families as well.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think we view the best housing model as one that has options for people at every rung of the ladder, potentially in a single development even, where you have mixed-income housing, which has all the benefits of families from different backgrounds being together.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And look, I believe that we just need to expand the supply overall.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We probably have a $500 million, a 500,000 unit shortfall in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's a very big number.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're only building about 25,000 units a year, so it would take decades to get there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And to do that, we're also going to need to mobilize the private sector.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we just need more housing built of all kinds, and we want to help with that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But again, we are very, very focused on people who are at every level of the income ladder, including those who are earning minimum wage or in danger of homelessness.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'll just say to close, I think using our pension dollars, like JPMorgan Chase uses our pension dollars that don't benefit our most vulnerable community members, but kind of trickles the money at that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is interested in making the most money it can with our money.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I would be a little uncomfortable with using public dollars again to do private financing for private companies to max out their profit on our dollars in another way.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think there needs to be some real guardrails and make sure that, you know, Sarah and Ella.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You still take too long.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's not enough money there to develop those units.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And getting them 10% at a clip is not going to reach the need.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I would encourage guardrails on that use.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is a huge discussion which we can't do justice to here because we're over time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Look, my solemn obligation is to protect the pension fund and to make sure that the three quarters of a million New Yorkers who have worked for the city or are still working for the city have a safe retirement.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I wake up every morning thinking about how I can make sure I live up to that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That doesn't mean we can't also, while

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: maximizing risk-adjusted returns, do some good in the world.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we're looking for win-wins, especially win-wins in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But it's a narrow path where we can get good returns, we can have a good impact, and I'm confident that we're going to have some great announcements on housing projects in the five boroughs, which you're going to love,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: which are going to meet those two standards.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So stay tuned.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You got it.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee thanks Comptroller Levine for his testimony and answers, concludes this portion of the budget hearing, and announces the committee will move to the Department of Finance segment in the committee room.
chapters.title: Chair Lee concludes Comptroller testimony and transitions to DOF segment

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Comptroller.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I think that...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That actually concludes this portion of our budget hearing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry we started late, but thank you for staying a little longer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you to all the council members.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Very great conversation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I think we're going to move into DOF in the committee room.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we will be back.

summary: The NYC Council Finance Committee held its final FY27 Executive Budget hearing, hearing testimony from OMB Director Sharif Solomon and Comptroller Mark Levine on the $124.7 billion executive budget, revenue forecasts, savings plans, and out-year gaps.

Council members questioned OMB extensively on Fair Fares expansion, DCWP funding, DOE contracts, CityFEPs cost containment, NYCHA capital funding, pension re-amortization, property tax reform, hiring delays, and agency-specific priorities across parks, housing, public health, and safety.

Comptroller Levine warned of an $8.8 billion FY28 gap driven by one-shot measures, urged formal rainy day fund rules targeting 16% of tax revenue, and presented scenarios on AI's potential $9-14 billion revenue impact, while supporting pied-à-terre tax revenue and pension-funded housing investments.
title: Committee on Finance
